visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast

TECHNIQUE:
Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique. Oral contrast was administered.Coronal and
sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Total DLP (Body) = 928 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
Atelectasis seen in the lung bases.  Atherosclerotic
calcifications are noted in the coronary arteries.  There is no evidence of
pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. There
is a trace amount of perihepatic ascites. There is no evidence of focal
lesions.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary
dilatation.  The gallbladder is distended and contains cholelithiasis with
mild pericholecystic fluid.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
A large simple cyst in the interpolar region of the left kidney measuring 7.2
x 8.6 cm.  There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis. 
There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Mild sigmoid
diverticulosis is noted without evidence of acute diverticulitis.  Otherwise,
the colon and rectum are within normal limits.  The appendix is normal.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The uterus is resected.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES:
There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture. 
Degenerative changes are seen in the visualized spine.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.  There
are small bilateral fat containing inguinal hernias.

NOTIFICATION:
The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 8:59 pm, 1 minutes after
discussion with attending radiologist.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary colic without frank cholecystitis

The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits. The contour of the
liver is smooth. There is no focal liver mass.  The main portal vein is patent
with hepatopetal flow. There is no ascites.

3 mm

bowel gas.

  Left kidney: 10.8 cm

The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Residual/recurrent choledocholithiasis, biliary stricture, or incomplete biliary drainage post-ERCP with stent placement; ongoing cholangitis
why_trace.information_gap: Whether the biliary tree (common bile duct, intrahepatic ducts) has been adequately drained after ERCP and stent placement, and whether residual stones, stricture, or biliary obstruction persists to explain the ongoing elevated bilirubin (total 5.1, direct 4.2), transaminitis (ALT 240, AST 179), and persistent RUQ discomfort
why_trace.expected_finding: Residual choledocholithiasis, biliary duct dilation, stricture at the level of the CBD or hilum, or confirmation of adequate stent position and biliary decompression; possibly pericholecystic changes consistent with acute cholecystitis given the distended gallbladder with stones, sludge, and wall edema on ultrasound
grounding: History: 'underwent ERCP on ___ with ___ placement' — stent placed for cholangitis
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 5.1 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)' — persistent hyperbilirubinemia post-ERCP
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Direct: 4.2 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)' — predominantly conjugated/obstructive pattern
Labs: 'Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 240.0 IU/L' and 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 179.0 IU/L' — ongoing hepatocellular/cholestatic injury
Prior imaging: 'The gallbladder is distended, and contains stones and sludge with wall edema' — cholecystitis cannot be excluded
Prior imaging: 'There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation' and 'CHD: 3 mm' — ultrasound shows no obvious dilation but is limited
Prior imaging: 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas' — pancreas and distal CBD not assessable on US
Physical examination: 'mildly tender to palpation on RUQ, negative ___ sign, no rebound, no guarding' — persistent RUQ tenderness without peritoneal signs
History: 'blood cultures with GNRs' — prior bacteremia from biliary source

study_inadequacy.evidence: The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is a cross-sectional modality that will fully visualize the pancreas (overcoming the bowel-gas limitation of ultrasound) and the physician's reasoning explicitly acknowledges the ultrasound's limitation in failing to image the pancreas and distal CBD, making the order both capable of resolving the study inadequacy and intentionally directed at it.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient has persistent hyperbilirubinemia, transaminitis, RUQ tenderness, and a distended gallbladder with wall edema on ultrasound after ERCP with stent placement for cholangitis, and MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive modality to evaluate the biliary tree anatomy, stent position, residual stones, and ductal pathology that ultrasound cannot adequately characterize.

LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grayscale and color Doppler ultrasound examination of the abdomen

The liver echotexture is normal and the contour is smooth. There is no

There is no intrahepatic bile duct dilation. The CBD measures 6
mm, tapering smoothly as it nears the papilla.

seen due to overlying bowel gas.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis; also concern for malignant biliary obstruction given history of breast cancer and incidental adrenal nodule
why_trace.information_gap: Whether there is a common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) or other biliary obstruction causing the pancreatitis — the CT showed a prominent pancreatic duct and the ultrasound demonstrated gallstones including a large immobile 17 mm stone, but the CBD was only 6 mm and the distal CBD/ampullary region was not fully characterized; the etiology of the pancreatitis (biliary vs. other) remains unconfirmed
why_trace.expected_finding: A filling defect or stone within the common bile duct or at the ampulla of Vater, or alternatively a stricture/mass at the biliary-pancreatic junction that would explain the markedly elevated transaminases (AST 407, ALT 465), elevated alkaline phosphatase (467), elevated bilirubin (total 1.8, direct 0.9), and massively elevated lipase (17740) in the setting of known gallstones
grounding: Lipase: 17740.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0) — massively elevated, confirming acute pancreatitis
AST: 407.0 IU/L, ALT: 465.0 IU/L — markedly elevated transaminases suggesting biliary/hepatic involvement
Alkaline Phosphatase: 467.0 IU/L (ref 35.0-105.0) — markedly elevated, consistent with biliary obstruction
Bilirubin, Total: 1.8 mg/dL, Direct: 0.9 mg/dL — elevated direct bilirubin suggesting biliary pathology
CT ABD: 'stranding and fluid seen surrounding the head, body, and tail of the pancreas... The pancreatic duct is prominent, although not enlarged'
US: 'There are multiple mobile gallstones with a larger, 17 mm stone which is immobile and located within the phrygian cap'
US: 'The CBD measures 6 mm, tapering smoothly as it nears the papilla' — CBD not dilated but distal segment not fully characterized
CT ABD: 'incompletely characterized 1 cm nodule of the left adrenal gland. The right adrenal gland is somewhat thickened' — raises concern for metastatic disease given breast cancer history

study_inadequacy.evidence: PANCREAS: The head and body of the pancreas are normal. The tail was not well seen due to overlying bowel gas.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP provides excellent soft-tissue visualization of the entire pancreas including the tail, directly resolving the ultrasound's technical inadequacy of non-visualization of the pancreatic tail due to bowel gas, and the physician's reasoning explicitly includes evaluation of the pancreatic duct and ductal pathology as part of its stated aims.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive next step to evaluate the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology given confirmed gallstones on ultrasound, a prominent pancreatic duct on CT, markedly elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin suggesting biliary involvement, and the need to determine whether ERCP is indicated — all without additional radiation or contrast.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
CT SCAN OF THE ABDOMEN AND PELVIS PERFORMED ON ___:


TECHNIQUE:
MDCT of the abdomen was performed followed by venous phase imaging
of the abdomen and pelvis.  Multiplanar reformations were provided.  Oral
contrast was administered.

FINDINGS:
The imaged lung bases are clear.

ABDOMEN:
There is interval increase in gallbladder distention with
development of small pericholecystic fluid.  In this patient with known
gallstones, early ____ is a concern and correlation with
ultrasound is advised.  A tiny hypodensity within the liver dome is unchanged.
There is mild intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation which is new from prior
exam.  The spleen is normal.  Adrenal glands and kidneys are stable with
symmetric enhancement and prompt excretion of contrast.  A stable hypodensity
within the right renal upper pole is likely a simple cyst and measures 16 x 22
mm.

In this patient with known necrotizing pancreatitis and indwelling drainage
(cystogastrostomy tubes) are again seen with unchanged position.  The overall
extent of the peripancreatic fluid collections and extensive soft tissue
thickening along the root of mesentery and adjacent to the pancreatic head is
stable to mildly increased.  There is loss of normal fat planes at the
confluence of the portal vein with the SMV and splenic vein with worsening
attenuation of the SMV at this level and concern for impending thrombosis.

PELVIS:
There is no dilated small bowel or findings to raise concern for
bowel obstruction.  The colon contains a moderate fecal load.  Urinary bladder
is distended, appearing normal.  Trace free fluid in the deep pelvis is noted.

BONES:
Unremarkable.

why_trace.expected_finding: Sonographic signs of acute cholecystitis (gallbladder wall thickening ≥4 mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign, gallstones with shadowing) and/or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Complicated urinary tract infection/urosepsis (given cloudy urine, >50 WBC/hpf, positive nitrite, many bacteria, RBC 6-10/hpf); cholangitis; pyelonephritis
why_trace.information_gap: The bedside ultrasound suggested a thickened gallbladder but was not a formal study; the physician needs a definitive characterization of the gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, stones, common bile duct dilation, and sonographic Murphy's sign to confirm or grade acute cholecystitis as the driver of sepsis — especially since the urinalysis simultaneously raises urosepsis as a competing source.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening (>3-4 mm), pericholecystic fluid, cholelithiasis, and/or a positive sonographic Murphy's sign consistent with acute cholecystitis; alternatively, common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis/cholangitis; or a normal gallbladder that would redirect attention to the urinary source.
grounding: History: 'Noted to have thickened gallbladder on bedside U/S'
History: 'patient is febrile to 102.6, has an elevated WBC, has a palpable gallbladder, and appears to be suffering from frank sepsis'
VS: 'febrile to 102.6, tachycardic to 114'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 16.3 K/uL (ref 4.0-11.0)'
Labs: 'Neutrophils: 85.0 % (ref 50.0-70.0)'
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 126.0 IU/L (ref 35.0-105.0)' — mildly elevated, raising biliary obstruction concern
Urinalysis: 'WBC: >50 #/hpf', 'Nitrite: POS', 'Bacteria: MANY', 'Urine Appearance: Cloudy' — competing urinary source of sepsis
Physical exam: 'soft, tender to palpation, mildly distended, palpable gallbladder'

discordance.evidence: Urinalysis: WBC >50 #/hpf, Nitrite: POS, Bacteria: MANY, RBC 6-10 #/hpf, Urine Appearance: Cloudy — in a patient with 'frank sepsis,' febrile to 102.6, tachycardic to 114, and altered mental status, this markedly abnormal urinalysis raises a competing source of sepsis (urinary tract / urosepsis) that the leading diagnosis of acute cholecystitis cannot explain and that could substantially change management.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered gallbladder ultrasound can only resolve the biliary side of the discordance — confirming or excluding cholecystitis — but cannot adjudicate the urinary source of sepsis, and while the reasoning explicitly acknowledges the competing urosepsis signal and frames the test as a means to confirm or redirect toward the urinary source, the order itself does not include any imaging or workup directed at the urinary tract, leaving the urosepsis hypothesis unresolved.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The history documents a thickened gallbladder on bedside ultrasound with fever, leukocytosis, tachycardia, and a palpable gallbladder, making a formal dedicated RUQ/liver-gallbladder ultrasound the appropriate next step to confirm acute cholecystitis and assess severity before any intervention decision.

Patient lives in ___, but has been in ___ recently  seeking second opinions on his condition. In ___ in ___  ___, he had abdominal pain and a CT which showed terminal  ileitis. visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Supine and upright views of the abdomen.

FINDINGS:
There is no pneumatosis or pneumoperitoneum. The bowel gas pattern is
nonspecific but nonobstructive with moderate fecal loading in the ascending,
transverse, sigmoid colon and rectum. There are no abnormal soft tissue
calcifications. The lung bases are clear. The visualized osseous structures
are unremarkable.

EXAMINATION:
MR ___:

TECHNIQUE:
T1 and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen and pelvis
were acquired within a 1.5 T magnet, including 3D dynamic sequences performed
prior to, during, and following the administration of 0.1 mmol/kg of Gadavist
intravenous contrast (9 cc). Oral contrast consisted of 900 mL of VoLumen. 1.0
mg of Glucagon was administered IM to reduce bowel peristalsis.

FINDINGS:


MR ENTEROGRAPHY:
The MRI appearance of the small bowel is within normal limits. Specifically,
normal distensibility is seen throughout the small bowel loops without
evidence of stricture. No segmental wall thickening, edema or hyperemia.  No
mucosal thickening seen. There is no mesenteric hyperemia or fibrofatty
proliferation.

The study is not intended to evaluate the colon given lack of bowel prep.
There is a relatively large volume of fecal material throughout their the
right hemicolon. No evidence of colitis is seen. The appendix is well
visualized, normal in appearance (14:62).

MRI OF THE ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT IV CONTRAST:
There is a abnormal appearance of the gallbladder. There is cholelithiasis
with small stones dependently. The gallbladder is distended with a
circumferentially thickened and edematous wall. The diameter of the wall
measures up to 1 cm. There is initial mucosal enhancement, somewhat
discontinuous in appearance at times (1302:41) without dramatic
hyperenhancement. On the more delayed sequences, there is progressive
heterogeneous enhancement of the wall, particularly at the level of the fundus
(16:107). There is a small amount of free fluid surrounding the gallbladder
and extending towards the inferior tip of the liver (10:25), without fluid
seen elsewhere within the abdominal cavity. There is also a small amount of
hyperemia of the adjacent liver parenchyma (1300 01:29).

The cystic duct is unable to be visualized throughout its coarse but does not
appear dilated. There is prominence of the cystic artery.

The remainder of the liver is normal in appearance with normal parenchymal
signal and enhancement. The spleen with accessory splenic tissue, pancreas,
adrenal glands and kidneys are unremarkable.

MRI OF THE PELVIS WITH AND WITHOUT IV CONTRAST:
The urinary bladder, visualized portion of the prostate and seminal vesicles
are unremarkable. There is no free pelvic fluid or pelvic lymphadenopathy.
Osseous structures are unremarkable.

NOTIFICATION:
Findings were discussed via phone by Dr. ___ with Dr
___ at 2pm on ___.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction; Crohn's-related terminal ileitis flare
why_trace.information_gap: The MRE has already identified an abnormal gallbladder with cholelithiasis, wall thickening up to 1 cm, pericholecystic free fluid, and adjacent hepatic hyperemia — but MRE is not the standard modality for gallbladder evaluation and cannot reliably assess for sonographic Murphy's sign, gallbladder wall vascularity on Doppler, or common bile duct dilation; a dedicated right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is needed to confirm or characterize acute cholecystitis and rule out biliary ductal obstruction.
why_trace.expected_finding: Sonographic Murphy's sign, gallbladder wall thickening >4 mm with pericholecystic fluid, and/or impacted cystic duct stone confirming acute cholecystitis; alternatively, a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis.
grounding: Prior imaging 2 (MRE): 'There is cholelithiasis with small stones dependently. The gallbladder is distended with a circumferentially thickened and edematous wall. The diameter of the wall measures up to 1 cm.'
Prior imaging 2 (MRE): 'There is a small amount of free fluid surrounding the gallbladder and extending towards the inferior tip of the liver (10:25)'
Prior imaging 2 (MRE): 'There is also a small amount of hyperemia of the adjacent liver parenchyma'
Prior imaging 2 (MRE): 'The cystic duct is unable to be visualized throughout its coarse but does not appear dilated.'
History: 'severe abdominal pain ___ after eating, prompting him to come to ED'
Labs: 'WBC 11.5' (mild leukocytosis with neutrophilia 82.6%)
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.4 mg/dL' and 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 102.0 IU/L' (LFTs unremarkable, but ductal obstruction not excluded)

study_inadequacy.evidence: The study is not intended to evaluate the colon given lack of bowel prep. There is a relatively large volume of fecal material throughout the right hemicolon.

discordance.evidence: There is a abnormal appearance of the gallbladder. There is cholelithiasis with small stones dependently. The gallbladder is distended with a circumferentially thickened and edematous wall. The diameter of the wall measures up to 1 cm... There is a small amount of free fluid surrounding the gallbladder... There is also a small amount of hyperemia of the adjacent liver parenchyma.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered RUQ ultrasound directly and appropriately addresses the discordant acute cholecystitis finding with well-matched reasoning, but the study_inadequacy flag — the non-diagnostic colon/ileocecal region due to lack of bowel prep — is entirely unaddressed by this order.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The MRE explicitly identified cholelithiasis, a distended gallbladder with circumferential wall thickening up to 1 cm, pericholecystic free fluid, and adjacent hepatic hyperemia — classic imaging features of acute cholecystitis — making a dedicated gallbladder ultrasound the appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis, assess ductal anatomy, and guide surgical or interventional management.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: gynecologic pathology (ovarian torsion, tubo-ovarian abscess, ovarian cyst), bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, or diabetic ketoacidosis-related ileus
why_trace.information_gap: The anatomic source of the right lower quadrant tenderness cannot be determined by history, exam, or labs alone — particularly in an obese patient where physical exam sensitivity is reduced and the differential includes both surgical (appendicitis, ovarian pathology) and non-surgical causes
why_trace.expected_finding: Periappendiceal fat stranding or an enlarged non-compressible appendix consistent with appendicitis, or alternatively an adnexal mass/free fluid suggesting gynecologic pathology, or colonic wall thickening suggesting diverticulitis

Physical examination: 'obese' — limits exam sensitivity
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 13.0 K/uL (ref 4.0-11.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Neutrophils: 81.4 % (ref 50.0-70.0)'
History: 'four days abdominal pain... became more intense... accompanied by nausea'
History: 'She denies any previous history of this type of pain'
Laboratory tests: 'Lipase: 23.0 IU/L' — pancreatitis effectively excluded
Laboratory tests: 'Glucose: 406.0 mg/dL' — uncontrolled diabetes, patient is female with obesity raising gynecologic differential

discordance.evidence: Glucose: 406.0 mg/dL (ref 70.0-100.0); Sodium: 131.0 mEq/L (ref 133.0-145.0); Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL (ref 0.4-1.1); Ketone: 10.0 mg/dL; Glucose (urine): 1000.0 mg/dL; Hyaline Casts: 5.0 #/lpf (ref 0.0-0.0) — in the setting of a soft, non-peritoneal abdomen with normal lactate and benign vitals

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is mechanically capable of evaluating surgical causes of abdominal pain, but the physician's reasoning focuses entirely on anatomic/surgical localization and does not acknowledge or plan to address the flagged metabolic discordance — markedly elevated glucose with ketonuria suggesting DKA/HHS as the primary driver — leaving that critical situation unhandled.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Four days of worsening abdominal pain with RLQ tenderness, leukocytosis (WBC 13.0), neutrophilia (81.4%), and an obese habitus that limits exam reliability make CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast the appropriate next step to identify or exclude a surgical source.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Axial helical MDCT images were obtained from the lung bases to the
pubic symphysis after administration of IV contrast.  Coronal and sagittal
reformations were generated.

DLP:
964.60 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:
The lungs demonstrate multiple round nodularities, including a 6-mm
nodule in the left lower lobe (2:4), a 3-mm nodule in the left lower lobe
(2:6), a 5-mm nodule in the right lower lobe (2:4) and a 5 mm nodule in the
right middle lobe (2:3).  Other smaller nodules are seen.  There is also mild
dependent atelectasis.  The visualized heart and pericardium are unremarkable.

CT ABDOMEN:
The liver enhances homogeneously, without focal lesions or
intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation.  The gallbladder is distended with
significant pericholecystic stranding. There is a 2.8 x 3.4 cm more hypodense
region in the neck subsequently evaluated by US and found to be a large
impacted gallstone. The remaining gallbladder is filled with hyperdense
material of an attenuation of approximately 50 Hounsfield units representing
tumefactive sludge. The gallbladder is impinging into the hilum and
compressing but not obstructing the portal vein, with a very attenuated
although patent right portal vein. There is no intrahepatic biliary duct
dilatation.  

The pancreas, spleen and adrenal glands are unremarkable.  The kidneys
demonstrate symmetric nephrograms and excretion of contrast. The small and
large bowels are within normal limits.  There is no evidence of diverticulitis
or appendicitis.  The intra-abdominal vasculature is unremarkable.  There is
no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There is no ascites or
abdominal free air.  No abdominal wall hernia is present.

PELVIC CT:
The urinary bladder is decompressed.  There is no pelvic wall or
inguinal lymphadenopathy.  No pelvic free fluid is observed.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
There are no lytic or blastic lesions concerning for
malignancy.  Degenerative changes about the thoracic spine are noted.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction; portal vein compression sequelae
why_trace.information_gap: The CT identified a distended gallbladder with pericholecystic stranding, a large impacted stone in the gallbladder neck, and tumefactive sludge, but could not fully characterize wall thickness, wall integrity, perfusion, or the presence of pericholecystic fluid/gas that would confirm acute cholecystitis versus simple cholelithiasis; additionally, the degree of portal vein compression and any biliary ductal involvement needs real-time sonographic assessment.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), sonographic Murphy's sign, pericholecystic fluid, or intraluminal gas confirming acute cholecystitis; alternatively, confirmation of the impacted neck stone and assessment of the common bile duct diameter to exclude choledocholithiasis/biliary obstruction.
grounding: Prior imaging 1: 'The gallbladder is distended with significant pericholecystic stranding.'

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT explicitly flagged a distended gallbladder with pericholecystic stranding and an impacted gallstone as the dominant finding, and ultrasound is the standard next-step modality to confirm acute cholecystitis, elicit a sonographic Murphy's sign, measure wall thickness, and evaluate the biliary tree — information the CT alone cannot reliably provide.

resolve.reason: CT abdomen with contrast is the appropriate modality to evaluate vascular patency at the SMA/celiac reconstruction site and assess for bowel ischemia, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets mesenteric ischemia as the most dangerous diagnosis to exclude in the context of the markedly disproportionate leukocytosis.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Given the markedly elevated WBC (31.3 K/uL), elevated transaminases (AST 106, ALT 86, ALP 159), epigastric pain radiating to the back, low-grade fever, and critically the known history of SMA/celiac occlusion with prior vascular re-implantation, CT abdomen with contrast is the appropriate next step to evaluate for mesenteric ischemia, hepatobiliary pathology, and other intra-abdominal sources that cannot be differentiated clinically.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Contrast-enhanced axial images of the abdomen and pelvis were
obtained with multiplanar reformatted images.

CT ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST:
Aside from hypoventilatory changes at the lung
bases, the lungs are clear without pericardial or pleural effusion.

There is marked distention of the gallbladder with pericholecystic fluid and
inflammatory fat stranding.  No radiodense gallstones are seen and no focal
fluid collections are observed. Hyperemia of the adjacent liver parenchyma is
noted. The liver, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable.
The kidneys enhance symmetrically and left hydronephrosis due to the known
ureteropelvic junction obstruction has improved compared ___.
Intra-abdominal loops of large and small bowel are of normal caliber. Patient
is status post right colonic resction and ileocolonic anastamosis. There is no
free intra- abdominal air. Atherosclerotic calcification of the abdominal
aorta is noted, though it remains of normal caliber.  Although this exam is
not tailored to evaluate arterial structures, unchanged occlusions of the
origin of the celiac trunk and superior mesenteric artery are reidentified,
with retrograde filling via a widely patent re- implanted inferior mesenteric
artery.

CT PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:
The rectum, sigmoid colon, uterus, adnexa, and
bladder are unremarkable.  No free fluid or pathologically enlarged pelvic
lymph nodes are seen.

Bone windows reveal no worrisome lytic or sclerotic lesions.

FINDINGS:
The cardiomediastinal silhouette is unchanged.  The pulmonary
vascularity appears normal.  The lungs remain clear without pleural effusion
or pneumothorax.  There is no evidence for pneumoperitoneum.  Dextroconvex
thoracic scoliosis is noted.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; acalculous cholecystitis with possible gangrenous progression
why_trace.information_gap: The CT shows marked gallbladder distension with pericholecystic fluid and inflammatory fat stranding but no radiodense stones — the physician needs real-time sonographic assessment of gallbladder wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign, and the biliary tree (CBD diameter, intrahepatic ductal dilation, presence of non-calcified/cholesterol stones invisible on CT) to confirm acute cholecystitis and evaluate for biliary obstruction driving the elevated transaminases and alkaline phosphatase.
why_trace.expected_finding: Thickened gallbladder wall (>3–4 mm), pericholecystic fluid, positive sonographic Murphy's sign, and possibly sludge or non-calcified stones; also assessment of common bile duct diameter to evaluate for choledocholithiasis given elevated ALT 86, AST 106, and ALP 159.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT has already identified the gallbladder as the primary pathologic focus, but ultrasound is the gold-standard modality for confirming acute cholecystitis (wall thickness, Murphy's sign, stones/sludge) and evaluating the biliary tree for obstruction, which CT with contrast is not optimally tailored to assess — directly addressing the gap left by the CT findings and the elevated liver enzymes.

why_trace.information_gap: The anatomic source and etiology of the acute abdomen cannot be determined from labs and exam alone — specifically, whether there is vascular compromise (mesenteric ischemia, infarction), a solid-organ event (hepatic/splenic hematoma or infarct), or a hollow-viscus process (cholecystitis, diverticulitis, obstruction)
why_trace.expected_finding: Evidence of mesenteric ischemia (bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis, portal venous gas, or lack of bowel wall enhancement), hepatic or splenic infarct/subcapsular hematoma given supratherapeutic INR, or alternatively cholecystitis/pericholecystic fluid or diverticulitis to explain the RUQ-predominant pain

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate modality to detect mesenteric ischemia and other vascular etiologies, and the physician's reasoning explicitly names mesenteric ischemia as the leading concern driving the order, directly addressing the flagged discordance between hemodynamic instability and benign exam.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With no prior imaging, an undifferentiated acute abdomen with hemodynamic instability, elevated lactate, marked leukocytosis, supratherapeutic INR, severe PVD, and abrupt-onset pain, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the single most efficient test to simultaneously survey all intra-abdominal structures and vascular perfusion.

resolve.reason: The ultrasound can evaluate biliary pathology but cannot resolve the core discordance flag—it cannot distinguish salivary, macroamylasemia, or bowel ischemia sources—and while the reasoning does acknowledge the amylase/lipase discordance, it dismisses it as 'biliary-origin hyperamylasemia' without a plan to investigate the alternative etiologies the flag specifically raises.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The combination of RUQ tenderness, positive Murphy's sign, post-prandial onset, prior similar episodes, mildly elevated ALT, leukocytosis with neutrophilia, and markedly elevated amylase (700 IU/L) in the setting of normal lipase strongly points to a biliary source — ultrasound is the first-line, radiation-free modality to evaluate the gallbladder and biliary tree.

resolve.reason: The ultrasound can assess biliary anatomy but cannot resolve the core discordance — massively elevated indirect bilirubin with paradoxically elevated haptoglobin, normal LDH, and absent urobilinogen — and the physician's reasoning acknowledges hemolysis as a possibility but does not directly address the contradictory hemolytic markers that make the diagnosis uncertain.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With T.bili 16.2 predominantly indirect, RUQ tenderness, jaundice, and no prior imaging at this institution, abdominal ultrasound is the appropriate first-line, non-invasive, radiation-free modality to evaluate the biliary tree, liver parenchyma, and gallbladder before committing to more invasive or advanced imaging.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits. The contour of the
liver is smooth. There is no focal liver mass. The main portal vein is patent
with hepatopetal flow. There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CHD measures 3 mm.

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is distended with multiple small shadowing
gallstones, gallbladder sludge, and a large, rounded, mildly hyperechoic
nonshadowing structure likely a sludge ball.  There is trace free fluid
adjacent to the gallbladder.  Sonographic ___ was negative.

PANCREAS:
The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

SPLEEN:
Patient is status post splenectomy.

KIDNEYS:
Limited views of the right kidney show no hydronephrosis.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

NOTIFICATION:
The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by ___
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 10:08 ___, 22 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis or biliary duct obstruction; hemolysis (though haptoglobin elevated argues against); hepatocellular disease; malignant biliary obstruction (given history of Hodgkin's lymphoma and radiation); primary sclerosing cholangitis or post-radiation biliary stricture
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound shows gallstones and sludge but no biliary ductal dilation and a normal CHD (3 mm), yet the patient has a markedly elevated total bilirubin of 16.2 with a dominant indirect component (15.3 indirect vs 0.9 direct); the physician needs to determine whether there is occult common bile duct pathology (stone, stricture, or mass) not visible on ultrasound, or whether the jaundice is truly pre-hepatic/hepatocellular in origin
why_trace.expected_finding: Either a common bile duct stone, biliary stricture, or periampullary/hilar mass causing obstruction not detected on ultrasound — or, alternatively, a normal biliary tree confirming that the jaundice is not obstructive in etiology
grounding: Bilirubin, Total: 16.2 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)
Bilirubin, Indirect: 15.3 mg/dL
Bilirubin, Direct: 0.9 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)
BILE DUCTS: There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CHD measures 3 mm.
GALLBLADDER: The gallbladder is distended with multiple small shadowing gallstones, gallbladder sludge, and a large, rounded, mildly hyperechoic nonshadowing structure likely a sludge ball.
History: distant history of hodgkins lymphoma s/p chemo/radiation
History: jaundice in the past with elevated T.bili in the setting of hospitalization with a negative liver workup. Per review of our records he has had a prominent indirect component in the past.
Alkaline Phosphatase: 168.0 IU/L (ref 40.0-130.0)
Haptoglobin: 227.0 mg/dL (ref 30.0-200.0)

study_inadequacy.evidence: portions of the pancreatic tail obscured by overlying bowel gas

discordance.evidence: Bilirubin, Indirect: 15.3 mg/dL ... Bilirubin, Total: 16.2 mg/dL ... Haptoglobin: 227.0 mg/dL (ref 30.0-200.0) ... Lactate Dehydrogenase (LD): 198.0 IU/L (ref 94.0-250.0) ... Urine Bilirubin: NEG ... Urobilinogen: NEG

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP directly resolves the study_inadequacy by providing complete pancreatic tail visualization unobstructed by bowel gas, and the reasoning explicitly targets the discordant bilirubin pattern by seeking to characterize the entire biliary tree, liver parenchyma, and pancreas — encompassing both flagged situations with appropriate intent.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary tree in detail when ultrasound is non-diagnostic but clinical jaundice is significant, especially given the history of prior radiation (risk of biliary stricture) and gallstones on ultrasound with a normal-caliber CBD that may still harbor a stone or stricture not visible sonographically.

baseline.patient_history: ___ is a ___ woman with who presented to the ___ with RUQ pain. She was transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities. visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits. The contour of the
liver is smooth. There is no focal liver mass. The main portal vein is patent
with hepatopetal flow. There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CHD measures 4 mm.

GALLBLADDER:
Cholelithiasis without definite gallbladder wall thickening. 
Gallbladder appears collapsed around multiple calculi.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying
bowel gas.

SPLEEN:
Normal echogenicity, measuring 11.7 cm.

KIDNEYS:
Limited views of the right kidney show no hydronephrosis.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone causing biliary obstruction
why_trace.information_gap: Whether a stone is lodged in the common bile duct (or cystic duct) causing biliary obstruction, given markedly elevated transaminases and bilirubin with a non-dilated CBD on ultrasound and a pancreas that was not adequately visualized
why_trace.expected_finding: A filling defect or stone within the common bile duct, cystic duct, or intrahepatic ducts; alternatively, subtle ductal dilation or periductal edema not detectable on ultrasound
grounding: Labs: ALT 528 IU/L (ref 0-40), AST 275 IU/L (ref 0-40), Alkaline Phosphatase 220 IU/L (ref 35-105), Bilirubin Total 2.3 mg/dL (ref 0-1.5), Bilirubin Direct 1.6 mg/dL (ref 0-0.3)
Ultrasound: 'Cholelithiasis without definite gallbladder wall thickening. Gallbladder appears collapsed around multiple calculi.'
Ultrasound: 'The CHD measures 4 mm. There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation.'
Ultrasound: 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas.'
History: 'transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities'
History: 'Gallstones - diagnosed ___ ago during ___ pregnancy'

study_inadequacy.evidence: The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is the ideal modality to image the pancreas and biliary ductal system without the bowel-gas limitation that rendered the ultrasound inadequate, and the physician's reasoning explicitly acknowledges the failed pancreatic visualization and the need to evaluate the distal CBD and surrounding structures.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The combination of RUQ pain, known cholelithiasis, markedly elevated ALT/AST/ALP/bilirubin, and an ultrasound that shows a non-dilated CHD (4 mm) but could not visualize the pancreas or distal CBD creates a classic indication for MRCP to detect choledocholithiasis before committing to ERCP.

why_trace.information_gap: No current institutional imaging is available — the only imaging referenced is from an outside hospital (OSH), and the treating physician needs direct visualization of the gallbladder, CBD, and the reported fundal mass to confirm stone burden, assess for acute cholecystitis, evaluate CBD dilation, and characterize the fundal lesion.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones with possible pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening consistent with acute cholecystitis, CBD dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis, and characterization of the fundal mass (polyp vs. neoplasm vs. artifact) reported on OSH ultrasound.

why_trace.expected_finding: MRCP is expected to demonstrate choledocholithiasis (stones within the dilated CBD), define the level and cause of biliary obstruction, and characterize the gallbladder fundal mass (polyp vs. adenocarcinoma vs. adenomyomatosis) that was flagged at the outside hospital.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT ABD AND PELVIS W/O CONTRAST:

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT images of the abdomen and pelvis were acquired
without intravenous contrast. Non-contrast scan has several limitations in
detecting vascular and parenchymal organ abnormalities, including tumor
detection.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 6.6 s, 51.8 cm; CTDIvol = 21.7 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,123.3 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,123 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
The heart is normal in size.  There is mild atherosclerotic
calcification involving the coronary arteries and thoracic aorta.  There is no
pericardial effusion or wall thickening.  Bilateral lung bases are clear. 
There is no focal consolidation.  There is no pleural effusion or
pneumothorax.

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver demonstrates homogeneous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions within the limitations of an unenhanced
scan.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary
dilatation.  There is fat stranding surrounding the gallbladder.  The
gallbladder is distended with no stones seen in the lumen, although ultrasound
is more sensitive.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions within the limitations of an unenhanced scan.  There is no
pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size.  There is no suspicious
renal lesions within the limitations of an unenhanced scan.  There is no
hydronephrosis.  There is no nephrolithiasis. There is no perinephric
abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
There is postsurgical change of Roux-en-Y.  Small bowel
loops demonstrate normal caliber and wall thickness throughout. There is
diffuse colonic diverticulosis without evidence of acute diverticulitis.  The
appendix is visualized and grossly unremarkable.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The visualized reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Mild atherosclerotic disease
is noted.

BONES:
There are moderate multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracolumbar
spine.

SOFT TISSUES:
There is a small fat containing umbilical hernia.

resolve.reason: The ordered right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is precisely the modality the CT report deferred to for gallstone detection and wall assessment, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets the CT's acknowledged insensitivity for gallstones, wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid in the setting of RUQ pain and peri-gallbladder fat stranding.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT explicitly flagged peri-gallbladder fat stranding and a distended gallbladder while acknowledging ultrasound's superior sensitivity for stones and wall changes, making a targeted gallbladder ultrasound the logical and well-motivated next step.

baseline.patient_history: ___ man with alcohol abuse, chronic pancreatitis, PUD  complicated by perforation s/p repair this past ___, CBD  stricture s/p ERCP with 2 stents ___ and mesenteric ischemia  s/p celiac artery stent who is s/p ERCP with stent removal and  gallstone removal ___ (yesterday) who presented to ___ last evening with abdominal pain following ERCP.     The patient does not remember much of what happened following  the ERCP except that he started having pain. His history is  otherwise very limited and obtained largely from medical records  and discussion with transferring physician. Per OSH transferring  MD, on admisssion there was no clear etiology to cause of pain  and he was treated conservatively with IVFs, Pip-Tazo and pain  control. The morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm  103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening  throughout the day. LFTs were non-obstructive and largely stable  from prior values. CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia,  mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of  right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma. Last  lactate 1.8. Based on severity of illness and recent ERCP he was  transferred for evaluation for complications following ERCP. why_trace.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP complication: hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible expansion or rupture, biloma, hepatic abscess, biliary perforation/leak, cholangitis, or mesenteric ischemia recurrence given prior celiac/SMA stents
why_trace.information_gap: The outside CT described pneumobilia and a possible subcapsular hepatic hematoma with high-density fluid, but this institution has no cross-sectional imaging of its own to characterize the extent, stability, or evolution of these findings — specifically whether the hematoma has expanded, ruptured, or become infected, and whether there is a biliary leak or abscess forming
why_trace.expected_finding: Characterization of the right hepatic subcapsular collection (size, density, rim enhancement suggesting abscess vs. simple hematoma), evidence of active bleeding or contrast extravasation, pneumobilia extent, free intraperitoneal fluid or air suggesting biliary perforation, and assessment of celiac/SMA stent patency given history of mesenteric ischemia
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Vitals: '100.1 PO 95/61 R Sitting 119 18 97 2L' — hypotension and tachycardia on arrival
Physical exam: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL', 'Bicarbonate: 16.0 mEq/L', 'Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L' — leukocytosis with metabolic acidosis
History: 'Mesenteric ischemia s/p celiac artery stent placement...s/p SMA stent placement' — vascular history requiring assessment
History: 'transferred for evaluation for complications following ERCP'
Imaging already resulted: '(none — only the baseline workup below was available)' — no imaging performed at this institution yet

discordance.evidence: Vitals prior to transfer: 't: 103.3 HR 115 RR 18 BP 159/22'; admission vitals: '95 / 61 R Sitting 119'; WBC 23.0 K/uL with 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 20.7 K/uL'; 'Lactate: 0.8 mmol/L' (current, at this institution) vs OSH 'Last lactate 1.8'; 'Bicarbonate: 16.0 mEq/L' and 'Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L'; CT at OSH revealed 'mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma' yet the patient has 'diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding' with fever, tachycardia, and hypotension (BP 95/61) — hemodynamic instability and markedly elevated WBC with high anion gap metabolic acidosis are out of proportion to what a simple post-ERCP subcapsular hematoma alone would explain, raising concern for an uncontrolled septic/ischemic process not yet imaged at this institution

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered contrast-enhanced CT abdomen directly targets the discordance by providing this institution's own cross-sectional imaging to characterize the hepatic collection, identify perforation, abscess, or biliary leak, and assess vascular stent patency — all of which could explain the hemodynamic instability and metabolic derangement that are out of proportion to the outside hospital's findings, and the reasoning explicitly frames this as the central clinical uncertainty.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient was transferred with an outside CT report of hepatic subcapsular hematoma and pneumobilia post-ERCP, but no imaging has been performed at this institution; with worsening fever (103.4→100.1 on arrival), leukocytosis (23k), metabolic acidosis (bicarb 16, AG 21), tachycardia, and RUQ guarding, a contrast-enhanced CT is essential to directly characterize the hepatic lesion, identify any biliary complication, and assess vascular patency.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen/pelvis with oral and intravenous contrast

TECHNIQUE:
Helical images were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis
following administration of oral and IV contrast material.  Coronal and
sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Total DLP = 307.19.

FINDINGS:


CHEST:
Trace left pleural effusion with mild adjacent left basilar
atelectasis.

HEPATOBILIARY:
There has been interval removal of a common bile duct stent. 
There is significant pneumobilia in the common bile duct extending into the
left intrahepatic bile duct and a trace amount extending into the right
intrahepatic bile duct.  There is also gas within the cystic duct and nearly
completely filling the gallbladder.  Additionally, there is very high density
material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII
spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm (02:17).  The hepatic parenchyma
demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is somewhat atrophic with numerous scattered
calcifications which are likely the sequela of chronic pancreatitis.  There is
no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesion or laceration.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
There is a percutaneous gastrostomy tube.  The stomach is
otherwise unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall
thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Multiple surgical clips are seen
adjacent to the second portion of the duodenum Diverticulosis of the sigmoid
colon is noted, without evidence of wall thickening and fat stranding.  The
appendix is not visualized.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm or retroperitoneal hematoma. 
Extensive atherosclerotic disease is noted.  There are stents at the origins
of the celiac and superior mesenteric arteries.  While this examination was
not tailored for evaluation of the arteries, atherosclerotic disease may cause
significant stenosis of the celiac axis, common hepatic artery, splenic
artery, as proximal superior mesenteric artery, bilateral renal arteries,
right external and internal iliac arteries, and right superficial femoral
artery.  There was the known thrombus in the celiac stent on the prior study.

BONES:
There is no acute fracture.  Anterior height loss of the T12 vertebral
body is unchanged compared ___ suggesting prior compression
fracture.  Irregularity of the posterolateral right ninth through eleventh
ribs and posterolateral left ninth and tenth ribs suggests prior fractures. 
No focal suspicious osseous abnormality.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP biliary complication: bile duct injury, biloma, or biliary leak; hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible biliary communication; cholangitis; hepatic abscess formation; or vascular injury to hepatic arterial supply compromising biliary tree
why_trace.information_gap: The CT shows a subcapsular hepatic collection (gas + high-density fluid at segment V/VIII) and extensive pneumobilia after ERCP stent removal, but cannot characterize whether there is an active biliary leak, bile duct disruption, or injury to the intrahepatic biliary radicles that would explain the collection and the patient's septic picture
why_trace.expected_finding: MRCP is expected to delineate the intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary anatomy in detail — specifically to identify a biliary leak or ductal disruption communicating with the subcapsular collection, residual filling defects (stones), stricture, or biliary-vascular fistula that CT contrast could not resolve
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'Based on severity of illness and recent ERCP he was transferred for evaluation for complications following ERCP'
Prior imaging: 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'

Prior imaging: 'significant pneumobilia in the common bile duct extending into the left intrahepatic bile duct and a trace amount extending into the right intrahepatic bile duct... gas within the cystic duct and nearly completely filling the gallbladder'
History: 'CBD stricture s/p CBD stent placement ___, s/p ERCP ___ for removal of stents'
History: 'ERCP with stent removal and gallstone removal ___ (yesterday)'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL'; 'Lactate: 0.8 mmol/L'; temperature 100.1 on admission after Tm 103.4 at OSH
Physical exam: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'

study_inadequacy.evidence: The appendix is not visualized.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The MRCP is well-suited and well-reasoned for the dominant post-ERCP biliary injury question, but it does not visualize the appendix (which was not seen on CT), leaving that study-inadequacy flag unaddressed.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT has identified a post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular gas-and-fluid collection with extensive pneumobilia but cannot characterize biliary ductal integrity; MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for mapping biliary anatomy and detecting leaks or ductal injuries that would guide the next intervention (repeat ERCP, percutaneous drainage, or surgery).

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen/pelvis with oral and intravenous contrast

TECHNIQUE:
Helical images were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis
following administration of oral and IV contrast material.  Coronal and
sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Total DLP = 307.19.

FINDINGS:


CHEST:
Trace left pleural effusion with mild adjacent left basilar
atelectasis.

HEPATOBILIARY:
There has been interval removal of a common bile duct stent. 
There is significant pneumobilia in the common bile duct extending into the
left intrahepatic bile duct and a trace amount extending into the right
intrahepatic bile duct.  There is also gas within the cystic duct and nearly
completely filling the gallbladder.  Additionally, there is very high density
material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII
spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm (02:17).  The hepatic parenchyma
demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is somewhat atrophic with numerous scattered
calcifications which are likely the sequela of chronic pancreatitis.  There is
no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesion or laceration.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
There is a percutaneous gastrostomy tube.  The stomach is
otherwise unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall
thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Multiple surgical clips are seen
adjacent to the second portion of the duodenum Diverticulosis of the sigmoid
colon is noted, without evidence of wall thickening and fat stranding.  The
appendix is not visualized.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm or retroperitoneal hematoma. 
Extensive atherosclerotic disease is noted.  There are stents at the origins
of the celiac and superior mesenteric arteries.  While this examination was
not tailored for evaluation of the arteries, atherosclerotic disease may cause
significant stenosis of the celiac axis, common hepatic artery, splenic
artery, as proximal superior mesenteric artery, bilateral renal arteries,
right external and internal iliac arteries, and right superficial femoral
artery.  There was the known thrombus in the celiac stent on the prior study.

BONES:
There is no acute fracture.  Anterior height loss of the T12 vertebral
body is unchanged compared ___ suggesting prior compression
fracture.  Irregularity of the posterolateral right ninth through eleventh
ribs and posterolateral left ninth and tenth ribs suggests prior fractures. 
No focal suspicious osseous abnormality.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

EXAMINATION:
MRCP:

TECHNIQUE:
T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen were
acquired in a 1.5 T magnet.
Intravenous contrast: 4.5 mL Gadavist.
Oral contrast:  1 cc of Gadavist mixed with 50 cc of water was administered
for oral contrast.

FINDINGS:
Lower Thorax: There are small right and trace left pleural effusions with
minimal adjacent atelectasis.

Liver: There is an approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense,
nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII (7:9). 
There are additional, smaller T2 hyperintense foci in the subcapsular aspect
of segment VIII more laterally measuring 1.1 x 0.6 cm and 1.1 x 0.2 cm (07:10,
1502:92).  There is out of phase compared to in phase loss of signal
suggesting hemosiderin deposition.  The liver otherwise demonstrates
homogeneous signal intensity throughout with no focal lesions identified.

Biliary: There is pneumobilia within the common bile duct, cystic duct,
gallbladder, and left intrahepatic bile ducts.  There is no intrahepatic or
extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  There is an aberrant insertion of the
posterior right intrahepatic bile duct on the left intrahepatic bile duct. 
There is no peribiliary enhancement.

Pancreas: The pancreas is atrophic with no focal lesions identified.  The main
pancreatic duct is top normal in diameter at 3 mm with a somewhat lobulated
contour in keeping with known chronic pancreatitis.

Spleen: The spleen is homogeneous in signal attenuation and enhancement
throughout with out of phase compared to in phase signal loss suggesting
hemosiderin deposition.  Small enhancing focus measuring 8 mm at the upper
pole of the spleen (series 1501, image 92) may reflect a small hemangioma.

Adrenal Glands: The right and left adrenal glands are normal and symmetric in
size and shape.

Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric and normal in size, signal intensity, and
enhancement.  There is a 4 x 6 mm T1 hyperintense, T2 hypointense,
nonenhancing lesion arising from the superior aspect of the right kidney
consistent with a proteinaceous or hemorrhagic cyst.

Gastrointestinal Tract:  A percutaneous gastrostomy tube is present.  The
stomach is otherwise grossly unremarkable.  Visualized loops of small bowel
are normal in caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement.  There is abnormal
thickening and mild hyper enhancement of the wall of the cecum and ascending
colon.

Lymph Nodes: There is no visualized retroperitoneal or mesenteric
lymphadenopathy.

Vasculature: There are vascular stents at the origins of the celiac and
superior mesenteric arteries.  There is no aneurysmal dilatation of the
visualized abdominal aorta.  The hepatic portal, superior mesenteric, and
splenic veins are patent.

Osseous and Soft Tissue Structures: No suspicious osseous lesion.  Generally
low signal intensity bone may reflect iron deposition.

NOTIFICATION:
The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 7:14 ___, less than 30
minutes after discovery of the findings.

TECHNIQUE:
PA and lateral radiographs of the chest

FINDINGS:
The lungs are mildly hyperinflated with diffuse prominence of interstitial
markings and lung vasculature.  There are bilateral small pleural effusions. 
Mild cardiomegaly.  Diffuse demineralization with bilateral acromioclavicular
arthropathy.

TECHNIQUE:
Chest PA and lateral

FINDINGS:
Bilateral pleural effusions, greater on the right with overlying atelectasis. 
No pneumothorax identified.  The size of the cardiomediastinal silhouette is
within normal limits.  Unchanged prominent interstitial markings bilaterally.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible active bleeding or expansion; biloma; hepatic abscess; biliary complication (bile leak, biliary obstruction); cholangitis
why_trace.information_gap: Whether the known hepatic subcapsular collection (segment V/VIII on CT, segment VIII on MRCP) is stable, expanding, or showing signs of active hemorrhage — and whether there is new or evolving free fluid or biliary pathology — without additional radiation exposure and at the bedside given the patient's clinical deterioration (fever to 103.4, WBC 23k, worsening pain, hemodynamic instability).
why_trace.expected_finding: Interval change in size of the hepatic subcapsular hematoma/collection (growth suggesting active bleeding or superinfection), new perihepatic free fluid suggesting bile leak or hemoperitoneum, or gallbladder wall changes suggesting cholecystitis as a contributing source.
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'the morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Physical examination: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Prior imaging 1 (CT): 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'

Prior imaging 2 (MRCP): 'approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII' with 'additional, smaller T2 hyperintense foci'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL', 'Hemoglobin: 9.7 g/dL', 'Hematocrit: 30.0 %'
Vitals: '100.1 PO 95 / 61 R Sitting 119 18 97 2L' — hypotension and tachycardia on admission

study_inadequacy.evidence: The appendix is not visualized.

discordance.evidence: There is abnormal thickening and mild hyper enhancement of the wall of the cecum and ascending colon. [MRCP] ... Vitals prior to transfer t: 103.3 HR 115 RR 18 BP 159/22 ... WBCs of 18k ... On admission WBC 23.0 K/uL, Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 16.0 mEq/L ... the appendix is not visualized [CT]

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The limited single-organ hepatic ultrasound and its reasoning are entirely focused on the known subcapsular hematoma/bile leak and cannot visualize the cecum/ascending colon or the appendix, leaving both the discordant colonic wall thickening (raising concern for ischemic/infectious colitis in a patient with mesenteric vascular disease) and the unvisualized appendix completely unaddressed.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient has a known hepatic subcapsular collection identified on both CT and MRCP following ERCP, is now clinically deteriorating with fever, leukocytosis to 23k, tachycardia, and worsening RUQ pain, making bedside ultrasound a rational, radiation-free, rapid tool to assess for interval expansion of the collection or new free fluid before deciding on invasive intervention.

baseline.patient_history: ___ man with alcohol abuse, chronic pancreatitis, PUD  complicated by perforation s/p repair this past ___, CBD  stricture s/p ERCP with 2 stents ___ and mesenteric ischemia  s/p celiac artery stent who is s/p ERCP with stent removal and  gallstone removal ___ (yesterday) who presented to ___ last evening with abdominal pain following ERCP.     The patient does not remember much of what happened following  the ERCP except that he started having pain. His history is  otherwise very limited and obtained largely from medical records  and discussion with transferring physician. Per OSH transferring  MD, on admisssion there was no clear etiology to cause of pain  and he was treated conservatively with IVFs, Pip-Tazo and pain  control. The morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm  103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening  throughout the day. LFTs were non-obstructive and largely stable  from prior values. CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia,  mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of  right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma. Last  lactate 1.8. Based on severity of illness and recent ERCP he was  transferred for evaluation for complications following ERCP. He cannot  recall much of history except that he "had an endoscopy, I don't  know what for but that's ok". He reports fevers, chills, in fact  he feels extremely cold at this moment, he reports severe  abdominal pain mostly in RUQ and worse with movement or touching  the area. He reports and vomiting of bilious fluid but no blood,  he cannot recall when his last BM was. No lightheadedness,  dizziness, vision changes, chest pain or SOB. why_trace.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible expansion or superinfection; biloma or hepatic abscess formation; biliary leak; cholangitis; percutaneous drain/catheter position assessment in gallbladder
why_trace.information_gap: Whether the known hepatic subcapsular fluid collection (segment V/VIII) has changed in size, developed new characteristics suggesting active bleeding or abscess formation, and whether the gallbladder catheter seen on prior ultrasound is appropriately positioned and draining — information that bedside ultrasound can provide rapidly without radiation or contrast.
why_trace.expected_finding: Interval change in the subcapsular hepatic fluid collection (enlargement suggesting active hemorrhage, or new internal echogenicity/debris suggesting superinfection/abscess), assessment of perihepatic free fluid, and confirmation of gallbladder catheter position and any residual gallbladder distension or pericholecystic fluid.
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'The morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Physical examination: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL' and 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 20.7 K/uL'
Prior imaging 1 (CT): 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'
Prior imaging 2 (MRCP): 'approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII'

Prior imaging 5 (US): 'A catheter is present looped in the expected location of the gallbladder without a fluid collection seen. The gallbladder is contracted' and 'Trace perihepatic and subhepatic fluid is noted'
Vitals: '100.1 PO 95 / 61 R Sitting 119 18 97 2L' — hypotension and tachycardia suggesting hemodynamic instability

study_inadequacy.evidence: Study was technically difficult secondary to overlying bandages and images are limited... No focal liver lesion is seen on this limited exam.

discordance.evidence: There is abnormal thickening and mild hyper enhancement of the wall of the cecum and ascending colon. [MRCP] ... Vitals prior to transfer t: 103.3 HR 115 RR 18 BP 159/22 ... WBCs of 18k ... There are stents at the origins of the celiac and superior mesenteric arteries ... There was the known thrombus in the celiac stent on the prior study.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered limited abdominal ultrasound with its reasoning is appropriately directed at reassessing the known hepatic subcapsular hematoma (addressing the study_inadequacy flag), but it entirely ignores the more pressing discordance flag — the unexplained cecal/ascending colon wall thickening in a patient with known celiac/SMA stents and prior in-stent thrombosis, fever, tachycardia, and marked leukocytosis suggesting possible mesenteric ischemia — which ultrasound is poorly suited to evaluate and which the physician's reasoning does not acknowledge.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient has a known hepatic subcapsular hematoma on prior CT/MRCP, is now febrile to 103.3°F with WBC 23K, worsening RUQ pain, and guarding — a repeat bedside ultrasound is a rational, rapid, radiation-free method to assess for interval progression of the collection or new complications at the bedside in a critically ill patient.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen/pelvis with oral and intravenous contrast

TECHNIQUE:
Helical images were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis
following administration of oral and IV contrast material.  Coronal and
sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Total DLP = 307.19.

FINDINGS:


CHEST:
Trace left pleural effusion with mild adjacent left basilar
atelectasis.

HEPATOBILIARY:
There has been interval removal of a common bile duct stent. 
There is significant pneumobilia in the common bile duct extending into the
left intrahepatic bile duct and a trace amount extending into the right
intrahepatic bile duct.  There is also gas within the cystic duct and nearly
completely filling the gallbladder.  Additionally, there is very high density
material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII
spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm (02:17).  The hepatic parenchyma
demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is somewhat atrophic with numerous scattered
calcifications which are likely the sequela of chronic pancreatitis.  There is
no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesion or laceration.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
There is a percutaneous gastrostomy tube.  The stomach is
otherwise unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall
thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Multiple surgical clips are seen
adjacent to the second portion of the duodenum Diverticulosis of the sigmoid
colon is noted, without evidence of wall thickening and fat stranding.  The
appendix is not visualized.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm or retroperitoneal hematoma. 
Extensive atherosclerotic disease is noted.  There are stents at the origins
of the celiac and superior mesenteric arteries.  While this examination was
not tailored for evaluation of the arteries, atherosclerotic disease may cause
significant stenosis of the celiac axis, common hepatic artery, splenic
artery, as proximal superior mesenteric artery, bilateral renal arteries,
right external and internal iliac arteries, and right superficial femoral
artery.  There was the known thrombus in the celiac stent on the prior study.

BONES:
There is no acute fracture.  Anterior height loss of the T12 vertebral
body is unchanged compared ___ suggesting prior compression
fracture.  Irregularity of the posterolateral right ninth through eleventh
ribs and posterolateral left ninth and tenth ribs suggests prior fractures. 
No focal suspicious osseous abnormality.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

EXAMINATION:
MRCP:

TECHNIQUE:
T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen were
acquired in a 1.5 T magnet.
Intravenous contrast: 4.5 mL Gadavist.
Oral contrast:  1 cc of Gadavist mixed with 50 cc of water was administered
for oral contrast.

FINDINGS:
Lower Thorax: There are small right and trace left pleural effusions with
minimal adjacent atelectasis.

Liver: There is an approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense,
nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII (7:9). 
There are additional, smaller T2 hyperintense foci in the subcapsular aspect
of segment VIII more laterally measuring 1.1 x 0.6 cm and 1.1 x 0.2 cm (07:10,
1502:92).  There is out of phase compared to in phase loss of signal
suggesting hemosiderin deposition.  The liver otherwise demonstrates
homogeneous signal intensity throughout with no focal lesions identified.

Biliary: There is pneumobilia within the common bile duct, cystic duct,
gallbladder, and left intrahepatic bile ducts.  There is no intrahepatic or
extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  There is an aberrant insertion of the
posterior right intrahepatic bile duct on the left intrahepatic bile duct. 
There is no peribiliary enhancement.

Pancreas: The pancreas is atrophic with no focal lesions identified.  The main
pancreatic duct is top normal in diameter at 3 mm with a somewhat lobulated
contour in keeping with known chronic pancreatitis.

Spleen: The spleen is homogeneous in signal attenuation and enhancement
throughout with out of phase compared to in phase signal loss suggesting
hemosiderin deposition.  Small enhancing focus measuring 8 mm at the upper
pole of the spleen (series 1501, image 92) may reflect a small hemangioma.

Adrenal Glands: The right and left adrenal glands are normal and symmetric in
size and shape.

Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric and normal in size, signal intensity, and
enhancement.  There is a 4 x 6 mm T1 hyperintense, T2 hypointense,
nonenhancing lesion arising from the superior aspect of the right kidney
consistent with a proteinaceous or hemorrhagic cyst.

Gastrointestinal Tract:  A percutaneous gastrostomy tube is present.  The
stomach is otherwise grossly unremarkable.  Visualized loops of small bowel
are normal in caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement.  There is abnormal
thickening and mild hyper enhancement of the wall of the cecum and ascending
colon.

Lymph Nodes: There is no visualized retroperitoneal or mesenteric
lymphadenopathy.

Vasculature: There are vascular stents at the origins of the celiac and
superior mesenteric arteries.  There is no aneurysmal dilatation of the
visualized abdominal aorta.  The hepatic portal, superior mesenteric, and
splenic veins are patent.

Osseous and Soft Tissue Structures: No suspicious osseous lesion.  Generally
low signal intensity bone may reflect iron deposition.
