why_trace.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP complication: hepatic subcapsular hematoma expansion, biloma, hepatic abscess, biliary leak, or vascular injury (hepatic artery/portal vein); also mesenteric ischemia given known celiac/SMA stents with possible in-stent stenosis/thrombosis; ascending cholangitis; intraperitoneal hemorrhage
why_trace.information_gap: Whether the known subcapsular hepatic collection (segment V/VIII) has expanded, developed into an abscess, or is associated with a biliary leak or active hemorrhage — and whether there is a new or evolving intra-abdominal process (e.g., vascular compromise, free perforation, or peritonitis) explaining the clinical deterioration (fever to 103.4, WBC 23k, worsening RUQ pain, altered mental status, tachycardia, hypotension) since the prior MRCP/CT
why_trace.expected_finding: Interval change in the hepatic subcapsular collection (enlargement, new rim enhancement suggesting abscess formation, or active extravasation of contrast suggesting hemorrhage); alternatively, new free fluid, pneumoperitoneum, bowel wall ischemia, or biliary leak with perihepatic fluid collection
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'the morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL' and 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 20.7 K/uL'
Vitals: '100.1 PO 95/61 R Sitting 119 18 97 2L' — hypotension and tachycardia on admission
Physical exam: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Prior imaging 2 (MRCP): 'approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII'
Prior imaging 1 (CT): 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'
History: 'mesenteric ischemia s/p celiac artery stent' and CT: 'known thrombus in the celiac stent on the prior study'
History: 'presented to ___ last evening with abdominal pain following ERCP' — temporal relationship to instrumentation
Labs: 'Lactate: 0.8 mmol/L' — currently not elevated but prior lactate 1.8 at OSH; 'Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L' — elevated

study_inadequacy.evidence: Study was technically difficult secondary to overlying bandages and images are limited... No focal liver lesion is seen on this limited exam.

discordance.evidence: There is abnormal thickening and mild hyper enhancement of the wall of the cecum and ascending colon. [MRCP] ... Vitals prior to transfer t: 103.3 HR 115 RR 18 BP 159/22 O2% 96% ... WBCs of 18k ... WBC on admission 23.0 K/uL ... Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L ... Bicarbonate: 16.0 mEq/L ... history of mesenteric ischemia s/p celiac artery stent placement ... atherosclerotic disease may cause significant stenosis of the celiac axis, common hepatic artery, splenic artery, as proximal superior mesenteric artery

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is capable of both characterizing the technically limited subcapsular hepatic collection (resolving the study inadequacy) and evaluating bowel wall enhancement/ischemia to adjudicate the discordant cecal/ascending colon thickening in the context of known mesenteric vascular disease, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets both the hepatic collection interval change and mesenteric ischemia as concurrent concerns driving the order.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient has clinically deteriorated since prior imaging (rising WBC from 18k to 23k, persistent high fever, worsening RUQ pain, altered mental status, tachycardia, and hypotension) with a known post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular collection and complex vascular history, making interval CT with IV contrast the appropriate next step to reassess the collection and exclude new or evolving intra-abdominal catastrophe.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
FINDINGS:
The liver has a normal homogeneous echotexture and smooth contour.  There are
no focal liver lesions.  The main portal vein is patent with appropriate
hepatopetal flow.  There is no intra or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  A
2.8-cm mobile cluster of large shadowing gallstones is noted, but there is no
gallbladder wall edema, pericholecystic fluid or tense gallbladder distention.
The sonographic ___ sign is negative.  Visualized portions of the
pancreas are homogeneous.  The body and tail obscured by overlying bowel gas. 
The spleen is normal.  There is no ascites.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: ureterolithiasis/renal colic, mesenteric adenitis, ovarian pathology (torsion or cyst), right-sided colonic pathology
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound identified gallstones but no sonographic evidence of acute cholecystitis, and it could not evaluate the appendix, right colon, retroperitoneum, or pelvic structures — leaving the anatomic source of RUQ/RLQ pain and right flank tenderness unidentified.
why_trace.expected_finding: Periappendiceal inflammatory changes consistent with appendicitis, OR a right ureteral stone with hydronephrosis explaining the flank tenderness, OR colonic/pericolonic inflammation suggesting diverticulitis or another bowel process
grounding: Physical examination: 'tender in RUQ and RLQ with no rebound/guarding... also had right flank tenderness'
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 11.7 K/uL' and 'Neutrophils: 91.6 %'

Prior imaging: 'A 2.8-cm mobile cluster of large shadowing gallstones is noted, but there is no gallbladder wall edema, pericholecystic fluid or tense gallbladder distention. The sonographic ___ sign is negative.'
Prior imaging: 'The body and tail obscured by overlying bowel gas' — pancreas incompletely visualized
Laboratory tests: 'Leukocytes: NEG... Blood: NEG' on urinalysis — makes simple UTI/hematuria less likely but does not exclude stone
History: 'progressive abd pain that started at 0300 and was periumbilical then moving to RUQ and RLQ' — migration pattern raises appendicitis concern
Laboratory tests: 'HCG, Urine, Qualitative: NEGATIVE' — ectopic pregnancy excluded

study_inadequacy.evidence: The body and tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

resolve.reason: The liver/gallbladder ultrasound can evaluate for biliary pathology contributing to the RUQ tenderness, but its narrow single-organ scope does not address the broader discordance between a soft non-peritoneal abdomen and the combination of hypotension, elevated anion gap, leukocytosis, and significant hematuria, which together suggest a more systemic or multi-organ process requiring wider evaluation.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With RUQ/RLQ tenderness, a self-reported history of liver disease, no prior imaging, and labs that do not clearly point to a single etiology, a liver/gallbladder ultrasound is the logical first-line imaging step to evaluate biliary and hepatic structures non-invasively and without radiation.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits.  The contour of
the liver is smooth.  There is no focal liver mass.  The main portal vein is
patent with hepatopetal flow.  There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation.  The CHD measures 2 mm.

GALLBLADDER:
There is no evidence of stones or gallbladder wall thickening.

PANCREAS:
The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

SPLEEN:
Normal echogenicity, measuring 6.6 cm.

KIDNEYS:
The right kidney measures 10.1 cm.  The left kidney measures 9.3 cm. 
Normal cortical echogenicity and corticomedullary differentiation is seen
bilaterally.  There is no evidence of masses, stones, or hydronephrosis in the
kidneys.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

Focal images were taken of the right lower quadrant, overlying the area of
concern as indicated by the patient.  The appendix is not able to be
visualized.  There is however a echogenic shadowing structure with focal mild
mass effect and relatively heterogeneous appearance of the fat.  Please see
subsequent CT scan for additional details..

why_trace.other_hypothesis: right-sided colonic pathology (e.g., cecal/ascending colon mass, Crohn's disease, mesenteric adenitis, epiploic appendagitis, right-sided colitis)
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound identified an echogenic shadowing structure with focal mild mass effect and heterogeneous fat in the right lower quadrant but could not visualize the appendix, leaving the nature of the RLQ abnormality undefined — CT is needed to characterize this structure and determine whether it represents appendicitis, diverticulitis, a mass, or fat stranding from another cause.
why_trace.expected_finding: Visualization of the appendix (dilated, wall-thickened, periappendiceal fat stranding consistent with appendicitis) OR a pericolic inflammatory process (diverticulitis of the cecum/ascending colon) OR an alternative RLQ mass/inflammatory focus explaining the echogenic shadowing seen on ultrasound
grounding: Prior imaging: 'The appendix is not able to be visualized. There is however a echogenic shadowing structure with focal mild mass effect and relatively heterogeneous appearance of the fat. Please see subsequent CT scan for additional details.'

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound explicitly identified an abnormal RLQ echogenic structure with fat heterogeneity but could not visualize the appendix, and the report itself directs to CT for additional details — CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the logical and standard next step to characterize this finding in a patient with RLQ tenderness, leukocytosis (WBC 14.1), and elevated anion gap.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast

TECHNIQUE:
Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Total DLP (Body) = 332 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
Visualized lung fields are within normal limits. There is no
evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic
or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within normal limits.
There is a small amount of intra-abdominal free air seen non dependently,
surrounding the liver.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:
There is a nodule in the left adrenal gland measuring 2.5 x 1.7 cm
(series 2: Image 16).  The right adrenal gland appears unremarkable.

URINARY:
There are patchy hypodensities seen predominantly in the lower poles
of the bilateral kidneys, which are concerning for pyelonephritis.  No
definite fluid collection is seen concerning for abscess formation.  There is
no hydronephrosis bilaterally.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
There is an area of apparent focal thickening in the
stomach, likely the antrum in the setting of underdistention (2: 21, 22).  The
remaining stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal
caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout. There is intra-abdominal
free air with a possible large focus of extraluminal air adjacent to a segment
of sigmoid colon, which may be the suspected region of perforation (series 2:
Image 45).  Scattered diverticula are noted in the sigmoid and descending
colon.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder is largely collapsed.  The distal ureters are
unremarkable.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The patient is status post hysterectomy.  A pessary is
noted in the vagina.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Extensive atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES:
There is severe levoconvex scoliosis in the lumbosacral spine with
associated degenerative changes.  Cortical deformities are seen involving the
left inferior and superior pubic rami, likely sequela of prior fractures. 
Chronic nondisplaced fracture of the left greater trochanter is also noted. 
No acute fractures identified.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Perforated diverticulitis with evolving/worsening peritonitis; pyelonephritis progression; possible contained perforation requiring interval reassessment
why_trace.information_gap: The prior CT identified a possible large focus of extraluminal air adjacent to sigmoid colon as a suspected perforation site, but the clinical picture (worsening LLQ pain, leukocytosis 18.3K, bandemia 6%, elevated lactate 2.5, anion gap 22, no flatus today) raises concern that the process may be evolving — the physician needs to reassess the extent of perforation, peritoneal contamination, and whether a drainable collection or worsening free air has developed since the first scan.
why_trace.expected_finding: Interval change in the amount of free intra-abdominal air adjacent to the sigmoid colon, development of a pericolic abscess or phlegmon, or progression of peritoneal contamination that would guide operative versus interventional versus continued non-operative management of the suspected sigmoid perforation.
grounding: Prior imaging: 'There is intra-abdominal free air with a possible large focus of extraluminal air adjacent to a segment of sigmoid colon, which may be the suspected region of perforation (series 2: Image 45)'

discordance.evidence: CT: 'There is intra-abdominal free air with a possible large focus of extraluminal air adjacent to a segment of sigmoid colon, which may be the suspected region of perforation' — yet physical exam shows 'Soft, nondistended, tender to palpation in the RLQ and LLQ with voluntary guarding' without peritoneal signs, and labs show WBC 18.3 K/uL, lactate 2.5 mmol/L (elevated), anion gap 22 mEq/L (elevated), sodium 129 mEq/L — imaging evidence of free perforation with a relatively benign abdominal exam is a marked discordance that cannot be explained by the leading diagnosis of gastroenteritis/diverticulitis alone.

resolve.reason: The repeat contrast CT directly targets the discordance between imaging-confirmed free air and a relatively benign exam by reassessing interval change in perforation extent, peritoneal contamination, and abscess formation, and the reasoning explicitly frames this as driving the operative-versus-non-operative decision — squarely addressing the flagged concern.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: A repeat contrast CT is clinically motivated by the evolving sepsis physiology (rising lactate, leukocytosis, no flatus) and the prior CT's inconclusive characterization of the perforation site, but repeating the same modality so soon raises radiation and contrast concerns and would ideally be justified by a documented clinical change or a specific surgical decision point.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
MDCT-acquired axial images were obtained through the abdomen and
pelvis after administration of 130 ml IV Optiray contrast.  Coronal and
sagittal reformats were displayed.

CT ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:
There is dependent subsegmental atelectasis, but
the lung bases are otherwise clear.  Soft tissue density and coarse
calcifications are noted in the right breast which is incompletely imaged. 
Visualized portion of the heart is unremarkable.

the liver, gallbladder, pancreas, spleen and bilateral adrenal glands are
normal.  The kidneys enhance and excrete contrast symmetrically without
evidence of hydronephrosis or hydroureter.  The nonopacified stomach and
intra-abdominal loops of small bowel are normal.  There is mild focal wall
thickening of the cecum with minimal associated fat stranding, but the
remainder of the colon is unremarkable.  There is are scatter diverticula of
the cecum. The appendix is normal.  There is no free air or fluid in the
abdomen.  No mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy meeting CT criteria
for pathologic enlargement is noted.  There is atherosclerotic calcification
of the aorta which is of normal caliber throughout.

CT PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:
The urinary bladder, distal ureters, uterus,
sigmoid colon and rectum are normal.  There is a 4.6 x 3.0 cm hypodense cystic
structure in the right adnexa.  The left adnexa demonstrates a 1.7 x 1.5 cm
cystic structure.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.  No pelvic or
inguinal lymphadenopathy is noted.

BONE WINDOWS:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified. 
There is multilevel degenerative change of the thoracolumbar spine.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Ovarian/adnexal pathology (right ovarian cyst torsion, hemorrhagic cyst, or ruptured cyst) given the 4.6 x 3.0 cm right adnexal cystic structure on CT; cecal pathology (cecal diverticulitis or early cecal mass) is also a secondary concern
why_trace.information_gap: The CT identified a 4.6 x 3.0 cm right adnexal cystic structure that is incompletely characterized — its internal architecture, vascularity, and relationship to the right ovary are unknown, and it is unclear whether this structure is the source of the RLQ pain
why_trace.expected_finding: Ultrasound is expected to characterize the right adnexal cyst further — specifically to determine if it is a simple vs. complex ovarian cyst, assess for internal echoes/septations/debris suggesting hemorrhagic or torsed cyst, and evaluate Doppler flow to the right ovary to assess for torsion
grounding: CT Pelvis: 'There is a 4.6 x 3.0 cm hypodense cystic structure in the right adnexa.'
CT Pelvis: 'The left adnexa demonstrates a 1.7 x 1.5 cm cystic structure.'
Physical exam: 'RLQ slightly distended and very TTP with focal guarding and minimal rebound.'
CT Abdomen: 'There is mild focal wall thickening of the cecum with minimal associated fat stranding... There are scatter diverticula of the cecum.'
CT Abdomen: 'The appendix is normal.'
History: 'acute onset RLQ abdominal pain x12h... She has never had pain like this before.'

Chest PA and lateral

FINDINGS:
Heart size is normal.  Mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal
limits.  Pulmonary vasculature is not engorged.  Left upper lobe opacity
corresponds the known mass seen on previous chest CT.  Minimal streaky
opacification and reticulation is seen within the lower lobes, corresponding
to areas of minimal fibrosis seen on the recent CT, without new focal
consolidation.  No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is present.  No acute
osseous abnormality is demonstrated.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Colitis (UC flare vs. crizotinib-related colitis), colonic perforation, mesenteric ischemia, malignant complication (peritoneal/colonic metastasis), ovarian pathology (though TAH-BSO noted), intra-abdominal infection/abscess
why_trace.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the LLQ pain cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone — specifically whether there is diverticulitis, colitis, perforation, abscess, or a malignancy-related complication in the abdomen/pelvis
why_trace.expected_finding: Pericolic fat stranding with or without diverticulitis, colonic wall thickening consistent with colitis or crizotinib-related bowel toxicity, free air or fluid suggesting perforation, or an intra-abdominal mass/metastatic lesion
grounding: History: '2 days LLQ abd pain... constant w waves of increased intensity... intermittent chills'
History: 'stage IV NSCLCA on crizotinib, UC on balsalazide'
History: 'TAH BSO' (rules out ovarian/uterine source but not all pelvic pathology)
Physical examination: 'Soft, non-distended, non-tender' (exam does not localize or confirm peritonitis)
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 15.9 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)' and 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 13.19 K/uL (ref 1.6-6.1)' indicating leukocytosis with neutrophilia
Laboratory tests: 'Lactate: 1.1 mmol/L' (normal, argues against ischemia but does not exclude it)
Prior imaging 1: Chest X-ray shows no acute abdominal free air visible on chest film, but does not evaluate the abdomen

discordance.evidence: Physical exam: 'Abd: Soft, non-distended, non-tender' vs. labs: 'White Blood Cells: 15.9 K/uL' with 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 13.19 K/uL' and 'Neutrophils: 83.1 %', alongside 'Sodium: 131.0 mEq/L' and 'Potassium: 6.6 mEq/L'

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast can anatomically explain the leukocytosis by identifying an intra-abdominal source, but the physician's reasoning focuses entirely on localizing abdominal pathology and does not acknowledge or pursue the electrolyte discordance (hyponatremia + hyperkalemia) that independently raises concern for an adrenal or systemic process not resolvable by abdominal CT alone.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient has LLQ pain with leukocytosis (WBC 15.9, ANC 13.19), a complex oncologic and inflammatory bowel background, and a soft non-tender abdomen that does not localize the source — cross-sectional imaging is the appropriate next step to characterize the abdominal pathology.

EXAMINATION:
RENAL U.S.:

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the kidneys and
bladder were obtained.

FINDINGS:
The right kidney measures 11.6 cm. The left kidney measures 12.6 cm. 
Previously seen right hydroureteronephrosis has resolved.  A 1.0 cm echogenic
stone in the lower pole of the left kidney likely corresponds to
nonobstructing stone seen in the prior CT abdomen and pelvis from ___.  Again seen is a 4.4 cm simple cyst in the mid to lower pole of the
right kidney.  Normal cortical echogenicity and corticomedullary
differentiation are seen bilaterally.  No evidence of a fluid collection.

The bladder is moderately well distended and normal in appearance.  The
prostate measures 5.3 x 3.5 x 3.7 cm with a total volume of 35 cc.

why_trace.expected_finding: Pericolonic fat stranding with or without diverticular thickening/perforation in the sigmoid/left colon consistent with diverticulitis, or alternatively a pelvic fluid collection/abscess, free air, or adnexal mass that would point toward a gynecologic or other source

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
AP and lateral views the chest.

FINDINGS:
The lungs are clear besides mild right basilar atelectasis.  Cardiomediastinal
silhouette is stable with tortuosity of the descending thoracic aorta.  No
acute osseous abnormalities.  Surgical clips project over the left lateral
chest wall.

EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen and pelvis.

TECHNIQUE:
Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 3.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 14.4 mGy (Body) DLP =
7.2 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 6.4 s, 50.1 cm; CTDIvol = 20.6 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,033.1 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,040 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
There is mild bibasilar dependent atelectasis.  Otherwise, the
visualized lung fields are within normal limits. There is no evidence of
pleural effusion.  The heart is slightly enlarged, without evidence of
pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic
or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within normal limits.

PANCREAS:
Partial fatty replacement is noted.  Otherwise, the pancreas has
normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of focal lesions or pancreatic
ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
Small circumscribed hypodensities in the lower pole of the left
kidney, too small to characterize but statistically cysts.  Otherwise, the
kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram.  There is no
evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no perinephric
abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
There is a small hiatal hernia.  The stomach is
unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall thickness,
and enhancement throughout.
Multiple diverticuli in the distal thickened sigmoid associated with
significant stranding.  There is a sizable air in fluid collection seen
extending from the retrouterine pouch posteriorly and superiorly and above
portion of the sigmoid colon.  The portion inferiorly posterior to the cervix
measures approximately 2.8 x 1.2 by 3.6 cm.  The component superiorly measures
8.1 x 2.3 x 5.5 cm.)  These findings are suggestive of complicated
____.
The rest of the colon is unremarkable.  The appendix is normal.

PELVIS:
There is an indwelling catheter within the bladder.  Air within the
bladder is likely iatrogenic.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
Uterus and adnexae are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES:
Sclerotic focus in the right ilium likely represents a bone island. 
Degenerative changes in the thoracolumbar spine and hips are noted.  There is
no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Perforated diverticulitis with pelvic abscess; possible interval change in abscess size/complexity or development of new complications (fistula, free perforation) after drain removal
why_trace.information_gap: Whether the pelvic abscess has changed in size, resolved, or developed new complications (e.g., enlargement, fistula formation, free perforation) following drain removal, given that the physical exam notes 'drain removed' and the prior CT showed a sizable air-and-fluid collection
why_trace.expected_finding: Interval change in the retrouterine/pelvic air-fluid collection (either decrease in size suggesting treatment response, or persistence/enlargement suggesting inadequate drainage or ongoing infection) and assessment for new complications such as fistula or free perforation
grounding: CT ABD & PELVIS: 'sizable air in fluid collection seen extending from the retrouterine pouch posteriorly and superiorly... The component superiorly measures 8.1 x 2.3 x 5.5 cm'
CT ABD & PELVIS: 'Multiple diverticuli in the distal thickened sigmoid associated with significant stranding'
Physical examination: 'drain removed. drain site c/d/I'
Physical examination: 'soft, appropriately tender, mildly distended'
Laboratory tests: 'C-Reactive Protein: 136.6 mg/L (ref 0.0-5.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 12.1 K/uL' with 'Neutrophils: 85.8%' and 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 10.41 K/uL'
History: 'lower abdominal pain that has been progressively worsening... fevers to 101, chills, nausea and anorexia... increasing abdominal distension with no BM or flatus'

study_inadequacy.evidence: "These findings are suggestive of complicated ____." — the radiologist's conclusion is redacted/incomplete, leaving the characterization of the sizable air-and-fluid collection (up to 8.1 x 2.3 x 5.5 cm extending from the retrouterine pouch) unspecified and the clinical question of its exact nature (abscess vs. perforated diverticulitis with free collection vs. other) formally unanswered.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the same modality and region as the prior inadequate study and is fully capable of characterizing the pelvic air-and-fluid collection, while the physician's reasoning explicitly targets the unresolved nature of that collection (abscess resolution vs. persistence/new complication) — directly filling the gap left by the redacted prior report.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The prior CT demonstrated a sizable pelvic abscess (up to 8.1 x 2.3 x 5.5 cm) associated with sigmoid diverticulitis, a drain was placed and subsequently removed as noted on exam, and repeat cross-sectional imaging is the standard method to confirm adequate resolution or detect complications after drain removal in this clinical context.

The patient states she  was seen in clinic approximately just over two weeks ago, at  which time she was having episodes of feeling lightheaded and  dizzy.  She states these episodes briefly resolved and then she  subsequently developed sharp abdominal pain.  She states the  pain is primarily in the left lower quadrant but also radiates  to the left upper quadrant.  She then developed diarrhea, which  she describes as mucus combined with bright red blood.  Patient  reports that in past few days she has been having ___ BM per  day however these are low volume and essentially all "slime and  blood."  Patient then started to have the lightheadedness and  dizziness again.  Patient has tried ranitidine and Maalox  without any relief.  Patient states that only thing that  relieves the pain is defecation. The patient states that the  abdominal pain does not seem to be associated with food.  She  gets it throughout the day and then has a constant dull ache in  the area.  She states that she is now frustrated and concerned  as she feels like something very serious is going on. Patient  has had some nausea but no vomiting.  She has stated that she  maintains adequate PO intake. Patient reports subjective fevers  at home and stated that she soaked through the sheets last  night.  Patient has not measured her temperature at home.   Patient has a history of hematochezia in the past and has had a  colonoscopy in ___, which showed diverticulosis.   . Patient denies any associated chest pain or shortness of breath.   She has never had any abdominal surgeries.  She does have a  history of hemorrhoids but states that they are not active  currently.  No recent travel out of the country. No sick  contacts with similar symptoms.   . In the ED temp 98.9, 131/66, 86, 18, 98% on RA, pain was ___  on presentation, initial exam notable for tenderness over LLQ  and + guiac on rectal exam. resolve.reason: CT abdomen with contrast can visualize the pancreas and would incidentally detect pancreatitis or a pancreatic mass, but the physician's reasoning frames the scan entirely around colonic pathology and explicitly dismisses the markedly elevated lipase as only 'mildly elevated,' mirroring the same discordance the flag identifies — the intent is colonic localization, not adjudication of the pancreatic enzyme elevation.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Two weeks of LLQ pain with bloody mucoid diarrhea, a falling hematocrit, thrombocytosis (702 K/uL), known diverticulosis, and no prior imaging at this encounter make CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast the appropriate first-line cross-sectional study to characterize the colonic and mesenteric anatomy and identify a structural or vascular cause.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast only.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT-acquired axial images from the lung bases to the pubic
symphysis were obtained after the administration of 130 cc of Optiray
intravenous contrast using a CT enterography protocol.  Coronal and sagittal
reformations were obtained.  No oral contrast was administered.

FINDINGS:


CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:
There are dependent atelectatic changes at both lung bases.  No pleural
effusions, nodules, or opacities are seen at the lung bases.  The liver,
gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, small bowel, large bowel,
and both ureters are normal.  Within the left adrenal gland, there is a round
homogeneous lesion that measures fat attenuation and has characteristics
consistent with a benign adenoma.  Within both kidneys, there are multiple
rounded hypoattenuating lesions, the most prominent of which is seen in the
left interpole and measures 1.3 x 1.4 cm.  These lesions measure slightly
higher attenuation than simple fluid, and have characteristics consistent with
complex cysts with a Bosniak classification of IIF.  Other such lesions are
seen in the left kidney lower pole and the interpole of the right kidney.  No
mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is seen.  No free air or free
fluid is identified.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:
The rectum, sigmoid colon, bladder, both adnexa are unremarkable.  The sigmoid
colon demonstrates diverticula consistent with diverticulosis with no evidence
of ____.  Within the uterus, there is a round 1.9 x 3.1 cm, well-
circumscribed lesion that likely represents a fibroid.  No pelvic or inguinal
lymphadenopathy is seen.  No pelvic free fluid is identified.

BONE WINDOWS:
There are multilevel degenerative changes, most prominent at
the level of L4-L5 with grade 1 anterolisthesis of the L4 on L5 vertebral body
and loss of disc height with vacuum phenomenon seen at this level.  There is
also loss of disc height and vacuum phenomenon and a slight anterolisthesis of
the L5 on S1 vertebral body.  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are
seen.  There is fatty infiltration at the falciform ligament.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: ischemic colitis, inflammatory bowel disease (colitis), colonic malignancy — given 2-week course of LLQ pain, bloody mucoid diarrhea, markedly elevated platelets (702 K/uL), low MCV/microcytic indices, elevated lipase/amylase, and a prior CT that was notably non-diagnostic for the symptomatic area (sigmoid diverticulosis without definitive diverticulitis, and the word 'diverticulitis' appears truncated/redacted in the report)
why_trace.information_gap: The prior CT abdomen/pelvis was performed without oral contrast and the sigmoid/descending colon findings are incompletely characterized — the report notes diverticula but the word describing the inflammatory status is redacted ('no evidence of ____'), leaving it unclear whether active diverticulitis, colitis, or mucosal ischemia is present; additionally, the patient's clinical course has progressed (worsening bloody mucoid stools, recurrent lightheadedness, decreased Hct from prior) since the first scan, raising concern that the disease has evolved
why_trace.expected_finding: Wall thickening, pericolonic fat stranding, or mucosal hyperenhancement of the descending or sigmoid colon consistent with diverticulitis, ischemic colitis, or inflammatory colitis; alternatively, a new finding such as free fluid or abscess that was absent on the prior scan
grounding: History: 'she subsequently developed sharp abdominal pain... diarrhea, which she describes as mucus combined with bright red blood... ___ BM per day however these are low volume and essentially all slime and blood'
History: 'Patient was sent for stat labs and Hct noted to be decreased from previous'
History: 'Patient reports subjective fevers at home and stated that she soaked through the sheets last night'
Labs: 'Platelet Count: 702.0 K/uL (ref 150.0-440.0)' — marked thrombocytosis suggesting chronic inflammation or iron deficiency

Labs: 'MCV: 79.0 fL (ref 82.0-98.0)', 'MCH: 25.8 pg (ref 27.0-32.0)', 'Iron: 45.0 ug/dL', 'Iron Binding Capacity, Total: 204.0 ug/dL (ref 260.0-470.0)' — microcytic indices with low TIBC consistent with iron deficiency or chronic disease
Labs: 'Lipase: 221.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)', 'Amylase: 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)' — elevated pancreatic enzymes unexplained by prior imaging
Prior CT: 'The sigmoid colon demonstrates diverticula consistent with diverticulosis with no evidence of ____' — inflammatory status of sigmoid colon is redacted/unclear
Prior CT: 'No free air or free fluid is identified' — prior scan did not show perforation but did not definitively exclude active colitis
Physical exam: '+TTP in LLQ and LUQ... Conjunctivae mildly pale... MM dry'

discordance.evidence: Lipase: 221.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0); Amylase: 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0) — yet CT reports 'The liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, small bowel, large bowel, and both ureters are normal' and the clinical picture is LLQ pain attributed to diverticular disease; additionally Platelet Count: 702.0 K/uL (ref 150.0-440.0) is markedly elevated

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The repeat CT abdomen with contrast is mechanically capable of re-evaluating the pancreas and could detect pancreatitis or a pancreatic mass missed on the prior scan, and the reasoning does briefly mention the unexplained lipase/amylase elevation as part of the rationale, but the primary intent is clearly directed at characterizing colonic pathology (diverticulitis, ischemic colitis), meaning the pancreatic enzyme discordance and marked thrombocytosis are addressed only incidentally rather than as a driving clinical question.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: A repeat CT with contrast is partially justified given clinical progression (worsening bloody stools, recurrent hemodynamic symptoms, falling Hct) and an inconclusive prior CT report, but the incremental yield over the already-performed CT enterography is uncertain without oral contrast or a clear interval change, and colonoscopy or CT colonography might more directly characterize the mucosal pathology driving bloody diarrhea.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast only.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT-acquired axial images from the lung bases to the pubic
symphysis were obtained after the administration of 130 cc of Optiray
intravenous contrast using a CT enterography protocol.  Coronal and sagittal
reformations were obtained.  No oral contrast was administered.

FINDINGS:


CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:
There are dependent atelectatic changes at both lung bases.  No pleural
effusions, nodules, or opacities are seen at the lung bases.  The liver,
gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, small bowel, large bowel,
and both ureters are normal.  Within the left adrenal gland, there is a round
homogeneous lesion that measures fat attenuation and has characteristics
consistent with a benign adenoma.  Within both kidneys, there are multiple
rounded hypoattenuating lesions, the most prominent of which is seen in the
left interpole and measures 1.3 x 1.4 cm.  These lesions measure slightly
higher attenuation than simple fluid, and have characteristics consistent with
complex cysts with a Bosniak classification of IIF.  Other such lesions are
seen in the left kidney lower pole and the interpole of the right kidney.  No
mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is seen.  No free air or free
fluid is identified.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:
The rectum, sigmoid colon, bladder, both adnexa are unremarkable.  The sigmoid
colon demonstrates diverticula consistent with diverticulosis with no evidence
of ____.  Within the uterus, there is a round 1.9 x 3.1 cm, well-
circumscribed lesion that likely represents a fibroid.  No pelvic or inguinal
lymphadenopathy is seen.  No pelvic free fluid is identified.

BONE WINDOWS:
There are multilevel degenerative changes, most prominent at
the level of L4-L5 with grade 1 anterolisthesis of the L4 on L5 vertebral body
and loss of disc height with vacuum phenomenon seen at this level.  There is
also loss of disc height and vacuum phenomenon and a slight anterolisthesis of
the L5 on S1 vertebral body.  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are
seen.  There is fatty infiltration at the falciform ligament.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT axial imaging was performed through the abdomen and pelvis
after administration of 100 mL of IV Optiray.  Multiplanar reformatted images
were then obtained.

CT ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:
4 mm nodular opacity in the posterior right
lower lobe (2:2) may be related to dependent atelectasis.  No pleural or
pericardial effusion is seen.  Note is made of a small hiatal hernia.

2 mm hypodensity in segment III of the liver is too small to accurately
characterize.  Again note is made of hypoattenuation along the falciform
ligament, consistent with focal fatty infiltration.  The gallbladder, spleen,
pancreas, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable.  A 13 mm cyst is again noted
in the interpolar region of the left kidney. Multiple regions of
hypoattenuation and cortical thinning overlying calices are consistent with
multiple foci of scarring, for example in the posterolateral aspect of the
right kidney, and along the inferior aspect of the left kidney.  Clawed
appearance to some of the renal calices is also suggestive of papillary
necrosis, for which clinical correlation is recommended.  Additionally, an
ill-defined region of hypoattenuation in the left upper pole, without
overlying cortical thinning, is less well seen than on the prior study. 

The stomach and small bowel appear normal.  The ascending and transverse colon
also appear normal.  The abdominal aorta maintains normal caliber throughout.

CT PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:
The urinary bladder is minimally distended.
Rounded region of increased attenuation measuring approximately 3.0 x 2.3 cm
on axial imaging is again noted within the uterus.  While no pelvic free fluid
is seen, note is made of subtle stranding of the mesenteric fat surrounding
the sigmoid colon and also extending into the left paracolic gutter adjacent
to the descending colon.  Note is made of multiple diverticula along the
descending and sigmoid colon.  While not distended, there is suggestion of
loss of normal mucosal fold pattern in the rectum.  Additionally, multiple
small lymph nodes measuring up to 8 mm in short axis are noted, particularly
concentrated along the sigmoid colon (2:58), in the left lower quadrant, and
in the paraaortic retroperitoneum.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
Degenerative changes are noted at multiple levels of the
lower thoracic spine and lower lumbar spine.  Note is made of exaggerated
lordosis of the lumbar spine.  No region of bony destruction is seen
concerning for malignancy.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Gynecologic pathology (ovarian torsion, tubo-ovarian abscess, ovarian cyst complication, or uterine fibroid degeneration); ischemic colitis; inflammatory bowel disease
why_trace.information_gap: The second CT identified subtle sigmoid/descending colon mesenteric fat stranding, loss of rectal mucosal fold pattern, reactive lymphadenopathy, and a uterine mass — but did not characterize the adnexa or uterine lesion in detail, leaving a gynecologic source of LLQ pain unresolved.
why_trace.expected_finding: A complex ovarian cyst, tubo-ovarian abscess, or evidence of fibroid degeneration/torsion that could explain the persistent LLQ pain, hematochezia, and constitutional symptoms in this female patient.
grounding: Prior imaging 2 CT pelvis: 'subtle stranding of the mesenteric fat surrounding the sigmoid colon and also extending into the left paracolic gutter adjacent to the descending colon'
Prior imaging 2 CT pelvis: 'Rounded region of increased attenuation measuring approximately 3.0 x 2.3 cm on axial imaging is again noted within the uterus'
Prior imaging 2 CT pelvis: 'multiple small lymph nodes measuring up to 8 mm in short axis are noted, particularly concentrated along the sigmoid colon, in the left lower quadrant'
Prior imaging 2 CT pelvis: 'suggestion of loss of normal mucosal fold pattern in the rectum'
History: 'pain is primarily in the left lower quadrant but also radiates to the left upper quadrant'
History: 'diarrhea, which she describes as mucus combined with bright red blood'
History: 'subjective fevers at home and stated that she soaked through the sheets last night'
Labs: 'Platelet Count: 702.0 K/uL' — markedly elevated, consistent with inflammatory or reactive process
Labs: 'Albumin: 3.2 g/dL' — mildly low, suggesting chronic inflammation or nutritional depletion

discordance.evidence: Lipase: 221.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0); Amylase: 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0); 'The liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, small bowel, large bowel, and both ureters are normal' (Prior imaging 1); 'The gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable' (Prior imaging 2); Platelet Count: 702.0 K/uL (ref 150.0-440.0)

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The transvaginal ultrasound is directed at characterizing pelvic/gynecologic pathology and does not address the primary flagged discordance — markedly elevated pancreatic enzymes (lipase 3.7× ULN, elevated amylase) in the setting of two CT scans reporting a normal pancreas — which requires pancreas-focused investigation (e.g., MRCP, dedicated pancreatic protocol CT, or EUS) rather than pelvic imaging.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The second CT showed non-specific sigmoid stranding and lymphadenopathy but also noted a uterine mass and did not fully characterize the adnexa; transvaginal ultrasound is the appropriate next step to evaluate for a gynecologic etiology of LLQ pain that CT may have undercharacterized.

She then developed diarrhea, which  she describes as mucus combined with bright red blood.  Patient  reports that in past few days she has been having ___ BM per  day however these are low volume and essentially all "slime and  blood."  Patient then started to have the lightheadedness and  dizziness again.  Patient has tried ranitidine and Maalox  without any relief.  Patient states that only thing that  relieves the pain is defecation. The patient states that the  abdominal pain does not seem to be associated with food.  She  gets it throughout the day and then has a constant dull ache in  the area.  She states that she is now frustrated and concerned  as she feels like something very serious is going on. Patient  has had some nausea but no vomiting.  She has stated that she  maintains adequate PO intake. Patient reports subjective fevers  at home and stated that she soaked through the sheets last  night.  Patient has not measured her temperature at home.   Patient has a history of hematochezia in the past and has had a  colonoscopy in ___, which showed diverticulosis.   . Patient denies any associated chest pain or shortness of breath.   She has never had any abdominal surgeries.  She does have a  history of hemorrhoids but states that they are not active  currently.  No recent travel out of the country. No sick  contacts with similar symptoms.   . In the ED temp 98.9, 131/66, 86, 18, 98% on RA, pain was ___  on presentation, initial exam notable for tenderness over LLQ  and + guiac on rectal exam.  Patient underwent CT ab/pelvis.  Patient got 4 mg IV morphine. GI was contacted was not  concerned, to see tomorrow, reccomending IV fluids, pain  control. . visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast only.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT-acquired axial images from the lung bases to the pubic
symphysis were obtained after the administration of 130 cc of Optiray
intravenous contrast using a CT enterography protocol.  Coronal and sagittal
reformations were obtained.  No oral contrast was administered.

FINDINGS:


CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:
There are dependent atelectatic changes at both lung bases.  No pleural
effusions, nodules, or opacities are seen at the lung bases.  The liver,
gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, small bowel, large bowel,
and both ureters are normal.  Within the left adrenal gland, there is a round
homogeneous lesion that measures fat attenuation and has characteristics
consistent with a benign adenoma.  Within both kidneys, there are multiple
rounded hypoattenuating lesions, the most prominent of which is seen in the
left interpole and measures 1.3 x 1.4 cm.  These lesions measure slightly
higher attenuation than simple fluid, and have characteristics consistent with
complex cysts with a Bosniak classification of IIF.  Other such lesions are
seen in the left kidney lower pole and the interpole of the right kidney.  No
mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is seen.  No free air or free
fluid is identified.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST:
The rectum, sigmoid colon, bladder, both adnexa are unremarkable.  The sigmoid
colon demonstrates diverticula consistent with diverticulosis with no evidence
of ____.  Within the uterus, there is a round 1.9 x 3.1 cm, well-
circumscribed lesion that likely represents a fibroid.  No pelvic or inguinal
lymphadenopathy is seen.  No pelvic free fluid is identified.

BONE WINDOWS:
There are multilevel degenerative changes, most prominent at
the level of L4-L5 with grade 1 anterolisthesis of the L4 on L5 vertebral body
and loss of disc height with vacuum phenomenon seen at this level.  There is
also loss of disc height and vacuum phenomenon and a slight anterolisthesis of
the L5 on S1 vertebral body.  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are
seen.  There is fatty infiltration at the falciform ligament.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT axial imaging was performed through the abdomen and pelvis
after administration of 100 mL of IV Optiray.  Multiplanar reformatted images
were then obtained.

CT ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:
4 mm nodular opacity in the posterior right
lower lobe (2:2) may be related to dependent atelectasis.  No pleural or
pericardial effusion is seen.  Note is made of a small hiatal hernia.

2 mm hypodensity in segment III of the liver is too small to accurately
characterize.  Again note is made of hypoattenuation along the falciform
ligament, consistent with focal fatty infiltration.  The gallbladder, spleen,
pancreas, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable.  A 13 mm cyst is again noted
in the interpolar region of the left kidney. Multiple regions of
hypoattenuation and cortical thinning overlying calices are consistent with
multiple foci of scarring, for example in the posterolateral aspect of the
right kidney, and along the inferior aspect of the left kidney.  Clawed
appearance to some of the renal calices is also suggestive of papillary
necrosis, for which clinical correlation is recommended.  Additionally, an
ill-defined region of hypoattenuation in the left upper pole, without
overlying cortical thinning, is less well seen than on the prior study. 

The stomach and small bowel appear normal.  The ascending and transverse colon
also appear normal.  The abdominal aorta maintains normal caliber throughout.

CT PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:
The urinary bladder is minimally distended.
Rounded region of increased attenuation measuring approximately 3.0 x 2.3 cm
on axial imaging is again noted within the uterus.  While no pelvic free fluid
is seen, note is made of subtle stranding of the mesenteric fat surrounding
the sigmoid colon and also extending into the left paracolic gutter adjacent
to the descending colon.  Note is made of multiple diverticula along the
descending and sigmoid colon.  While not distended, there is suggestion of
loss of normal mucosal fold pattern in the rectum.  Additionally, multiple
small lymph nodes measuring up to 8 mm in short axis are noted, particularly
concentrated along the sigmoid colon (2:58), in the left lower quadrant, and
in the paraaortic retroperitoneum.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
Degenerative changes are noted at multiple levels of the
lower thoracic spine and lower lumbar spine.  Note is made of exaggerated
lordosis of the lumbar spine.  No region of bony destruction is seen
concerning for malignancy.

PELVIC ULTRASOUND, ___:


FINDINGS:
Initial transabdominal scan was performed.  Due to limited use of
the uterus and the adnexal structures, a transvaginal study was then
performed.

The uterus measures approximately 5.7 x 3.1 x 3.8 cm.  Within the left side of
the uterus, in the region of the fundus, there is a heterogeneous mass
measuring approximately 2.4 x 2.2 x 2.1 cm, likely representing a fibroid.  No
other lesions are identified.  Myometrial echotexture is otherwise
unremarkable.  The endometrial stripe does not appear expanded.  It measures
approximately 1-2 mm in thickness.

The left ovary measures 1.6 x 1.0 x 1.2 cm and is unremarkable.  The right
ovary is not well visualized; however, there is no evidence of a right adnexal
mass.  There is no free fluid.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Ischemic colitis or inflammatory bowel disease (primary concern given bloody mucoid diarrhea, LLQ/LUQ pain, mesenteric fat stranding on CT); urologic pathology (renal cysts with Bosniak IIF classification, papillary necrosis pattern, hematuria history, cortical thinning/scarring on second CT) as secondary concern requiring surveillance/characterization
why_trace.information_gap: The second CT abdomen noted Bosniak IIF complex renal cysts, cortical thinning, calyceal changes suggestive of papillary necrosis, and an ill-defined left upper pole hypoattenuation that was 'less well seen than on the prior study' — the physician needs real-time sonographic characterization of these renal lesions to assess for interval change, obstruction, or a complication (e.g., infected/hemorrhagic cyst) that could be contributing to the clinical picture.
why_trace.expected_finding: Characterization of the known bilateral complex renal cysts (Bosniak IIF), assessment for hydronephrosis or perinephric fluid/abscess, and evaluation of the ill-defined left upper pole lesion that was less conspicuous on the most recent CT compared to the prior study.
grounding: Prior imaging 2 (CT Abdomen): 'Clawed appearance to some of the renal calices is also suggestive of papillary necrosis, for which clinical correlation is recommended'
Prior imaging 2 (CT Abdomen): 'an ill-defined region of hypoattenuation in the left upper pole, without overlying cortical thinning, is less well seen than on the prior study'
Prior imaging 2 (CT Abdomen): 'Multiple regions of hypoattenuation and cortical thinning overlying calices are consistent with multiple foci of scarring'
Prior imaging 1 (CT Abdomen): 'Within both kidneys, there are multiple rounded hypoattenuating lesions... characteristics consistent with complex cysts with a Bosniak classification of IIF'
History: 'history of... kidney cysts kidney stones hematuria'
Laboratory tests: 'Estimated GFR = 50 if non African-American... GFR<60 = Chronic Kidney Disease'
History: 'she was having episodes of feeling lightheaded and dizzy... subsequently developed sharp abdominal pain... radiates to the left upper quadrant'

study_inadequacy.evidence: The right ovary is not well visualized; however, there is no evidence of a right adnexal mass.

discordance.evidence: Lipase: 221.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0); Amylase: 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0); 'The liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, small bowel, large bowel, and both ureters are normal' (Prior imaging 1); 'The gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands appear unremarkable' (Prior imaging 2)

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: A renal ultrasound is directed entirely at characterizing known renal cysts and papillary necrosis changes, and neither its imaging capability nor the physician's stated reasoning addresses the unvisualized right ovary (study inadequacy) or the discordance between markedly elevated lipase/amylase and normal pancreatic imaging on prior CT.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The renal ultrasound addresses a real documented gap — multiple Bosniak IIF cysts, papillary necrosis pattern, cortical scarring, and a regressing left upper pole lesion noted on the second CT — but the primary acute presentation (bloody mucoid diarrhea, mesenteric fat stranding, loss of rectal mucosal fold pattern) is colonic in origin and the renal findings are unlikely to explain the acute symptoms, making this order a reasonable but lower-priority adjunct rather than the most pressing next step.

baseline.patient_history: Mrs. ___ is an ___ year-old female with a history of diverticulosis presenting with increasing abdominal pain over since last ___.  She reports that 2 weeks ago she had symptoms of a UTI and was evaluated by her PCP who treated her with Bactrim.  She eventually had E.Coli grow from her urine culture, however, developed a rash with the Bactrim and was subsequently changed to a ciprofloxacin.  Her urinary symptoms improved, but she persisted to feel unwell and started to  develop abdominal pain.  She reports that the pain is in the central lower portion of her abdomen, crampy in nature, without radiation, but associated with increased pain during bowel movements but no with PO intake.  She has not had a bowel movement for 3 days, however, her last bowel movement was uncomfortable.  She reports no fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, changes in her urine (no particulate matter), chest pain, shortness-of-breath, or vaginal drainage.  Currently she reports that her pain has improved slightly since arriving in the emergency department.  She continues to feel tired but has not had nausea or vomiting.  She denies any  fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness-of-breath.  She had a colonoscopy in ___ which showed sigmoid and ascending diverticulosis. visible_prior_imaging.report:
FINDINGS:
Frontal and lateral views of the chest showed no acute
cardiopulmonary process.  The pleural and pulmonary structures are
unremarkable.  Prominence of the right pulmonary artery is unchanged from
___.  The aorta is tortuous and the cardiac silhouette is normal.  There is
no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.  No focal consolidation is evident.  A VP
shunt catheter is seen coursing along the right hemithorax.  Interval rib
fracture seen on left ___ and 7th ribs with healing callus.  Degenerative
changes of the glenohumeral joint and right acromioclavicular joint are again
noted.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: colovesical or colovaginal fistula (given diverticulosis history, prior UTI with E. coli, TAH-BSO), bowel obstruction, colonic abscess, malignancy recurrence/progression (lung adenocarcinoma history), urologic source (elevated creatinine, prior UTI)
why_trace.information_gap: The physician cannot determine from history, exam, and labs alone whether the patient has acute diverticulitis with or without complications (abscess, perforation, fistula), nor can they exclude other intra-abdominal pathology causing suprapubic pain, constipation, and mildly elevated lactate in a patient with known diverticulosis and recent E. coli UTI.
why_trace.expected_finding: Pericolonic fat stranding with wall thickening of the sigmoid colon consistent with acute diverticulitis, possibly with a pericolic abscess or evidence of a colovesical fistula (air in bladder); alternatively, a colonic mass or obstruction.
grounding: History: 'history of diverticulosis presenting with increasing abdominal pain'
History: 'colonoscopy in ___ which showed sigmoid and ascending diverticulosis'
History: 'E.Coli grow from her urine culture' and 'urinary symptoms improved, but persisted to feel unwell and started to develop abdominal pain'
History: 'pain is in the central lower portion of her abdomen, crampy in nature... associated with increased pain during bowel movements'
History: 'has not had a bowel movement for 3 days'
Physical examination: 'soft, distended, TTP suprapubic... no rebound or guarding'
Laboratory: 'Lactate: 2.1 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0)' — mildly elevated
Laboratory: 'Creatinine: 1.5 mg/dL' with eGFR ~33 — renal impairment relevant to contrast decision but not prohibitive
Laboratory: 'Neutrophils: 75.9 %' — relative neutrophilia despite normal WBC
History: 'Past Medical History: Adenocarcinoma of the lung' — malignancy history raises concern for metastatic or new primary process
History: 'Past Surgical History: TAH-BSO' — gynecologic source excluded but fistula risk relevant

discordance.evidence: Lactate: 2.1 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0) [above normal]; vitals: '97.4 66 ___ 100% room air'; ABD: 'soft, distended, TTP suprapubic... no rebound or guarding'; WBC: 9.2 K/uL (normal range)

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast can directly resolve the discordance by identifying or excluding a complication (abscess, perforation, fistula, obstruction, or malignancy) that would explain the mildly elevated lactate despite a benign exam and normal WBC, and the physician's reasoning explicitly acknowledges the elevated lactate as a driver of concern for occult serious pathology.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the standard cross-sectional imaging to confirm diverticulitis, characterize complications (abscess, perforation, fistula), and evaluate for alternative diagnoses in a patient with known diverticulosis, suprapubic pain, constipation, mildly elevated lactate, and a recent E. coli UTI that may represent a fistulous connection.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
FINDINGS:
Frontal and lateral views of the chest showed no acute
cardiopulmonary process.  The pleural and pulmonary structures are
unremarkable.  Prominence of the right pulmonary artery is unchanged from
___.  The aorta is tortuous and the cardiac silhouette is normal.  There is
no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.  No focal consolidation is evident.  A VP
shunt catheter is seen coursing along the right hemithorax.  Interval rib
fracture seen on left ___ and 7th ribs with healing callus.  Degenerative
changes of the glenohumeral joint and right acromioclavicular joint are again
noted.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT-acquired axial images were obtained from the lung bases to
the pubic symphysis following the uneventful administration of 100 cc
Visipaque intravenous contrast.  Coronally and sagittally reformatted images
were generated and reviewed.

FINDINGS:


CT ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST:
Although this study is not tailored for the
evaluation of supradiaphragmatic contents, the visualized lung bases show
posterior dependent positional changes but no pleural effusion, focal
consolidation, or pulmonary nodules.  Limited evaluation of the heart shows no
pericardial effusion.  The descending thoracic aorta shows extensive atheroma
and calcified plaque narrowing the vessel lumen (2:5).

The liver enhances homogeneously without perfusion defects or focal liver
lesions.  The portal venous system opacifies satisfactorily with contrast.  No
intra- or extra-hepatic biliary dilation is seen.  The patient is status post
cholecystectomy.  A right-sided ventricular shunt catheter is unchanged in
position with the tip terminating anterior to the liver.  The pancreas is
fatty infiltrated but otherwise unremarkable.  The spleen and bilateral
adrenal glands are unremarkable.  Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and
excrete contrast normally without evidence of hydronephrosis or solid renal
mass.  The left kidney contains numerous subcentimeter hypodensities in the
cortex which are too small to fully characterize by CT.

The stomach and intra-abdominal loops of small and large bowel are
unremarkable without evidence of wall thickening or obstruction.  No free air
or ascites is present.  There is extensive atherosclerotic disease of the
descending aorta with ectasia of the distal infrarenal portion measuring 2.5 x
2.3 cm (2:41), which is not significantly changed from ___.  An
uncomplicated 6.5 cm fat-containing ventral hernia is noted through which the
VP shunt courses.

CT PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:
The patient is status post hysterectomy.  The
urinary bladder and rectum are unremarkable.  A left likely ovarian cyst is
increased in size from both prior studies, now measuring 6.2 x 3.6 cm (2:63),
which previously measured 3.0 x 2.5 cm in ___ and 1.5 x 1.1 cm in ___.  The
sigmoid colon contains multiple diverticula and a 5.5 x 4.2 x 3.1 cm rim
enhancing thick-walled collection of air and fecalized material with no
definite tract to the adjacent sigmoid colon.  This rim-enhancing collection
is closely approximated to the left posterior dome of the urinary bladder and
the sigmoid colon without evidence of air in urinary bladder to suggest
fistula.  There is pericolonic mesenteric fat stranding in this region.  There
is no free pelvic fluid or inguinal/pelvic lymphadenopathy.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
There is grade 1 anterolisthesis of L5 on S1 and
multilevel severe degenerative changes in the lumbar spine with loss of
intervertebral disc height, endplate sclerosis and osteophytosis.  No
suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified in the bone.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Ovarian/adnexal pathology (rapidly enlarging left pelvic cyst — possible ovarian torsion, hemorrhagic cyst, or malignancy); colovesical or colovaginal fistula formation from diverticular abscess
why_trace.information_gap: The CT identified two concurrent pelvic abnormalities — a 5.5 cm rim-enhancing pericolic abscess abutting the bladder/sigmoid and a rapidly enlarging left pelvic cyst (now 6.2 x 3.6 cm, up from 1.5 x 1.1 cm in prior studies) — and it is unclear whether the cyst is a simple ovarian/paraovarian cyst, a hemorrhagic cyst, a tubo-ovarian abscess, or a malignant process, and whether it is related to or separate from the diverticular collection.
why_trace.expected_finding: Characterization of the left pelvic cyst's internal architecture (septations, solid components, internal echoes, vascularity on Doppler) to distinguish a simple/hemorrhagic cyst from a complex adnexal mass or tubo-ovarian abscess, and to assess for any connection with the adjacent diverticular collection.
grounding: CT Pelvis: 'A left likely ovarian cyst is increased in size from both prior studies, now measuring 6.2 x 3.6 cm (2:63), which previously measured 3.0 x 2.5 cm in ___ and 1.5 x 1.1 cm in ___'
CT Pelvis: 'A 5.5 x 4.2 x 3.1 cm rim enhancing thick-walled collection of air and fecalized material with no definite tract to the adjacent sigmoid colon'

CT Pelvis: 'This rim-enhancing collection is closely approximated to the left posterior dome of the urinary bladder and the sigmoid colon without evidence of air in urinary bladder to suggest fistula'
History: 'TAH-BSO' (prior bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy — making a 'left ovarian cyst' anatomically unexpected and raising concern for malignancy or other pathology)
History: 'Adenocarcinoma of the lung' (oncologic history raises concern for metastatic or new primary pelvic malignancy)
Physical exam: 'TTP suprapubic... no rebound or guarding'
History: 'pain is in the central lower portion of her abdomen, crampy in nature'

discordance.evidence: Lactate: 2.1 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0) ... a 5.5 x 4.2 x 3.1 cm rim enhancing thick-walled collection of air and fecalized material ... closely approximated to the left posterior dome of the urinary bladder and the sigmoid colon ... pericolonic mesenteric fat stranding in this region ... ABD: Obese, soft, distended, TTP suprapubic ... no rebound or guarding ... her pain has improved slightly since arriving in the emergency department

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The transvaginal ultrasound can characterize the pelvic cyst but cannot resolve the primary flagged discordance — the mismatch between a large rim-enhancing fecalized abscess on CT and the reassuringly benign clinical exam with mildly elevated lactate — because ultrasound is not directed at adjudicating that imaging-versus-clinical severity conflict.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT explicitly identified a rapidly enlarging left pelvic cyst that CT cannot fully characterize, and transvaginal ultrasound is the standard next-step modality for adnexal lesion characterization in a patient with known pelvic pathology and lower abdominal pain.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
Abdomen and pelvic CT.

TECHNIQUE:
Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 0.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 2.4 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.2
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 2.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 9.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 4.8
mGy-cm.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Interval change in pericolonic abscess (enlargement, rupture, or development of fistula); possible bowel obstruction from inflammatory mass; less likely gynecologic complication from fibroid

why_trace.expected_finding: Interval change in the pericolonic abscess — either enlargement suggesting treatment failure, new free air or free fluid suggesting perforation/rupture, or development of a fistulous tract; alternatively, interval improvement confirming antibiotic response

There is a hypodense fluid collection with questionable rim enhancement which appears separate from the sigmoid colon but tracks along its posterior aspect and measures 4.0 x 2.9 x 2.6 cm, most consistent with an organizing pericolonic abscess'

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The prior CT demonstrated an organizing pericolonic abscess measuring 4.0 x 2.9 x 2.6 cm with sigmoid diverticulitis, the patient completed antibiotics with initial improvement but now has recurrent pain, making interval CT essential to assess whether the abscess has changed and guide the decision between continued antibiotics, percutaneous drainage, or surgical intervention.

Small right pleural effusion with adjacent atelectasis.  Severe

ABDOMEN:
A 2.2 cm hypoattenuating lesion in the left hepatic lobe is unchanged
since at least ___.  Multiple hepatic and perihepatic calcifications
adjacent left hepatic lobe are unchanged.  No new or enlarging hepatic
lesions.  Hepatic veins and portal veins are patent.  Too small to
characterize renal lesions are characterized on prior MRI as cysts and
hemorrhagic cysts.  The pancreas is atrophic.  The adrenal glands and spleen

thickening, fat stranding, and a small amount of adjacent fluid involving the

and slightly higher than water in attenuation (20 Hounsfield units).  No
pneumoperitoneum.  The visualized portions of the mesenteric vasculature are
patent.

The uterus is unremarkable on CT for age.

LYMPH NODES:
No enlarged abdominal or pelvic lymph nodes are visualized.

No aggressive osseous lesions are
demonstrated.  Postsurgical changes in the anterior abdominal wall are noted.

hyperdense structures inferior to the left capsule that resemble anchors for
hernia mesh.  These are all seen dating back to ___ and are stable.  A lipoma
in the proximal anterior thigh subjacent to the rectus femoris is noted.

___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at approximately 16:45,
approximately 30 minutes after discovery of the findings.

why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary ductal dilation, gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid (cholecystitis), or hepatic vascular abnormality (e.g., hepatic vein congestion consistent with right heart failure/elevated BNP of 13271); possibly further characterization of the known hepatic lesion or ascites

why_trace.expected_finding: Better soft-tissue characterization of the known left hepatic lobe lesion (to confirm it remains a benign hemangioma or hemorrhagic cyst versus a new/evolving lesion in the context of her hematologic disorder), and improved delineation of the loculated peritoneal fluid to assess for abscess, hemorrhage, or peritoneal disease — all without contrast given her CKD stage III (creatinine 1.8, eGFR 28–34).

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Multiplasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis including noncontrast,
arterial and venous plase imaging. Multiplanar reformats provided.

FINDINGS:


LUNG BASES:
There has been interval enlargement of large paraesophageal
hiatal hernia, with retained air and fluid contents.  Moderate emphysema is
seen at the lung bases.  There are peripheral fibrotic changes, suggestive of
interstitial lung disease.  Some bibasilar atelectasis is also present.  There
are no pleural or pericardial effusions.  Calcifications are present in the
aortic valve and left anterior descending coronary artery.

ABDOMEN:
A collection of free air is noted anterior to the liver, and
outlining bowel loops in the right upper quadrant.  At the root of the
mesentery, a few locules of gas are also present, along with a small amount of
simple fluid collection and minimal soft tissue stranding.  Trace free fluid
is also noted tracking into the perisplenic region, bilateral paracolic
gutters, and pelvis.

The stomach and small bowel are unremarkable.  Colonic diverticulosis is noted
without definite evidence of diverticilis. There is no bowel dilation to
suggest obstruction.  No abnormal wall thickening, enhancement, pneumatosis,
or mesenteric or portal venous gas to suggest bowel ischemia.

The liver enhances homogeneously, without focal masses.  Stable intrahepatic
biliary dilatation is noted, secondary to prior cholecystectomy.  There is
moderate atrophy of the pancreas, with scattered cystic-appearing lesions that
are consistent with known IPMN.  The spleen is unremarkable.  

The adrenals are normal.  The kidneys are slightly atrophic with cortical
thinning and lobulation.  However, contrast enhancement and excretion are

visible_prior_imaging.report:
STUDY:
Abdominal ultrasound.

FINDINGS:
The liver demonstrates a normal echotexture without focal lesion or
intrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The portal vein is patent with hepatopetal
flow.

The gallbladder is distended, but shows no stones, wall edema or
pericholecystic fluid.  The common bile duct is 2 mm in caliber.

Pancreatic head and body appear normal and the tail is mildly obscured by
overlying bowel gas.

The IVC appears unremarkable.

The spleen measures 7.4 cm in its long axis.  It is normal appearing.

why_trace.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema, peripancreatic stranding, necrosis, ductal dilation, or a pancreatic mass that would confirm and characterize acute pancreatitis or an underlying pancreatic lesion driving the enzyme elevation.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase 146 (>2× ULN), amylase 160 (elevated), epigastric pain radiating to chest, nausea, weight loss, and an incomplete pancreatic evaluation on ultrasound (tail obscured by bowel gas), a dedicated pancreatic-protocol CT is the appropriate next step to characterize the pancreas fully and assess severity/etiology.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN) PORT:

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits.The contour of the
liver is smooth. There is no focal liver mass.  A tiny 5 mm echogenic focus in
the right lobe of the liver likely represents a calcified granuloma. Main
portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow. There is trace ascites along the
inferior edge of the right liver lobe.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CBD measures 7 mm,
which is mildly dilated, although no shadowing stone is identified.

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is mildly distended, containing biliary sludge
and shadowing stones.  There is trace pericholecystic fluid but no gallbladder
wall thickening or edema.

PANCREAS:
Imaged portion of the pancreas demonstrates a small amount of
peripancreatic fluid surrounding the head of the pancreas.  No masses or
pancreatic ductal dilation are seen and portions of the pancreatic tail are
obscured by overlying bowel gas.

Limited imaging of the right kidney shows no hydronephrosis.

A trace right pleural effusion is incidentally noted.

NOTIFICATION:
The findings were discussed by Dr. ___ with Dr. ___
telephone on ___ at 4:12 AM, 10 minutes after discovery of the findings.

FINDINGS:
The liver demonstrates slightly increased echogenicity, which could
represent hepatic steatosis, otherwise, is unremarkable without focal hepatic
lesions or masses.  There is no intra- or extra-hepatic biliary ductal
dilation with the CBD measuring up to 5 mm.

The portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow.  There is no free fluid.

The gallbladder is non-relaxed, although not markedly distended, containing
considerable sludge.  The wall is slightly thickened, without intramural edema
or pericholecystic fluid.  The gallbladder is tender to palpation with the
probe.

The pancreatic duct is mildly prominent measuring up to 3 mm.  No focal
pancreatic abnormalities are otherwise identified.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction / choledocholithiasis; transplant-related complication (e.g., transplant renal artery/vein pathology is less likely given pain location, but biliary etiology driving pancreatitis is a key concern); SLE-related serositis/vasculitis
why_trace.information_gap: The etiology of the markedly elevated lipase (1219 IU/L) is established as acute pancreatitis, but the CAUSE of the pancreatitis is unknown — specifically, whether there is biliary pathology (gallstones, biliary sludge, common bile duct dilation, or cholecystitis) driving it, which would change management
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary sludge, gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid (cholecystitis), or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as the precipitant of pancreatitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder would push toward other etiologies (medication/tacrolimus-related, hypertriglyceridemia — though triglycerides are 45, or SLE-related)
