History: 'no RUQ pain prior to this morning'
History: 'Her last meal was yesterday at 8PM with no unusual foods. Denied alcohol intake'
Physical exam: '+ tenderness to percussion and palpation with intermittent rebound tenderness and guarding diffusely throughout abdomen'

discordance.evidence: Serum potassium lab value: 'Potassium: 7.8 mEq/L' versus 'Potassium, Whole Blood: 5.5 mEq/L'; physical exam: 'tachycardic, regular rhythm'; history: 'She has not taken any medications today' including immunosuppression; tacroFK: 2.9 ng/mL (ref 5.0-20.0) — markedly subtherapeutic in a transplant patient on dual immunosuppression; additionally, diffuse rebound tenderness and guarding ('intermittent rebound tenderness and guarding diffusely throughout abdomen') is out of proportion to the expected focal epigastric findings of pancreatitis alone

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered liver/gallbladder ultrasound and its reasoning are directed entirely at identifying a biliary cause of pancreatitis, and neither the test nor the stated reasoning addresses the three most pressing flagged discordances: the critically subtherapeutic tacrolimus level (2.9 ng/mL) in a non-adherent transplant patient, the markedly discordant potassium values (7.8 vs 5.5 mEq/L) requiring urgent cardiac-risk clarification, or the diffuse peritoneal signs disproportionate to uncomplicated pancreatitis that raise concern for a more serious intra-abdominal process.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >20× the upper limit of normal confirming acute pancreatitis, a right-upper-quadrant/abdominal ultrasound is the standard first-line imaging to identify a biliary etiology (gallstones, CBD dilation), which is the most common and immediately actionable cause, and is appropriate before considering CT or MRCP.

baseline.patient_history: ___ is a ___ with ESRD from lupus nephritis s/p  renal transplant on dual immunosuppression and hypertension who  presents with acute onset abdominal pain that started this  morning. She reports that she was in her usual state of health  until this morning when she was getting a ride to work. Her  stomach started to hurt where her seatbelt was going across it,  mid stomach. She got out of the car at work and tried to sit  down to relieve the pain, but it began to radiate to her back.  At first she tried to work but the pain was so bad that she had  to leave, and eventually presented to the ED for evaluation. Her  last meal was yesterday at 8PM with no unusual foods. Denied  alcohol intake, no RUQ pain prior to this morning.   In the ED, initial VS were pain ___ no heart rate recorded  155/105 18 100%. Received 5 mg reglan for nausea and 3 doses of  dilaudid (record says 1 mg, patient states .5 mg) and 2L IVF.  She was found to have a lipase of 1219m elevated AST of 56,  normal ALT as well as ALP and Tbili. WBC 5.9, H/H of 10.2/33.6,  Cr 1.5, K 5.5, BUN 36, lactate 1.5. She was evaluated by renal  transplant who agreed with admission for management including  trending of potassium and checking tacrolimus level, and  continuation of immunosuppression.  Upon transfer to the floor, her vital signs were 98.9 151/93 119  22 99% RA. She is having ___ abdominal pain, with nausea and  one episode of green watery emesis during the interview. She has  not taken any medications today. Denies other systemic symptoms  such as fever, chills, dysuria, change in urinary habits,  diarrhea. Last BM was yesterday which was normal.    Past Medical History: * ESRD - ___ SLE nephritis, on peritoneal dialysis every night  (followed by Dr. ___ * SLE - (Dx ___ with ESRD on PD for past ___ years, myositis,  pericarditis & pericardial effusion * HTN - ___ * Sjogren syndrome - ___ * BOOP - ___ * Inflammatory arthropathy * Depression * Orthostatic hypotension - ___   Social History: ___ Family History: Sister: SLE   Mother: ___ mellitus   Father: Healthy   ___ grandmother: asthma and HTN

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Grayscale and color Doppler ultrasound images were obtained of the
right upper quadrant.

FINDINGS:
The liver is normal in echotexture, with no focal lesions.  There is
no intra or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  The gallbladder is
unremarkable.  There is no cholelithiasis or sonographic signs of
cholecystitis.  The main portal vein is patent and demonstrates normal
hepatopetal flow.  The pancreatic head and body are unremarkable, however the
distal body and tail are not well visualized secondary to overlying bowel gas.
The common bile duct measures 3 mm.  There is no evidence of ascites.  The
spleen is homogeneous and normal in size, measuring 10 cm.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis or biliary obstruction as a precipitant of pancreatitis; pancreatic duct pathology (stricture, disruption); less likely SLE-related vasculitis affecting pancreatic vasculature
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound could not visualize the distal pancreatic body and tail, and did not evaluate the pancreatic duct; the etiology of the markedly elevated lipase (1219 IU/L) and the structural integrity/ductal anatomy of the pancreas remain undefined, and biliary causes cannot be fully excluded by ultrasound alone given limited pancreatic visualization.
why_trace.expected_finding: MRCP is expected to reveal either a dilated or obstructed pancreatic duct, choledocholithiasis/common bile duct stone not seen on ultrasound, pancreatic duct disruption, or peripancreatic fluid/necrosis — findings that would characterize the severity and etiology of pancreatitis and guide management.
grounding: Lipase: 1219.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Prior imaging: 'the distal body and tail are not well visualized secondary to overlying bowel gas'
Prior imaging: 'The gallbladder is unremarkable. There is no cholelithiasis or sonographic signs of cholecystitis.'
Prior imaging: 'The common bile duct measures 3 mm. There is no intra or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.'
Creatinine: 1.5 mg/dL (ref 0.4-1.1)
History: 'ESRD from lupus nephritis s/p renal transplant on dual immunosuppression'
Bilirubin, Total: 0.4 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)
Triglycerides: 45.0 mg/dL (ref 0.0-149.0)

study_inadequacy.evidence: the distal body and tail are not well visualized secondary to overlying bowel gas

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is capable of visualizing the distal pancreatic body and tail, the pancreatic duct, and the biliary tree without the bowel-gas limitation that rendered the ultrasound inadequate, and the physician's reasoning explicitly identifies the incomplete pancreatic visualization and ductal assessment as the primary gap driving the order.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With a lipase of 1219 IU/L confirming pancreatitis, a normal gallbladder on ultrasound but incomplete pancreatic visualization (distal body and tail obscured by bowel gas), MRCP is the appropriate next step to evaluate the pancreatic duct, rule out choledocholithiasis, and characterize the extent of pancreatic pathology without nephrotoxic contrast — critical in this renal transplant patient with creatinine 1.5 mg/dL.

baseline.patient_history: ___ with CAD s/p MI and CABG, A-fib, CKD, PVD, HTN and DM  recently admitted to ___ from ___ for hypotension, dehydration and acute/chronic renal failure returned to ___ ED early on ___ AM with new, sudden-onset, postprandial epigastric and left upper quadrant pain. His pain began shortly after  eating a light dinner on ___. The pain did not improve with rest. Given persistence of his epigastric pain, pt called ___ and was  brought into ___. Pt reportedly  hemodynamically stable upon arrival.  He was kept NPO and resuscitated w/ IVF. Pt endorsed nausea following arrival at hospital and vomited several time, the quality of which he describes as bilious.   Labs at OSH notable for WBC of 11, lipase of 1000, alk phos 199, ALT 79/AST 140, amylase of 20. BCx obtained at time of arrival positive in two bottles for GNRs. Pt underwent extensive workup to exclude hepatobiliary cause for presenting symptoms. All studies were negative. CTA did demonstrate, however, high grade stenosis of SMA and ___, and he was transferred to ___ for evaluation for possible stent repair.  Pt denies any prior, similar episodes. Denies fevers, chills, melena, BRBPR, hematochezia, unintentional weight loss. why_trace.other_hypothesis: Chronic mesenteric ischemia / acute-on-chronic mesenteric ischemia due to high-grade SMA and celiac artery stenosis; hepatobiliary pathology (elevated transaminases, alk phos); possible bowel wall ischemia or infarction
why_trace.information_gap: The OSH CTA identified high-grade stenosis of the SMA and celiac axis, but the anatomy, degree of collateralization, and presence of end-organ ischemic injury to the bowel wall or solid organs has not yet been characterized at this institution with cross-sectional imaging; additionally, the patient has CKD (eGFR ~42) making iodinated contrast risky, so non-contrast MRI is chosen to evaluate mesenteric and hepatobiliary anatomy without nephrotoxic contrast.
why_trace.expected_finding: Evidence of bowel wall ischemia (thickening, edema, or signal abnormality of small bowel), hepatic or splenic ischemic changes, or characterization of the mesenteric vascular anatomy and collateral flow — all without administering nephrotoxic iodinated contrast given the patient's CKD.
grounding: History: 'CTA did demonstrate, however, high grade stenosis of SMA and ___, and he was transferred to ___ for evaluation for possible stent repair'

Labs: 'ALT 107, AST 85, Alkaline Phosphatase 148' — persistent hepatobiliary enzyme elevation warranting abdominal imaging
History: 'Pt underwent extensive workup to exclude hepatobiliary cause for presenting symptoms. All studies were negative'
History: 'PVD s/p left TKA... underwent Silverhawk atherectomy and balloon angioplasty' — extensive systemic atherosclerosis supporting mesenteric ischemia as leading diagnosis

resolve.reason: no trigger detected — step-2 skipped

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Given the OSH finding of high-grade SMA/celiac stenosis as the leading explanation for postprandial epigastric pain in a patient with extensive atherosclerotic disease, and given CKD precluding safe repeat iodinated contrast administration, a non-contrast MRI abdomen is a reasonable next step to characterize mesenteric and solid-organ ischemic injury at the receiving institution.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Multisequence multiplanar MR images were acquired of the abdomen
according to the department's MRCP protocol.  Contrast was not administered
due to a low calculated GFR.

FINDINGS:
The liver is normal in signal characteristics, without mass or
intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  The gallbladder is mildly enlarged
and demonstrates layering sludge, but is decreased in size from comparison CT
of ___, without wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid.  The
biliary tree is normal appearing, without filling defects to suggest stone. 
The pancreas is normal in appearance, the pancreatic duct is normal in
caliber.    There is no pancreas divisum.  In the medial limb of the left
adrenal, a 1.4 cm adenoma is again visualized.  The kidneys are normal in
appearance without masses, hydronephrosis.  A hyperintense focus in the
inferior pole of the left kidney measures 1.5 cm and likely represents a
hemorrhagic cyst although this is incompletely characterized.  The visualized
abdominal aorta is normal in caliber.  There is no retroperitoneal lymph node
enlargement.  Loops of small and large bowel are grossly unremarkable.  There
is no intraperitoneal free fluid.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With troponin now negative (ruling out NSTEMI as the primary driver), a lipase >15× the upper limit of normal, WBC of 26.7 K/uL, history of recent endoscopic esophageal dilation, and post-procedural fevers, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to characterize the severity of pancreatitis and exclude a perforation or other surgical emergency.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits. The contour of the
liver is smooth. There is no focal liver mass. The main portal vein is patent
with hepatopetal flow. There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CHD measures 5 mm.

GALLBLADDER:
Cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening.

PANCREAS:
The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

SPLEEN:
Normal echogenicity, measuring 10.2 cm.

KIDNEYS:
Limited views of the right kidney show no hydronephrosis.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
FINDINGS:
The liver appears minimally coarsened, with unusual echogenic
appearance to the biliary tree, which on live scanning demonstrates suggestion
of pneumobilia.  The common duct measures 5 mm, more prominent as compared to
___.  The gallbladder is status post recent drainage on ___, now mildly distended with shadowing echogenic material and demonstrates
persistent wall thickening.  ___ sign is positive despite morphine therapy.
There is normal hepatopetal flow within the main portal vein.  The spleen
measures 11 cm.

TECHNIQUE:
MDCT images were acquired from the lung bases through the pubic
symphysis following administration of intravenous contrast, with multiplanar
reformations.

CT ABDOMEN:
With the exception of bilateral dependent atelectasis, the lung
bases are clear.  There is no pleural effusion.  The heart is normal in size
without pericardial effusion.

Trace pneumobilia is noted anteriorly along the liver, likely sequela of
recent transhepatic cholecystostomy drainage.  The gallbladder demonstrates
persistent wall thickening and hyperemia, but significantly decompressed since
___.  The gallbladder now contains hyperdense material.  There is
no intrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The common duct is somewhat prominent,
measuring 5 mm.  The spleen, a small splenule, and adrenal glands are
unremarkable.  The nephrograms are symmetric without hydronephrosis or
hydroureter.

The stomach, small and large bowel loops are normal in caliber.  Note is made
of a few small foci of extraluminal air along the superior aspect of the
greater gastric curvature (for example, 2, 23 and 2, 21), felt to be
intravenous in distribution and trace in volume, etiology and clinical
significance unclear.

There is marked inflammatory stranding centered around the pancreas and fluid
associated with this inflammatory process, with thickening of bilateral
Gerota's fascia, consistent with ____.  Small amount of simple fluid
also layers along the dependent portion of the right paracolic gutter. 
Allowing for such, the pancreatic parenchyma enhances normally, without
evidence of necrosis.  There is no pseudocyst.  Arterial and venous structures
are patent.  No pseudoaneurysm or thrombosis is apparent.  There is
significant inflammatory stranding and fluid around the duodenum, involving
the second and third, possibly fourth segments, likely reactive.

There is no mesenteric or retroperitoneal adenopathy.

CT PELVIS:
The bladder, distal ureters, prostate, and rectum appear within
normal limits.  There is no inguinal or pelvic side wall adenopathy.  No free
fluid in the pelvis.

BONE WINDOW:
No focal concerning lesion.

baseline.patient_history: ___ year old woman with history of chronic abdominal pain  presents with worsened midepigastric pain for 4 days.  One month  ago, the patient underwent a total hysterectomy for uterine  cancer.  She spent 3 weeks at a rehab where she was receiving  dilaudid for pain control (presumably for the surgical wound  pain).  Upon arriving at home, the patient noticed pain in her  midepigastrum mostly only after she takes POs.  This pain is  worse when she eats dairy products, but also happens with other  foods and drink.  Since going home, the patient did not take any  dilaudid.  The patient always thinks that abdominal pain is  associated with cancer---such as the uterine cancer she was  recently diagnosed with.  Review of systems is positive for decresed appetite (because of  pain), nausea without vomiting, abdominal pain (just in  midepigastric area), chronic urinary incontinence and feeling of  dysuria, vertigo (from BPPV), and possible depression.    She denies fevers, chills, sore throat, cough, chest pain,  shortness of breath, diarrhea, lower abdominal pain, bleeding  anywhere, vaginal bleeding, or rash.   Past Medical History: Uterine cancer s/p TAH/BSO Chronic abdominal pain Hypertension   Hyperlipidemia,   Status post total knee replacement and revision   Spinal stenosis   S/p cholecystectomy  Distant hernia repair   Cataracts s/p surgery   Nasal polyps s/p removal   Urinary incontinence   Cystocele   Breast calcifications    Diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon   CBD dilation s/p sphinctorotomy x 2 in ___   Social History: ___ Family History: Brother with DM, ___ on HD   Sister with breast and colon ca   Brother with CAD s/p CABG   Father with CVA

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
MDCT images were acquired through the abdomen and pelvis from the
level of the lung bases to the pubic symphysis after administration of the
intravenous contrast.  Sagittal and coronal reformats were generated and
reviewed.

FINDINGS:


CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:
The visualized lung bases appear normal.
There is no pleural effusion.  Visualized portion of the heart appears
unremarkable, with no evidence of pericardial effusion.

Multiple tiny subcentimeter hypodense lesions are noted in the liver (2:15 and
2:17), too small to characterize and are stable since the prior study.  There
is a stable dilatation of the intra- and extra-hepatic bile ducts,  with a
dilated common bile duct measuring 15.6 mm (300B:26).  The patient is status
post cholecystectomy with surgical clips noted in the gallbladder bed.  The
pancreatic duct appears normal.  The adrenal glands and the spleen appear
unremarkable.  Again noted are tiny punctate calcifications in the pancreatic
parenchyma in the region of the tail and head of the pancreas, unchanged since
the prior study.  There is no evidence of ____.  Both kidneys
show multiple subcentimeter hypodensities, too small to characterize and most
probably represent renal cortical cysts.  Both kidneys show symmetric
opacification and excretion of contrast.  No hydroureteronephrosis noted.  The
stomach and small bowel appear unremarkable.  Multiple diverticulae are seen
diffusely distributed through the colon.

There is no evidence of intra-abdominal free fluid or free air.  There is no
significant retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:
The bladder is distended with no wall
thickening or internal filling defect.  The uterus is status post
transabdominal hysterectomy. There is stranding in the left pelvis, extending
along the anterior abdominal wall and subcutaneous soft tissues,  in the
region of the surgical access for hysterectomy.  There is a fluid collection
with ___ value of 29, seen along the left lateral pelvic wall, causing lateral
displacement of the left ureter. The collection has an enhancing wall and
measures 2.7 x 1.6 cm. There are multiple diverticula in the distal sigmoid
colon.  

The diagnostic consideration in this patient would include a postoperative
pelvic abscess with surrounding inflammatory fat stranding, related to the
recent hysterectomy.The diagnosis of sigmoid diverticulitis with pelvic
abscess is a less likely possibility due to the following reasons- the absence
of a culprit diverticulum ,  fat stranding localized more to the left lateral
pelvic wall and not centred around the sigmoid colon ,  absence of reactive
colonic wall thickening and the pelvic abscess located farther away from the
sigmoid diverticuli.

A small stable right inguinal hernia containing small bowel is again noted.
There is no pelvic free fluid.  There is no significant pelvic lymphadenopathy
detected.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
Diffuse degenerative changes noted in
the distal thoracic and lumbar spine, with grade 1 anterolisthesis of L5 on S1
with bilateral pars interarticularis defect.  Also noted are ossific densities
in the bilateral gluteal subcutaneous soft tissue, most likely representing
injection granulomas.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Biliary-duct obstruction / choledocholithiasis or biliary stricture (given dilated CBD at 15.6 mm on CT, prior sphincterotomies x2, post-cholecystectomy state, and postprandial midepigastric pain); also possible sphincter of Oddi dysfunction or recurrent biliary pathology
why_trace.information_gap: The CT shows a persistently dilated common bile duct (15.6 mm) in a post-cholecystectomy patient with prior sphincterotomies, but CT cannot characterize the cause of the dilation (stone, stricture, sludge, ampullary lesion) or assess the biliary tree in detail; MRCP is needed to define the ductal anatomy and identify any obstructing pathology
why_trace.expected_finding: A filling defect (stone or sludge) within the common bile duct, a biliary stricture, or ampullary pathology explaining the ductal dilation; alternatively, a normal duct lumen that would redirect the workup
grounding: CT report: 'stable dilatation of the intra- and extra-hepatic bile ducts, with a dilated common bile duct measuring 15.6 mm'
History: 'CBD dilation s/p sphincterotomy x 2'
History: 'S/p cholecystectomy'
History: 'pain in her midepigastrum mostly only after she takes POs'
Labs: 'Lipase: 80.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — mildly elevated
CT report: 'The pancreatic duct appears normal'
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 64.0 IU/L', 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.6 mg/dL', 'ALT: 18.0 IU/L' — liver enzymes currently normal, not excluding intermittent biliary obstruction

baseline.patient_history: ___ with history of small intestine bacterial overgrowth (SIBO,  diagnosed ___, h.pylori s/p antibiotic treatment, with  episodic abdominal pain since ___ who presents with  subacute worsening of abdominal pain with bloating starting 5  days ago. Since the ___ prior to admission, the patient  developed fairly sudden onset of abdominal pain ___ with assoc  nausea, no vomiting or diarrhea or BRBPR. She has been passing  flatus, and last BM was day prior to admission. It is  epigrastric and in the lower abdominal areas, with some  radiation to the back. There was no clear trigger, though the  patient suspects a dietary source. She has had associated severe  bloating. . For the past year the patient has had episodic abdominal pain,  epigastric and lower abdomen with some associated back pain, not  relieved with bowel movements with possible trigger of certain  foods. The patient has been on a low FOD/MAP diet that is gluten  and dairy free, without complete resolution of these episodes.  She also has had loose watery bowel movements, sometimes 2 times  a day, starting in ___. She has had outpatient workup,  starting with an EGD and colonoscopy in ___ which was normal  (including biopsies), abd CT in ___ which showed periportal  edema and pericholecystic fluid but was otherwise unremarkable.  Labs from ___ including CBC, stool studies (giardia,  cryptosporidum, c.diff, campylobacter, salmonella and shigella),  and CRP were normal. ___ LFTs were normal, amylase 110,  lipase 91. She states that she has been tested before for celiac  disease and all workup was negative (original record of this not  available). She has tried acid-suppressive medications in the  past which have not been helpful. . She denies any fevers, chills, rashes, sun sensitivity,  hair/skin/nail changes. She does have dry mouth for the past few  weeks. She did travel to ___ for 5 days, but symptoms did not  worsen at that time. . All other review of systems negative. . visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Grayscale and Doppler ultrasound images of right upper quadrant
were obtained.

FINDINGS:
The liver maintains normal echogenicity and echotexture without focal lesions.
There is no intra or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  The common bile
duct measures 2 mm.  The gallbladder is collapsed without stones or
pericholecystic fluid.  The main portal vein is patent with antegrade flow. 
The pancreas is unremarkable without focal lesions.  Limited views of the
right kidney and inferior vena cava are unremarkable.  There is no ascites in
the right upper quadrant.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: bowel obstruction, mesenteric/lymph node pathology, gynecologic pathology (ovarian cyst/torsion), inflammatory bowel disease, peritoneal process, or occult intra-abdominal pathology not captured by RUQ ultrasound
why_trace.information_gap: The RUQ ultrasound has cleared the biliary tree and gallbladder, but the source of epigastric and lower abdominal pain with back radiation, bloating, and mildly elevated lipase (66, above ref 60) remains anatomically unlocalized — the pelvis, small bowel, mesentery, retroperitoneum, and gynecologic structures have not been imaged
why_trace.expected_finding: The physician is hoping to identify a structural or inflammatory abnormality — such as bowel wall thickening, mesenteric stranding, a pelvic mass or ovarian pathology, pancreatic edema/peripancreatic stranding, lymphadenopathy, or early obstruction — that explains the acute-on-chronic pain pattern and bloating
grounding: History: 'epigastric and in the lower abdominal areas, with some radiation to the back'
History: 'severe bloating'
Laboratory: 'Lipase: 66.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — mildly above upper limit of normal
Prior imaging: 'The gallbladder is collapsed without stones or pericholecystic fluid' — biliary source excluded
Prior imaging: 'The pancreas is unremarkable without focal lesions' — limited pancreatic views on US only
History: 'abd CT in ___ which showed periportal edema and pericholecystic fluid but was otherwise unremarkable' — prior CT showed fluid, current episode may have new findings
Physical examination: 'slightly distended, TTP in lower abdominal area and epigastric area. No RUQ pain. no rebound or guarding'
History: 'episodic abdominal pain since ___' with 'outpatient workup... EGD and colonoscopy in ___ which was normal'

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: no trigger detected — step-2 skipped

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The RUQ ultrasound has excluded biliary pathology but leaves the lower abdomen, pelvis, retroperitoneum, and full pancreas unevaluated; given the multi-focal pain (epigastric and lower abdominal), back radiation, mildly elevated lipase, and a year of unexplained episodic symptoms, a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the logical next step to survey the entire cavity.

baseline.patient_history: ___ ___ man with HTN and HLD who presents  with nausea and vomiting x 2 days. He reports that 2 days ago he  and his family ate at a restaurant, and that evening he  developed lower abdominal cramping accompanied by severe chills. He therefore came to the ED,  and in triage had an episode of bilious emesis. No CP or SOB. No  orthopnea or PND. He has no subjective fevers, no urinary  symptoms. No cough or hemoptysis. why_trace.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis as a cause of pancreatitis; mesenteric ischemia given elevated lactate and sepsis physiology; infectious/toxic gastroenteritis with systemic involvement
why_trace.information_gap: The prior CT abdomen with contrast reportedly showed 'no acute intra-abdominal process' and a normal-appearing pancreas, yet the patient has markedly elevated lipase (487) and amylase (424), persistent lactic acidosis (3.4 → 2.8), fever, and sepsis physiology — raising the question of whether the initial CT was inadequate, timed too early, or whether a biliary/pancreatic ductal abnormality (e.g., choledocholithiasis, early necrotizing pancreatitis) was missed.
why_trace.expected_finding: Peripancreatic fat stranding, pancreatic necrosis, or pancreatic ductal dilation/choledocholithiasis that would explain the markedly elevated pancreatic enzymes; alternatively, mesenteric ischemia or another occult intra-abdominal source explaining the persistent lactic acidosis and sepsis physiology

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: A repeat CT abdomen with contrast is reasonable given the discordance between the markedly elevated pancreatic enzymes/lactic acidosis and the reportedly normal prior CT, though the prior CT was already contrast-enhanced and the incremental yield of an immediate repeat is uncertain without a clear change in clinical status; an MRCP or RUQ ultrasound might more specifically address biliary etiology.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
AP and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:
The cardiomediastinal silhouettes are within normal limits.  There is a
heavily calcified aortic arch.  The bilateral hila are unremarkable.  There is
evidence of bronchial wall thickening most conspicuous in the lower lobes,
suggestive of small airways disease.  There is a left a lower lobe opacity
which is concerning for infection or sequelae of aspiration the appropriate
clinical setting.  There is no right pleural effusion.  Difficult to exclude a
trace left pleural effusion.  There is no pneumothorax.

EXAMINATION:
CT abdomen with contrast

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT images of the abdomen were acquired with
intravenous contrast. No oral contrast was administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 5.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 26.5 mGy (Body) DLP =
13.2 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 3.3 s, 35.5 cm; CTDIvol = 9.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 322.0
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 335 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
The visualized heart and pericardium are within normal limits. 
There is mild bibasilar atelectasis, more marked on the left.

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver is normal in size and attenuation.  There is no intra
or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.  There is a bilobed 2.2 x 1.8 cm
hepatic cyst at the liver dome (02:11).  The gallbladder is within normal
limits.  Subcentimeter hepatic hypodensities are too small to characterize on

CT.:


PANCREAS:
The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal concerning lesions.  There is no pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is
no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size. There is no
hydronephrosis.    Subcentimeter hypodensities throughout both kidneys are too
small to characterize on CT.  There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
The stomach is unremarkable.  Visualized small bowel loops
demonstrate normal caliber and wall thickness.  The visualized colon is
unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no evidence of retroperitoneal or mesenteric
lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no upper abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Mild atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES:
There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis; gallstone pancreatitis (gallstone as etiology of elevated lipase/amylase not excluded by CT)
why_trace.information_gap: The CT abdomen showed a normal-appearing pancreas without peripancreatic stranding, yet lipase is 487 and amylase is 424 — the etiology of pancreatic enzyme elevation is unexplained. Specifically, whether gallstones or biliary sludge (not reliably detected on CT) are driving a biliary/gallstone pancreatitis picture is unknown, because CT has low sensitivity for cholelithiasis and microlithiasis.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary sludge, or a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis or biliary obstruction as the precipitant of the elevated pancreatic enzymes; alternatively, sonographic signs of acute cholecystitis (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign) that CT may have missed.

Labs: Lipase: 487.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)

CT Abdomen: 'There is no intra or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.'
History: 'nausea and vomiting x 2 days... lower abdominal cramping accompanied by severe chills'

discordance.evidence: Troponin T: 0.21 ng/mL (ref 0.0-0.01); Lactate: 3.4 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0) > 2.8 mmol/L on repeat; 'CT abdomen w/contrast showed no acute intra-abdominal process. The pancreas is normal in attenuation and there is no peripancreatic fat stranding'; Lipase: 487.0 IU/L; Amylase: 424.0 IU/L

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ordered liver/gallbladder ultrasound and its reasoning are entirely focused on explaining the elevated pancreatic enzymes via a biliary etiology, while the most critical flagged discordance — a troponin T 21× the upper limit of normal with persistently elevated lactate suggesting a primary cardiac event (NSTEMI) — is completely unaddressed by this imaging order or the stated reasoning.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Ultrasound is the first-line modality for detecting gallstones and biliary pathology — the most common correctable cause of pancreatitis — which CT with contrast is known to miss, and the CT explicitly noted no biliary ductal dilatation but did not characterize the gallbladder for stones.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Multiple axial images of the abdomen and pelvis from lung bases
through the pubic symphysis were obtained following the uneventful
administration of oral and 130 cc Optiray IV contrast.

FINDINGS:


CT ABDOMEN:
There is minor bibasilar atelectasis.  No pleural or pericardial effusion.

The liver, spleen and adrenal glands are normal in appearance.  There is a
7-mm low attenuation lesion in the upper pole of the right kidney which is too
small to characterize, likely representing a small cyst.  There is no
hydronephrosis.  The gallbladder is fluid filled.  

There is ill defined fluid in the retroperitoneum centered about the pancreas.
There is non-enhancement in the region of the distal body and tail of
pancreas, less than 30% of the total gland worrisome for early necrosis. There
are no well defined fluid collections. There is fluid tracking in the
paracolic gutters and a small amount of hemorrhagic fluid layering in the
pelvis.  The extent of ill-defined fluid has progressed, and non-enhancement
of a portion of the distal pancreas is new.  The wall of the descending
duodenum is also newly thickened.

There is sigmoid diverticulosis without evidence of diverticulitis.  Bowel
loops are normal caliber without evidence of obstruction.  There is no
retroperitoneal or abdominal adenopathy.

CT PELVIS:
The bladder is decompressed with a Foley in place.  There is contrast
opacifying the bladder.  There is no pelvic lymphadenopathy.

There are no lytic or sclerotic bone lesions.

why_trace.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema, peripancreatic fat stranding, or necrosis consistent with acute pancreatitis; also evaluation for biliary ductal dilation (choledocholithiasis as etiology), bowel obstruction (dilated loops with transition point), or signs of mesenteric ischemia (bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis, portal venous gas)

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT ABD AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed with IV
contrast only. Multiplanar reformations were provided.

DOSE:
DLP 846 mGy-cm

FINDINGS:
Lung Bases:  Mild basilar atelectasis is noted without pleural effusion. The
imaged heart is within normal limits of size without pericardial effusion
seen. A small hiatal hernia is noted.

Abdomen: The liver enhances normally without focal lesion. The gallbladder is
unremarkable. No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation is seen.
The spleen is unremarkable though a small amount of perisplenic free fluid is
noted. There is a metallic bullet-shaped foreign body adjacent to the left
twelfth rib, unchanged. Surgical clips are seen adjacent to the distal body of
the pancreas.  There is peripancreatic fat stranding which is concerning for
____.  No signs of pancreatic necrosis or pseudocyst formation.
Adrenal glands are normal bilaterally. The kidneys enhance symmetrically and
excrete contrast promptly. Fat containing lesion arising from the lower pole
of the left kidney is most compatible with an AML. Hypodense simple cysts in
the right kidney are noted as well as tiny hypodensities too small to
characterize. The gallbladder appears normal. The abdominal aorta is normally
opacified with normal course and caliber with widely patent major branches. No
retroperitoneal hematoma or lymphadenopathy is seen.

The stomach and duodenum appear normal.

Pelvis: Loops of small bowel demonstrate no signs of ileus or obstruction. 
Surgical clips are noted in the left mid abdomen.  No signs of appendicitis.
The colon appears unremarkable aside from the presence of scattered
diverticulosis.  The uterus is absent. No adnexal masses are seen. No free
pelvic fluid. The urinary bladder is only partially distended.  No inguinal
lymphadenopathy.

Bones: No worrisome lytic or blastic osseous lesion is seen.    Degenerative
changes are mild with Schmorl's nodes at L4 level.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary pathology (cholelithiasis, choledocholithiasis, biliary sludge) as a precipitant of pancreatitis; hepatic pathology given markedly elevated AST/ALT
why_trace.information_gap: The CT identified peripancreatic fat stranding consistent with pancreatitis but did not characterize the biliary system for gallstones or biliary sludge, which is the most common correctable etiology of acute pancreatitis and would directly change management; additionally, the markedly elevated transaminases (AST 157, ALT 69) and lipase (14240) raise concern for a biliary/hepatic source that CT with IV contrast alone may not fully delineate.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary sludge, or a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis as the precipitating cause of pancreatitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder to shift suspicion toward other etiologies (hypertriglyceridemia, medication-related, or post-surgical adhesion-related).
grounding: Labs: Lipase: 14240.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Labs: Amylase: 840.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)
Labs: Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 157.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)
Labs: Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 69.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)
CT report: 'There is peripancreatic fat stranding which is concerning for ____. No signs of pancreatic necrosis or pseudocyst formation.'
CT report: 'The gallbladder is unremarkable. No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation is seen.'
History: 'sudden onset abdominal pain, N/V' after eating
History: 'Afib, on coumadin' — anticoagulation makes hemorrhagic pancreatitis or other bleeding source relevant

discordance.evidence: Lactate: 3.2 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0); Lipase: 14240.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0); 'Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present, no rebound tenderness or guarding'

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ultrasound can identify a biliary etiology but cannot resolve the core discordance flag—the striking mismatch between massively elevated lactate/lipase with prior hypotension and a completely benign abdominal exam—because the physician's reasoning focuses entirely on etiology localization rather than on assessing for occult hemodynamic compromise or severe pancreatitis complications (e.g., necrosis, vascular involvement) that would explain the discordance.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT confirmed peripancreatic inflammation consistent with pancreatitis and a lipase of 14,240 IU/L, but ultrasound is the first-line modality to evaluate for gallstones and biliary ductal dilation as the etiology, which CT with IV contrast (no oral contrast, no dedicated biliary protocol) is less sensitive for detecting.

baseline.patient_history: Pt is ___ year old woman with past history of UC s/p colectomy  ___ presenting with acute worsening of abdominal pain.  She  was diagnosed with UC ___ years ago and suffered three flairs  where she would experience blood and mucous in her feces.  Last  ___ she had a flair that was treated with moderate success  with asacol and medicated suppositories but had acute worsening  in late ___.  She was found to have a toxic megacolon that was  removed at this institution on ___.  She currently is planned  for three reconstructive bowel surgeries but is currently with  an illiostomy.   She states that she had crampy abdominal pain starting three  days ago.  She states they were akin to menstrual cramps but  worsened last night to the point where she did not have dinner.   She was then awoken from sleep at 2 am with severe, constant LUQ  pain that wrapped around her left side to her back.  Nothing  made the pain better and it was worsened by laying on her left  side. visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Supine and upright abdominal radiographs are provided.

There is a normal bowel gas pattern without evidence of obstruction.  Am
ileostomy is present in the right lower quadrant.  There is no free air.  The
visualized lung bases are clear.  Osseous structures are unremarkable.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:


TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT images of the abdomen and pelvis were obtained
with oral and intravenous contrast.  Sagittal and coronal reformations were
also performed.

FINDINGS:


CT ABDOMEN:
Patchy opacities, dependent portions of the lower lungs are most
consistent with atelectasis.  There is no pleural effusion.

The liver, gallbladder, spleen, and adrenal glands appear within normal
limits.  There is mild fat stranding about the pancreas which demonstrated
slight fullness.  A small hypodense focus in the interpolar region of the
right kidney measuring 4 mm is too small to characterize but unchanged.  

There is new mild left-sided hydronephrosis, and the left ureter is moderately
dilated down to the pelvic inlet after which its course is difficult to
follow.  There is a small calcification near the left ureterovesical junction,
but already present before and probably a phlebolith lying near the area.  Fat
stranding is present about the left kidney and the nephrogram is slightly
delayed.  The left renal vein is mildly narrowed as it crosses beneath
superior mesenteric artery, which arises from the aorta at a highly acute
angle. 

The patient is status post colectomy.  There is a rectal stump with persistent
inflammatory mucosal enhancement, mild wall thickening, and surrounding fat
stranding.

CT PELVIS:
Intermingled with small bowel along the superior aspect of the
bladder to the right of midline is a small rim well-circumscribed focus of gas
and fluid which is suspected to represent a segment of small bowel within the
pelvis, noting collapsed but poorly delineated small bowel in the area, but it
is difficult to exclude a small fluid collection measuring up to perhaps 28 x
13 mm in axial ___ (2:64).  There are also small tubular
fluid-containing structures along each pelvic sidewall, but quite small and
more extensive on the left than right, which may represent a small
postoperative fluid collections (2:63)  On the right, areas of slight fluid
accumulation measure up to approximately 24 x 14 mm in axial ___.  On
the right it is hard to distinguish post-operative change from small bowel. 
The major mesenteric arteries and veins appear patent.  There is no ascites.

BONE WINDOWS:
There are no suspicious lytic or blastic lesions.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Urolithiasis/obstructive uropathy (left ureteral stone vs. phlebolith), biliary pathology (choledocholithiasis as pancreatitis etiology), or nutcracker phenomenon causing left renal vein compression
why_trace.information_gap: The CT identified new left hydronephrosis with a possible calcification near the left ureterovesical junction and a delayed left nephrogram, but could not definitively characterize whether this represents an obstructing ureteral stone versus a phlebolith; additionally, the biliary tree was not well characterized on CT and a gallstone/biliary cause of the markedly elevated lipase (715) and amylase (285) has not been excluded
why_trace.expected_finding: The physician is hoping to see either (a) a definitive obstructing calculus in the left ureter/UVJ to explain the hydronephrosis, or (b) gallstones, biliary duct dilation, or gallbladder wall thickening that would identify a biliary etiology for the acute pancreatitis
grounding: Labs: Lipase: 715.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Labs: Amylase: 285.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)
CT Abdomen: 'There is mild fat stranding about the pancreas which demonstrated slight fullness'
CT Abdomen: 'There is new mild left-sided hydronephrosis, and the left ureter is moderately dilated down to the pelvic inlet'
CT Abdomen: 'There is a small calcification near the left ureterovesical junction, but already present before and probably a phlebolith lying near the area'
CT Abdomen: 'Fat stranding is present about the left kidney and the nephrogram is slightly delayed'
CT Abdomen: 'The liver, gallbladder, spleen, and adrenal glands appear within normal limits' — gallbladder described only grossly, no dedicated biliary assessment
History: 'She was then awoken from sleep at 2 am with severe, constant LUQ pain that wrapped around her left side to her back'

study_inadequacy.evidence: There is new mild left-sided hydronephrosis, and the left ureter is moderately dilated down to the pelvic inlet after which its course is difficult to follow. There is a small calcification near the left ureterovesical junction, but already present before and probably a phlebolith lying near the area.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: Abdominal ultrasound can confirm hydronephrosis and assess the proximal ureter and UVJ region for an obstructing calculus versus phlebolith, and the reasoning explicitly names this goal, but ultrasound has limited ability to trace the full course of the distal ureter beyond the pelvic inlet — the precise region the CT failed to characterize — making it unlikely to definitively resolve the obstructive uropathy question that a dedicated CT urogram or repeat thin-slice non-contrast CT would better address.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Ultrasound is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary tree (gallstones, CBD dilation) as the most common reversible cause of acute pancreatitis given lipase 715, and to better characterize the left hydronephrosis and possible UVJ calcification flagged on CT as indeterminate.

why_trace.expected_finding: Peripancreatic fat stranding and/or fluid consistent with acute pancreatitis (Balthazar grading), and critically, presence or absence of free air/perforation, biliary ductal dilation, or organized necrosis that would change immediate management.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CT ABD AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT acquisition was performed DURING SINGLE PHASE after the
administration of nonionic IV contrast. Oral contrast was also administered.
Multiplanar reformats were obtained.

DOSE:
303 mGy*cm

FINDINGS:
Partial visualized left larger than right small pleural effusions with passive
atelectasis.

No focal liver lesions identified.  Post cholecystectomy.  Patent SMV, splenic
vein, portal venous system. Homogeneous appearance of the pancreas without
apparent focal lesion or main ductal dilation.  A small amount of ascites is
present throughout the abdomen and pelvis. Fat stranding is noted throughout
the anterior pararenal space adjacent to the pancreas, with mild generalized
retroperitoneal fat stranding.

Normal spleen and adrenals.

No hydronephrosis.  A tiny peripheral subcentimeter midpole left renal
hypodensity, probable cyst or angiomyolipoma.

Contrast within stomach. Mildly thickened distal duodenal folds. Contrast and
stool within the colon. The retrocecal appendix contains contrast and
demonstrates no more adjacent fat stranding in the remainder of the abdomen
and pelvis. Normal TI. No small bowel dilation.

Normal caliber abdominal aorta. Patent celiac trunk and SMA.

Nondistended bladder. Retroverted anteflexed uterus. No pelvic adenopathy.

Mild within evident total spine. No suspicious osseous lesions.

EXAMINATION:
CHEST (PA AND LAT):

FINDINGS:
PA and lateral views of the chest provided.

Moderate, bilateral pleural effusions are seen. Bilateral, lower lung
opacities likely represent moderate bibasilar atelectasis more likely than
pneumonia. Imaged osseous structures are intact.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: post-ERCP complication such as duodenal perforation, worsening peripancreatic necrosis/fluid collection, or vascular complication
why_trace.information_gap: The prior CT showed only fat stranding and a small amount of ascites without focal pancreatic necrosis or ductal disruption, but the lipase is markedly elevated at 4890 IU/L and the patient's pain has worsened and spread — the physician needs to know whether the pancreatitis has progressed to necrotizing disease, developed a peripancreatic fluid collection/pseudocyst, or whether a post-ERCP perforation has occurred that was not captured on the initial single-phase scan.
why_trace.expected_finding: Development of pancreatic or peripancreatic necrosis (non-enhancing pancreatic parenchyma), an enlarging peripancreatic fluid collection, or evidence of duodenal/retroperitoneal perforation (extraluminal air or worsening retroperitoneal stranding) not present on the prior CT.
grounding: Laboratory tests: Lipase: 4890.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
History: 'epigastric pain immediately after the ERCP which worsened and then spread to b/l lower quadrants'
Physical examination: 'Diminished bowel sounds throughout', '[+] Tenderness diffusely', '[+] mildly distended', 'P = 108' (tachycardia)

Prior imaging 1 CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST: 'Fat stranding is noted throughout the anterior pararenal space adjacent to the pancreas, with mild generalized retroperitoneal fat stranding. A small amount of ascites is present throughout the abdomen and pelvis. Homogeneous appearance of the pancreas without apparent focal lesion or main ductal dilation.'
Prior imaging 1: 'Mildly thickened distal duodenal folds'
History: 'Last ate yesterday am prior to ERCP'

discordance.evidence: Lipase: 4890.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0) with 'Homogeneous appearance of the pancreas without apparent focal lesion or main ductal dilation' and only 'Fat stranding is noted throughout the anterior pararenal space adjacent to the pancreas, with mild generalized retroperitoneal fat stranding' on CT; additionally, HR 108, BP 106/69, O2Sat 94% RA, moderate bilateral pleural effusions on CXR, and small ascites throughout abdomen/pelvis suggest a severity of systemic illness (hemodynamic borderline instability, respiratory compromise, bilateral effusions) markedly out of proportion to the relatively benign pancreatic CT appearance of homogeneous pancreas without necrosis or ductal dilation.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The repeat contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis is capable of resolving the discordance between the near-normal pancreatic CT appearance and the severe systemic illness by detecting pancreatic necrosis, evolving fluid collections, or occult perforation, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets this gap — citing the mismatch between the benign initial CT and the clinical deterioration as the driving concern.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: A repeat contrast-enhanced CT is a recognized tool for staging pancreatitis severity and detecting complications, but the short interval since the prior CT and the radiation dose concern make it only partially justified unless there is clear clinical deterioration; the worsening pain, tachycardia, and markedly elevated lipase do provide a reasonable clinical trigger for reassessment.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction with gallstone pancreatitis; the 'other' fraction reflects the high suspicion for a common bile duct stone as the precipitating etiology underlying the pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: Whether a gallstone is currently impacted in or has recently passed through the common bile duct, and whether the dilated CBD seen on the history narrative harbors an obstructing stone that was not visualized on the prior RUQ ultrasound
why_trace.expected_finding: A dilated common bile duct (>6-7 mm) with or without a visible choledocholithiasis, or evidence of acute cholecystitis (pericholecystic fluid, wall thickening, sonographic Murphy's sign) that would confirm gallstone pancreatitis and guide urgent ERCP vs. cholecystectomy planning
grounding: Labs: Lipase: 9035.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0) — massively elevated, confirming acute pancreatitis
Labs: Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 563.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0) — markedly elevated transaminitis
Labs: Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 434.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0) — markedly elevated
Labs: Alkaline Phosphatase: 214.0 IU/L (ref 35.0-105.0) — elevated, suggesting biliary obstruction
Labs: Bilirubin, Total: 3.0 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5) — elevated, consistent with biliary obstruction
Physical exam: +mild scleral icterus — clinical sign of hyperbilirubinemia/biliary obstruction
History: 'A RUQ u/s demonstrated gallstones, but no obstructed stone; CBD was dilated but again no stone seen' — prior ultrasound inconclusive for choledocholithiasis
History: 'Father had gallstones' — positive family history for cholelithiasis
History: 'sharp epigastric pain... approximately 2 hours later she felt the sharp epigastric pain again' after fatty meal (hollandaise sauce) — classic biliary/gallstone trigger

study_inadequacy.evidence: A RUQ u/s demonstrated gallstones, but no obstructed stone; CBD was dilated but again no stone seen.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The reasoning correctly identifies the unresolved CBD stone question and the information gap from the prior ultrasound, but ordering a repeat single-organ ultrasound — the same modality already shown to be insensitive for distal CBD stones — does not meaningfully improve the capability to resolve choledocholithiasis compared to MRCP or EUS, which are the appropriate next-step studies for this indication.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The history narrative already states a RUQ ultrasound was performed showing gallstones and a dilated CBD without a visible stone, so a repeat single-organ liver/gallbladder ultrasound is somewhat redundant; however, it is partially justified if the prior study was done at an outside facility or if the physician wants a formal in-house read, and the dilated CBD without identified stone still leaves the question of choledocholithiasis open — though MRCP or EUS would be more sensitive for CBD stones.

EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The liver is diffusely echogenic. The contour of the liver is smooth. 
Several simple hepatic cysts are noted.  The largest in the right lobe
measures 2.4 x 2.0 x 1.6 cm.  The main portal vein is patent with hepatopetal
flow. There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CBD measures 11 mm.

GALLBLADDER:
Cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening.

PANCREAS:
The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

SPLEEN:
Normal echogenicity, measuring 9.8 cm.

KIDNEYS:
Limited views of the right kidney show no hydronephrosis.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction causing gallstone pancreatitis; occult common bile duct stone not visualized on ultrasound
why_trace.information_gap: Whether a stone is impacted in or migrated through the common bile duct — the CBD is dilated at 11 mm on ultrasound but no stone was visualized, yet the transaminitis, elevated bilirubin, and markedly elevated lipase strongly suggest a biliary etiology; MRCP is needed to definitively characterize the CBD and detect any occult choledocholithiasis
why_trace.expected_finding: A filling defect (stone) within the common bile duct or distal biliary tree, or alternatively confirmation that the CBD is clear (ruling out choledocholithiasis as the precipitant), along with assessment of pancreatic duct integrity and peripancreatic changes
grounding: Prior imaging 1: 'The CBD measures 11 mm' and 'Cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening'

History: 'Labs were notable for a transaminitis, elevated bilirubin, leukocytosis, and significantly elevated lipase. A RUQ u/s demonstrated gallstones, but no obstructed stone; CBD was dilated but again no stone seen.'
Labs: 'Lipase: 9035.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)'
Labs: 'Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 563.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)'
Labs: 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 434.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 3.0 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)'
Physical examination: '+mild scleral icterus'
History: 'Father had gallstones'

study_inadequacy.evidence: portions of the pancreatic tail obscured by overlying bowel gas

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is the appropriate next-step modality to overcome the ultrasound's technical inadequacy — it will fully visualize the pancreas (including the tail), the pancreatic duct, and the biliary tree without bowel-gas interference, and the physician's reasoning explicitly encompasses both the occult CBD stone and pancreatic duct integrity, directly targeting the unanswered questions left by the inadequate ultrasound.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The combination of a dilated CBD (11 mm) without a visible stone on ultrasound, transaminitis (AST 434, ALT 563), elevated bilirubin (3.0), scleral icterus, and massively elevated lipase (9035) creates a clear clinical gap that MRCP — the non-invasive gold standard for CBD stone detection — is ideally suited to resolve before deciding on ERCP versus conservative management.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
Second opinion CT abdomen/pelvis.

TECHNIQUE:
Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Unavailable.  Study performed at outside hospital.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
Limited evaluation of the lung bases is notable for a small left
non hemorrhagic pleural effusion

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver is diffusely heterogeneous in signal intensity with
multiple peripheral low-density opacities scattered throughout the liver when
compared to in and out of phase imaging from outside MR demonstrates signal
drop on out of phase imaging consistent with focal hepatic steatosis.  Along
the hepatic capsule adjacent to segment 2 is a multiloculated approximately 2
x 3 x 1.7 cm collection along the gastrohepatic ligament (02:18).  There is no
evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder
is within normal limits.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is diffusely heterogeneous in signal intensity with
mild dilatation of the main pancreatic duct with few cystic structures within
the body and tail measuring up to 0.2 x 0.6 cm (02:27).  Peripancreatic fat
stranding is noted.  There hypoenhancement within the pancreatic head
worrisome for pancreatic necrosis (02:42).

SPLEEN:
Normal in size.  No splenomegaly.  Mild perisplenic free fluid noted. 
1.4 cm accessory spleen is noted.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
The stomach is unremarkable.  Loss of normal fat plane
between the pancreatic head and duodenum with fat stranding along the distal
stomach and duodenum.  Small bowel loops otherwise demonstrate normal caliber,
wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  The colon and rectum are within
normal limits.  The appendix is not visualized however no secondary signs of
acute appendicitis.  No extraluminal air.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
The visualized reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  No atherosclerotic disease
is noted.

BONES:
There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

NOTIFICATION:
The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by ___,
M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 9:22 pm, 5 minutes after discovery of
the findings.

why_trace.expected_finding: Better characterization of the extent of pancreatic necrosis, the ductal anatomy (main pancreatic duct integrity/disruption), and the precise nature and extent of the multiloculated collection abutting the GE junction — findings that would directly inform whether endoscopic, interventional, or surgical drainage is appropriate.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Ultrasonography of the liver and gallbladder.

FINDINGS:
The liver echotexture is normal.  No focal intrahepatic lesion is
detected. There is slight prominence of the intrahepatic bile ducts. The CBD
is markedly dilated, measuring up to 1.5 cm in diameter, with rapid distal
tapering.  No ductal stones are appreciated.  The gallbladder is normal. A
large right upper pole renal cyst measures up to 8.4 cm in diameter. There is
no ascites.

These findings are in agreement with the reference CT examination performed
earlier today.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound confirmed gallstones but could not visualize the pancreas or distal CBD, leaving the etiology of the markedly elevated lipase (2632) unresolved; MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for detecting choledocholithiasis and evaluating the pancreatic duct without radiation or contrast nephrotoxicity risk (relevant given eGFR ~44).

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary and pancreatic ductal anatomy in detail given markedly elevated lipase and cholestatic liver enzymes with a periampullary duodenal diverticulum noted on CT, a gallstone on repeat ultrasound, and no ductal dilation seen on prior imaging — CT and US have limited sensitivity for small CBD stones and subtle ductal pathology.

baseline.patient_history: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo F with CLL s/p multiple rounds of  chemotherapy, last in ___ (Campath), with ongoing bulky  adenopathy and splenomegaly who presents today with RUQ and  epigastric pain that radiates to her back. The pain started an  hour or so after dinner and grew progressively worse throughout the evening. The pain is sharp and constant, rated as a ___ on  arrival and ___ out of 10 currently. She has been nauseated  throughout the evening and morning as well. She reports one  episode of emesis. She denies fevers or chills. She has been  having regular BM's. She denies melena, hematochezia, or  ___ stools. She knows that she has cholelithiasis, but  denies any history of biliary colic.     Past Medical History: Oncologic Hx: She completed two cycles of R-CVP back in ___ as part of her  initial treatment for CLL. She did not have a significant  response to treatment though her white count did normalize after  treatment. However, the patient remained with a predominance of  lymphocytes. She continued to have bulky lymphadenopathy both  above and below the diaphragm following this treatment, did have  slight interval decrease overall with the exception of a slight  increase in the size of her lymph nodes in the right  supraclavicular chain. She has remained with massive  splenomegaly. She had an extended hospitalization in ___ for  further workup for fever and night sweats. Her disease status  was reassessed with a bone marrow biopsy, which confirmed her  known history of CLL. She also had a lymph node biopsy of the  right supraclavicular node in order to rule out transformation  of her disease, which was also consistent with CLL without any  evidence of transformation. However, there was note of caseating  granuloma concerning for TB. She did have a PPD placed, which  was positive. Of note, she also developed a rash in this  setting, which eventually resolved. However, it was thought to  be related to TB, noted to be granuloma annulare on biopsy.  Ultimately, it was felt that she had extrapulmonary TB. She was  ultimately started on TB medication regimen with rifampin, INH,  ethambutol, and pyrazinamide. The patient was started on that at  the time of discharge from hospital on ___. At that  point, she was still having high fevers. After a few days of  being on this regimen, her high fevers improved. Of note, due to  a poor tolerability with anorexia, nausea, weight loss, and  fatigue, we switched her regimen. The ethambutol and  pyrazinamide were discontinued on ___ and moxifloxacin  was added. She completed a six-month course of her TB medicines,  which she completed back in ___. The patient refused to take  the medications any longer. She then had a slowly rising white  blood count over the past   couple of months. Also has had a depressed platelet count. Her  CT scans have overall been stable, but remained with persistent  bulky disease above and below the diaphragm with massive  splenomegaly. Our recommendation had been to proceed with a  fludarabine-based regimen given her bulky disease, but until  recently the patient refused any treatment and we had been  monitoring her off treatment. She noted at the beginning of  ___ of her plans to go to ___ in ___ for five or six  months. As a result, she agreed to receive treatment with FCR  regimen, which she began on ___. The goal of this was to  cytoreduce her disease before she leaves for ___. Our plan  is to try to get two cycles in with time to recover prior to her  departure. She presents today for evaluation and countcheck  following her second cycle.   .   OTHER Past Medical History:   1. CLL. Please refer to OMR note ___ for extensive details.   2. Extrapulmonary TB diagnosed ___, now s/p 6 months of 4-drug  therapy with rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide.   3. Hypothyroidism   4. OA   4. OA   Social History: ___ Family History: Non-contributory

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast.  No oral contrast was
administrated.  Coronal and sagittal reformatted images provided.

FINDINGS:
There are bibasilar atelectasis at the lung bases. There is no
pleural effusion.  There is no pericardial effusion. Heart size is normal.

The liver appears fatty.  There is no intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary
duct dilatation.  The gallbladder appears normal.  No gallstones are seen.

The pancreas appears edematous, with surrounding peripancreatic free fluid,
consistent with ____.  The pancreas enhances homogeneously.
There are no fluid collections.

The spleen, adrenal glands are normal.  The kidneys enhance symmetrically and
excrete contrast symmetrically with no evidence of hydronephrosis.  There is
no perinephric stranding around the kidneys.  Loops of small and large bowel
appear grossly within normal limits. There is no evidence of bowel
obstruction.  The appendix is seen on image 2:59 and appears normal, also
better seen on series 300B:33. There is free fluid in the abdomen.  There is
no free air.  There are no pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or
mesenteric lymph nodes.

CT PELVIS:
The urinary bladder, prostate, seminal vesicles, rectum and
sigmoid appear within normal limits.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.
There are no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the pelvic or inguinal
area according to the CT size criteria.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are seen.

why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones in the gallbladder or common bile duct (choledocholithiasis), or biliary sludge, that would identify a gallstone etiology for the pancreatitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder/biliary tree would shift suspicion toward hypertriglyceridemia, alcohol, or other causes.

The liver appears fatty.  There is no intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary
duct dilatation.  The gallbladder appears normal.  No gallstones are seen.

The pancreas appears edematous, with surrounding peripancreatic free fluid,
consistent with ____.  The pancreas enhances homogeneously.

excrete contrast symmetrically with no evidence of hydronephrosis.  There is

obstruction.  The appendix is seen on image 2:59 and appears normal, also
better seen on series 300B:33. There is free fluid in the abdomen.  There is
no free air.  There are no pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or
mesenteric lymph nodes.

sigmoid appear within normal limits.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.
There are no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the pelvic or inguinal
area according to the CT size criteria.

No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are seen.

FINDINGS:

excrete contrast symmetrically with no evidence of hydronephrosis.  There is

patient pain.  Echogenic liver consistent with fatty infiltration. The
gallbladder appears within normal limits. No gallstones are seen. CBD measures
3 mm.  No intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation is seen. Portal vein is patent.
Free fluid is seen, likely due to findings of ____ and seen on
CT earlier today.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar T1- and T2-weighted breath-hold independent imaging
was performed on a 1.5 Tesla magnet, including dynamic, sequential images were
obtained prior to, during and after the uneventful intravenous administration
of 0.1 mL/kg of gadolinium-DTPA.

FINDINGS:
Lung bases, there are small bilateral pleural effusions with
overlying atelectasis.

The liver is of normal signal intensity with no focal abnormality.  No
intrahepatic bile duct dilatation.  The common bile duct is not dilated.
Within the gallbladder sludge is seen within the neck ( Se 6; Im 27).  There
may be some tiny punctate stones also within the dislodge.  Gallbladder wall
is not thickened.

There is an extensive amount of ascites seen throughout the abdomen, in the
perihepatic, perisplenic regions, as well as both paracolic gutters, the
anterior pararenal spaces, and within the subcutaneous tissues of the lateral
abdominal wall.  Peripancreatic free fluid is also seen.

The entire pancreas is of low signal intensity in keeping with edema on T1-
weighted imaging. It does enhance homogeneously on post-contrast imaging
indicating that no newcrosis is present. There is a focal fluid collection
identified in relation to the inferior border of the body of the pancreas
which measure 13 x 25 mm in diamter and is seen on image (6;17). The
pancreatic duct is well visualized within the tail and is of abnormal caliber
in this region. In the body of the pancreas, there is a serpiginous linear low
signal intensity structure on T1- weighted imaging, which lies quite
anteriorly .This is seen on series 18, image 28. This may represent the
pancreatic duct. If so, this is dilated( measuring 4.8 mm in diameter) with
multiple areas of strictures. Alternatively, this could represent a small
linear fluid collection that is evolving.

The SMV, splenic vein, and portal vein are patent with no evidence of
thrombosis.  The SMA, splenic artery are patent and are not thrombosed.  No
aneurysmal dilatation seen in relation to the arteries.

The spleen, both kidneys, and both adrenal glands are of normal signal
intensity with no focal abnormality.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: pancreatic ductal disruption, evolving pancreatic necrosis, or a vascular complication (e.g., pseudoaneurysm, splenic/portal vein thrombosis)
why_trace.information_gap: The MRCP identified an ambiguous serpiginous structure in the pancreatic body that could represent either a dilated/strictured pancreatic duct or an evolving linear fluid collection, and a small focal fluid collection at the inferior border of the pancreatic body; a contrast-enhanced CT with non-contrast phase can better characterize pancreatic parenchymal enhancement (to detect interval necrosis) and delineate vascular structures and fluid collections with higher spatial resolution than MRI in this acutely ill patient.
why_trace.expected_finding: Interval development of pancreatic necrosis (non-enhancing pancreatic parenchyma), evolution or enlargement of the peripancreatic fluid collection seen on MRCP (13x25 mm), or clarification of the ambiguous ductal/fluid structure in the pancreatic body; alternatively, a vascular complication such as splenic or portal vein thrombosis.
grounding: Lipase: 7578.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0) — massively elevated, confirming acute pancreatitis

MRCP FINDINGS: 'In the body of the pancreas, there is a serpiginous linear low signal intensity structure on T1-weighted imaging...This may represent the pancreatic duct. If so, this is dilated (measuring 4.8 mm in diameter) with multiple areas of strictures. Alternatively, this could represent a small linear fluid collection that is evolving.'
MRCP FINDINGS: 'There is a focal fluid collection identified in relation to the inferior border of the body of the pancreas which measure 13 x 25 mm in diameter'

MRCP FINDINGS: 'The entire pancreas is of low signal intensity in keeping with edema on T1-weighted imaging. It does enhance homogeneously on post-contrast imaging indicating that no necrosis is present.'
MRCP FINDINGS: 'There is an extensive amount of ascites seen throughout the abdomen'
CT Abdomen FINDINGS: 'The pancreas appears edematous, with surrounding peripancreatic free fluid...The pancreas enhances homogeneously. There are no fluid collections.'
WBC: 14.5 K/uL — leukocytosis suggesting systemic inflammatory response
Glucose: 270.0 mg/dL — hyperglycemia consistent with pancreatic endocrine dysfunction

study_inadequacy.evidence: Limited views were obtained from the liver and gallbladder due to patient pain... Limited views were obtained of the liver and gallbladder due to patient pain.

resolve.reason: The CT abdomen is mechanically capable of better characterizing the biliary system and detecting gallstones/sludge, but the physician's reasoning is entirely focused on pancreatic necrosis, the ambiguous ductal structure, and fluid collection characterization — not on resolving the technically limited gallbladder/biliary evaluation — making coverage of the study_inadequacy flag incidental at best.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The MRCP raised specific unresolved questions about pancreatic duct integrity and a focal fluid collection, and contrast-enhanced CT with a non-contrast phase is the standard modality for grading pancreatitis severity and detecting necrosis, which directly guides management escalation.
