visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
This study was imported from an outside institution for re-review.
A second opinion interpretation was requested as only a brief verbal report of
the study was known at the time.  MDCT-acquired axial images of the abdomen
and pelvis were obtained with IV and oral contrast at 5-mm slice thickness. 
Additional coronal and sagittal reconstructions were performed at 3-mm slice
thickness.

FINDINGS:
Included views of the lungs demonstrate mild dependent atelectasis
bilaterally.  There is no pericardial or pleural effusion.  The heart size is
top normal.

A large multicystic mass is seen at the head of the pancreas (2:32), measuring
up to 50 x 47 mm axially.  Coarse internal calcifications are seen (2:34). 
There are multiple irregular septations with varying thickness.  This lesion
abuts the second portion of the duodenum, where there is asymmetric wall
thickening (2:31).  Neighboring fat stranding is present.  The distal pancreas
appears normal.  The pancreatic duct is not dilated, measuring 3 mm.  The
portal vein, SMV, and splenic veins are patent and appear separate from the
lesion.  The SMA is preserved. A prominent retrocrural node (2:29) does not
meet CT criteria for lymphadenopathy.

The CBD is normal in caliber.  The gallbladder is unremarkable.  A 15 mm right
hepatic cyst is present (2:11).  Other subcentimeter hypodense lesions within
the liver (2: 11,25,29) are too small for further characterization.  The
spleen, adrenal glands, stomach, and kidneys are normal.  Mild atherosclerotic
calcification of the abdominal aorta is present.  There is no mesenteric
lymphadenopathy, and no free air or free fluid. 

The rectum, sigmoid colon, intrapelvic loops of small and large bowel,
bladder, uterus, and adnexa appear normal. There is no intrapelvic
lymphadenopathy or free fluid.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
There is no acute fracture or dislocation.  No concerning
blastic or lytic lesions are seen.  Anterior osteophytosis at L2/3 is seen.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Pancreatic head cystic neoplasm (e.g., mucinous cystic neoplasm, IPMN, solid pseudopapillary tumor, or cystadenocarcinoma) with possible vascular involvement or ductal communication; the dominant concern has shifted from acute pancreatitis to characterization of a complex pancreatic mass
why_trace.information_gap: The outside CT identified a large multicystic mass at the pancreatic head with coarse calcifications, irregular septations, and abutment of the duodenum, but was acquired at an outside institution at 5-mm slice thickness with only a brief verbal report — the physician needs higher-resolution arterial and venous phase imaging to characterize vascular anatomy (SMA, SMV, portal vein encasement/invasion), ductal communication, and lesion enhancement pattern to guide surgical planning or further workup
why_trace.expected_finding: Detailed multiphasic enhancement characteristics of the cystic pancreatic head mass (arterial vs. venous phase enhancement of septations/nodules), precise relationship to peripancreatic vasculature (SMA, SMV, portal vein), and any evidence of arterial encasement or venous thrombosis that would change resectability assessment
grounding: Prior imaging 1 FINDINGS: 'A large multicystic mass is seen at the head of the pancreas (2:32), measuring up to 50 x 47 mm axially. Coarse internal calcifications are seen (2:34). There are multiple irregular septations with varying thickness.'
Prior imaging 1 FINDINGS: 'This lesion abuts the second portion of the duodenum, where there is asymmetric wall thickening (2:31).'
Prior imaging 1 TECHNIQUE: 'This study was imported from an outside institution for re-review... MDCT-acquired axial images... at 5-mm slice thickness.'
Prior imaging 1 FINDINGS: 'The portal vein, SMV, and splenic veins are patent and appear separate from the lesion. The SMA is preserved.'
Labs: 'Lipase: 247.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — elevated lipase consistent with pancreatic pathology
History: 'acute-onset sharp epigastric pain this morning 6:30am without association with food intake, waxing and waning but progressively worsening'

Coarse internal calcifications are seen (2:34). There are multiple irregular septations with varying thickness. This lesion abuts the second portion of the duodenum, where there is asymmetric wall thickening (2:31).

resolve.reason: The ordered multiphasic CTA abdomen with reconstructions is directly capable of resolving the discordance by providing high-resolution characterization of the complex pancreatic head mass (enhancement pattern, ductal communication, vascular relationships) that the outside CT could not, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets this mass as the dominant finding requiring further characterization — encompassing the flagged discordance between elevated lipase and cystic neoplasm morphology.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The outside CT demonstrated a 50x47 mm multicystic pancreatic head mass with irregular septations and duodenal abutment but was limited by slice thickness and lack of dedicated multiphasic protocol; a dedicated CTA with pre- and post-contrast phases and reconstructions is the appropriate next step to characterize vascularity and resectability of this lesion.

baseline.patient_history: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o female who presented with an episode of  epigastric pain. Yesterday evening she had a large seafood  dinner with salmon tartare, scallops with buttery sauce, and  wine. She went to bed at midnight, and at 2am she began to  experience a sharp pain that radiated upward. She had one BM  prior to arrival to ED, soft, nonbloody, no melena. She  mentioned that the pain got worse and she had some diaphoresis  on her trip into the hospital, but she thinks that may have been  secondary to anxiety. Her stressor includes ___ daughter who is  moving to college. She denies nausea, vomiting, constipation or  diarrhea.   .   In the ED vitals were 97.2 67 128/71 18 100% RA. She notably had  a lipase of 214. She had a RUQ U/S and chest x-ray which were  unremarkable. Per patient she was offered morphine 4mg but  refused. However, ED records show she was given morphine. She  did get aspirin 325mg. She also took a GI cocktail (simethicone,  ondansetron, Donnatal). Pain resolved around 3am. She was  admitted for evaluation and management of epigastric pain.  Vitals upon transfer were 97.8, 79, 123/57, 18, 94% RA.   .   On the floor, patient feels much better. Suggests she is getting  hungry. She has ___ pain and notes that the GI cocktail helped.  She notes that she was on a 7-day course for bladder infection,  but cannot remember the antibiotic. The husband will bring the  abx today/tomorrow.   .   ROS:   + per HPI. Also notes vision changes that she is seeing ophthal  outpatient for (visual distortions likely due to vessel disease)    .   - Denies fever, chills, night sweats, headache, rhinorrhea,  congestion, sore throat, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain,  current abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea,  constipation, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, hematuria. baseline.patient_history: The patient is a ___ y/o F with pMHx significant for HTN, HLD,  GERD presenting with abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting and  transaminitis found a PCP's office.    Patient was in her usual state of health until ___ when she  noted abdominal pain and N/V after eating scallops for dinner.  She continued to have intermittent episodes of vomiting  overnight with resolution of her symptoms the next morning. She  had ___ days without recurrence and was able to take in POs  normally. On ___ she had additional RUQ/epigastric pain, N/V  and loose stools after eating fish for dinner that night. She  presented to ___ on ___ for further evaluation and she was  noted to have LFTs in the 700s with a normal Tbili and lipase of  ___. She was sent to the ___ ED for further evaluation. RUQ ultrasound  difficult to assess but showed a collapsed gallbladder and  recommended repeating.   Patient given: 1L IVF    On the floor, patient reports feeling well. She denies pain,  N/V/D. why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones (cholelithiasis) or sludge in the gallbladder, a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis, or pericholecystic fluid/wall thickening consistent with cholecystitis — any of which would point to a biliary/gallstone etiology unifying the markedly elevated transaminases (AST 441, ALT 550, ALP 192) and lipase (1089).

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the right upper
quadrant were obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The hepatic parenchyma appears within normal limits.The contour of the
liver is smooth. There is no focal liver mass.  The main portal vein is patent
with hepatopetal flow.  The hepatic veins are patent. There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CBD measures 6 mm,
which is at the upper limits of normal for a patient of this age.

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is collapsed, and difficult to evaluate.

PANCREAS:
The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

KIDNEYS:
Limited views of the right kidney demonstrate no hydronephrosis or
large mass.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

TECHNIQUE:
PA and lateral views of the chest.

FINDINGS:
There is a small amount of localized scarring at the right base. The lungs are
otherwise clear without a focal consolidation or edema. There is no pleural
effusion or pneumothorax. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is normal. Cervical
spine fusion hardware is partially imaged.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction; gallstone pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: The gallbladder could not be adequately evaluated on the prior ultrasound because it was collapsed, leaving open the question of cholelithiasis, cholecystitis, or a stone in the CBD as the unifying etiology for both the transaminitis and the markedly elevated lipase
why_trace.expected_finding: A distended, now-evaluable gallbladder with gallstones, pericholecystic fluid, wall thickening, or a CBD stone/dilation that would confirm biliary pathology (gallstone pancreatitis or cholecystitis) as the cause of the combined hepatocellular injury and lipase elevation

resolve.reason: The repeat single-organ gallbladder/liver ultrasound directly targets the collapsed-gallbladder inadequacy with sound reasoning about NPO-repletion, but it does not address the equally flagged pancreatic tail obscuration by bowel gas, leaving that component of the technically inadequate prior study unresolved.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The prior RUQ ultrasound explicitly stated the gallbladder was 'collapsed and difficult to evaluate' and recommended repeating it, and the clinical picture of postprandial RUQ/epigastric pain, markedly elevated transaminases, and lipase of 1089 makes biliary pathology (gallstone pancreatitis or choledocholithiasis) the leading unifying diagnosis that requires gallbladder visualization to confirm or exclude.

On reevaluation this am,  pt with improved pain and nausea. However, per ED report, pt  admitted as failed PO challenge, vomited after gingerale and  crackers with increased pain.   In the ED, she was given multiple doses of IV morphine (totaling  about 36mg), IV LR, Zofran, Tylenol and ceftriaxone. visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
Chest radiograph

TECHNIQUE:
Chest PA and lateral

FINDINGS:
There is pectus excavatum resulting in slight blurring of the right heart
border.  The lungs are clear without focal consolidation, edema, effusion, or
pneumothorax.  The heart is perhaps top-normal in size.  The mediastinum is
not widened.  The bones are diffusely demineralized.  Multilevel degenerative
changes are mild.  The imaged bowel gas pattern in the upper abdomen is
unremarkable.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction, hypercalcemia-induced pancreatitis, gallstone pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: Whether there is a biliary etiology (gallstones, choledocholithiasis, biliary ductal dilation, or cholecystitis) driving or complicating the presentation, and whether the pancreas itself shows sonographic abnormality
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder wall thickening, or common bile duct dilation suggesting a biliary/gallstone etiology; alternatively, pancreatic edema or peripancreatic fluid
grounding: History: '1 day of acutely epigastric band-like constant pain'
History: 'Pt was initially placed on the pancreas pathway in the ED'
History: 'h.o ETOH use'
Laboratory: 'White Blood Cells: 16.9 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)'
Laboratory: 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 14.35 K/uL (ref 1.6-6.1)'
Laboratory: 'Calcium, Total: 12.3 mg/dL (ref 8.4-10.3)'
Laboratory: 'Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L (ref 8.0-20.0)'
Physical examination: '+TTP epigastric area and periumbilical area, no guarding or rebound'
Laboratory: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 93.0 IU/L', 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.8 mg/dL', 'ALT: 27.0 IU/L' — liver enzymes normal but biliary obstruction not excluded

discordance.evidence: Calcium, Total: 12.3 mg/dL (ref 8.4-10.3) — markedly elevated; White Blood Cells: 16.9 K/uL with Absolute Neutrophil Count: 14.35 K/uL and Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L — these findings (hypercalcemia, elevated anion gap) are not explained by acute pancreatitis alone and raise concern for an underlying etiology (e.g., hypercalcemia-induced pancreatitis, malignancy, hyperparathyroidism) that changes management beyond the routine pancreas pathway.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The abdominal ultrasound can identify a biliary etiology for pancreatitis but cannot resolve the critical discordance — the marked hypercalcemia (12.3 mg/dL) with elevated anion gap demands investigation for hyperparathyroidism or malignancy (e.g., PTH/PTHrP levels, neck ultrasound, further labs), which this study does not address and the reasoning only briefly acknowledges without making it a primary diagnostic target.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The patient was placed on a pancreas pathway with epigastric band-like pain, ETOH history, leukocytosis (WBC 16.9), and notably elevated calcium (12.3 mg/dL), making biliary/gallstone pancreatitis a leading concern that ultrasound is the first-line modality to evaluate.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
Chest radiograph

TECHNIQUE:
Chest PA and lateral

FINDINGS:
There is pectus excavatum resulting in slight blurring of the right heart
border.  The lungs are clear without focal consolidation, edema, effusion, or
pneumothorax.  The heart is perhaps top-normal in size.  The mediastinum is
not widened.  The bones are diffusely demineralized.  Multilevel degenerative
changes are mild.  The imaged bowel gas pattern in the upper abdomen is
unremarkable.

EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The liver is diffusely echogenic.  There may be a small area of focal
fatty sparing near the gallbladder fossa.  The contour of the liver is smooth.
No focal liver mass. The main portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow.  No
ascites.

BILE DUCTS:
No intrahepatic biliary dilation. The common bile duct measures 5
mm.

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is not distended.  No evidence of stones or
gallbladder wall thickening.

PANCREAS:
The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
distal body and tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

SPLEEN:
Normal echogenicity, measuring 9.9 cm.

KIDNEYS:
The left kidney measures 11.2 cm. There is a 3.1-cm thin-walled
anechoic structure with through transmission in the left lower pole,
consistent with a cyst that has a single thin septation.  Limited views of
both kidneys show no hydronephrosis.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Hypercalcemia-induced pancreatitis or alternative etiology; also considering bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, or other intra-abdominal pathology given incomplete pancreatic visualization on ultrasound and markedly elevated calcium (12.3 mg/dL)
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound explicitly states that 'portions of the pancreatic distal body and tail [are] obscured by overlying bowel gas,' leaving the pancreas incompletely evaluated; the physician needs cross-sectional imaging to fully characterize the pancreas (edema, necrosis, peripancreatic fluid) and to identify any alternative or contributing intra-abdominal pathology
why_trace.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema, peripancreatic fat stranding, or fluid collections consistent with acute pancreatitis — or alternatively, an unexpected finding (e.g., bowel obstruction, mesenteric pathology, or a mass) that explains the clinical picture given the incomplete ultrasound and the notable hypercalcemia

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound incompletely visualized the pancreas due to bowel gas, the patient has a markedly elevated WBC (16.9 K/uL), significant leukocyturia/pyuria, hypercalcemia (12.3 mg/dL) as a potential pancreatitis trigger, failed PO challenge, and ongoing clinical concern — CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to fully characterize the pancreas, grade severity, and exclude alternative diagnoses.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; gallstone pancreatitis with biliary component; complex peripancreatic/pericholecystic collection
why_trace.information_gap: The outside abdominal ultrasound showed gallstones and a 'complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas' — the treating physician needs a higher-quality, dedicated hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution to characterize that collection, assess the common bile duct for dilation or stones, and evaluate the gallbladder wall for acute cholecystitis, given markedly elevated transaminases (AST 800, ALT 634), elevated lipase (2238), rising lactate (3.8→4.7), and the ERCP fellow already being consulted.
why_trace.expected_finding: Common bile duct dilation (suggesting choledocholithiasis driving the transaminase elevation and possibly the pancreatitis), pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening consistent with acute cholecystitis, or better characterization of the complex peripancreatic/pericholecystic collection seen on outside imaging.

History: 'Case discussed with ERCP fellow'

Labs: 'Lipase: 2238.0 IU/L'
History: 'lactate 3.8 > 4.7'
History: 'Limited bedside US showed gallstones'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 1.7 mg/dL'
Physical exam: 'moderate epigastric and upper abdominal tenderness RUQ > LUQ'

discordance.evidence: lactate 3.8 > 4.7; ABD US with gallstones and complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas; WBC 25 (outside) / 17.1 K/uL (current); AST 800.0 IU/L; ALT 634.0 IU/L; Anion Gap: 22.0 mEq/L — alongside 'soft, moderate epigastric and upper abdominal tenderness' and 'abd pain improving but still ~6 out of 10'

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The dedicated hepatobiliary ultrasound is directly aimed at characterizing the complex peri-pancreatic/gallbladder collection, assessing CBD dilation, and evaluating for cholecystitis — the exact discordant findings (rising lactate, markedly elevated transaminases, complex collection on outside imaging out of proportion to the soft exam) that the flag identifies as unexplained by gallstone pancreatitis alone.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The outside ultrasound identified a 'complex collection in the region of the gallbladder or head of pancreas' and gallstones, but was performed at an outside facility; a dedicated single-organ hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution is needed to characterize the CBD, gallbladder, and collection before ERCP planning proceeds.

EXAMINATION:
LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN):

TECHNIQUE:
Grey scale, color, and spectral Doppler ultrasound images of the
abdomen were obtained.

FINDINGS:


LIVER:
The liver is diffusely echogenic. The contour of the liver is smooth.
There is no focal liver mass. The main portal vein is patent with hepatopetal
flow.  No ascites is demonstrated in the left and right lower quadrants.

BILE DUCTS:
There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CBD is mildly
dilated and measures 7 mm.

GALLBLADDER:
There is cholelithiasis.  The posterior wall the gallbladder is
not well seen secondary to posterior shadowing from gallstones.  The anterior
wall does not appear thickened.  Inferior to the gallbladder is a
heterogeneous, predominantly hypoechoic region that does not demonstrate
internal vascularity and does not clearly demonstrate normal peristaltic
activity seen with bowel.

PANCREAS:
Overlying bowel gas obscures detailed evaluation of the pancreas.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis with gallstone pancreatitis; peripancreatic or pericholecystic fluid collection/abscess; pancreatic necrosis
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound identified a heterogeneous hypoechoic region inferior to the gallbladder of unclear origin (peripancreatic fluid collection vs. pericholecystic abscess vs. other mass), and the pancreas itself was not visualized due to overlying bowel gas — the physician cannot characterize the extent of pancreatic inflammation, necrosis, or ductal/biliary obstruction from US alone.
why_trace.expected_finding: CT is expected to delineate the pancreas fully — specifically to identify peripancreatic fluid collections, pancreatic necrosis, or hemorrhage consistent with severe/necrotizing pancreatitis — and to characterize the heterogeneous inferior gallbladder region (fluid collection, abscess, or mass), as well as assess for choledocholithiasis or biliary ductal dilation beyond the 7 mm CBD seen on US.

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate cross-sectional modality to directly resolve both the study inadequacy (pancreas not visualized on US due to bowel gas) and the clinical discordance (rising lactate with complex peripancreatic collection disproportionate to soft exam), and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets pancreatic necrosis, collection characterization, and biliary assessment — the precise concerns flagged.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >2000, markedly elevated transaminases, a rising lactate (3.8→4.7), leukocytosis, and an uncharacterized heterogeneous pericholecystic/peripancreatic collection on US with the pancreas itself not visualized, CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to stage severity of pancreatitis, identify necrosis, and define the ambiguous fluid collection.

baseline.patient_history: ___ male with history of GERD, hypertension, atrial  fibrillation, and alcohol abuse was admitted with abdominal  pain.   He reports that at approximately 8pm on the night prior to  admission, he deveoped abdominal pain. The pain persisted until  approximately 4am on the day of admission. The pain improved for  several hours, and he was able to sleep. Shortly afterwards  however his pain returned and steadily worsened, resulting in  his coming to the ED. He describes his abdominal pain as the  following: character is dull, onset was sudden, location is in  his epigastrium and radiating to both his right and left flank,  duration has been constant and worsening, and the pain worsens  with eating. Associated symptoms include nonbloody / nonbilious  vomiting, increased burping, and nausea. His last bowel movement  was on the morning of admission, and he describes it as being  normal, although perhaps a little pale. He otherwise denies any  change in his bowel movements. He reports that he had this pain  approximately 2 weeks ago, lasting 45 minutes, but improving  without any intervention.  Upon arrival in the ED, temp 98.8, HR 98, BP 234/50, RR 18, and  pulse ox 96% on room air. His exam was notable for being  uncomfortable appearing, tenderness to palpation in RUQ, guiac  positive yellow stool. Labs were notable for ALT 107, AST 140,  Alk Phos 150, and TB 5. ECG was unremarkable. CT Abd/Pelvis did  not reveal an acute intra-abdominal process. RUQ US revealed  fatty liver only. CXR was unremarkable. He received zofran 4mg  IV x 1, magic mouthwash, donnatol elixir, viscous lidocaine, and  morphine 4mg IV x 1. His blood pressure improved to . . . GI was  consulted in the ED and recommended consideration of HIDA or  MRCP. Prior to leaving the Emergency Department, he ate a ___  sandwich. resolve.reason: A repeat dedicated gallbladder/biliary ultrasound is a reasonable next step and directly targets the open biliary question, but given that the prior RUQ ultrasound was already non-diagnostic for the biliary system, repeating the same modality (even as a 'single organ' study) risks another inadequate result, whereas the GI-recommended HIDA or MRCP would more definitively resolve the biliary obstruction question — making this order only a partial answer to the flagged study inadequacy.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The combination of RUQ tenderness, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (direct bilirubin 2.2, total 5.0), markedly elevated GGT (1118), elevated transaminases, pale stool, and pain worsening with eating strongly points to a biliary etiology that a dedicated RUQ/gallbladder ultrasound is the first-line modality to evaluate.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
FINDINGS:
Examination of the abdomen is limited due to poor sonographic penetration. 
Within these limitations the liver appears heterogeneous and echogenic
consistent with fatty infiltration.  The gallbladder is unremarkable, without
stones, sludge, or wall thickening.  The portal vein is patent with
hepatopetal flow. The common bile duct measures 3 mm and is normal. No
intrahepatic biliary dilatation is present.

PA AND LATERAL VIEWS OF THE CHEST:
The heart size is normal.  There appears
to be calcified right hilar lymph nodes suggesting prior granulomatous
disease.  Calcified aortic knob is present.  The pulmonary vascularity is not
engorged.  The lungs are clear.  There is hyperinflation of lungs with
flattening of the diaphragms.  No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is seen. 
No acute osseous abnormalities are present. No free air under the diaphragms.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis; biliary pathology (e.g., common bile duct stone, cholangitis, or biliary stricture); less likely alcoholic hepatitis with biliary component; aortic pathology given markedly elevated BP on arrival
why_trace.information_gap: The prior CT abdomen/pelvis 'did not reveal an acute intra-abdominal process' but was likely performed without IV contrast, and the RUQ ultrasound was limited by poor sonographic penetration and showed only fatty liver — leaving the biliary tree (intrahepatic ducts, common bile duct, pancreatic head/duct) inadequately characterized despite a markedly cholestatic lab picture (TB 5.0, direct bili 2.2, ALP 150, GGT 1118) and elevated amylase (144).
why_trace.expected_finding: A dilated common bile duct, choledocholithiasis, pancreatic head mass or ductal dilation, or periductal/hepatic parenchymal abnormality that would explain the mixed hepatocellular-cholestatic pattern and elevated amylase — findings that the non-contrast or limited prior CT and the technically limited ultrasound could not exclude.
grounding: Labs: 'Gamma Glutamyltransferase: 1118.0 IU/L (ref 8.0-61.0)'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 5.0 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)' and 'Bilirubin, Direct: 2.2 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)'
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 150.0 IU/L (ref 40.0-130.0)'
Labs: 'Amylase: 144.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)'
Prior imaging 1: 'Examination of the abdomen is limited due to poor sonographic penetration'
History: 'CT Abd/Pelvis did not reveal an acute intra-abdominal process'
History: 'GI was consulted in the ED and recommended consideration of HIDA or MRCP'
History: 'pain is in his epigastrium and radiating to both his right and left flank'
History: 'alcohol abuse' and 'GERD'
Prior imaging 1: 'The common bile duct measures 3 mm and is normal. No intrahepatic biliary dilatation is present' — but limited by poor penetration

study_inadequacy.evidence: Examination of the abdomen is limited due to poor sonographic penetration.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen with contrast can visualize the biliary tree and pancreas and thus partially addresses the technically limited ultrasound, but it is inferior to MRCP or HIDA for detecting choledocholithiasis and biliary pathology — the specific concern flagged — and the reasoning, while clinically sound, does not acknowledge or justify why CT is preferred over the explicitly recommended MRCP/HIDA, leaving the most capable modality for the open biliary question unordered.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The combination of markedly elevated GGT (1118), elevated total and direct bilirubin (5.0/2.2), elevated ALP (150), elevated amylase (144), epigastric/RUQ pain radiating to flanks, and technically limited prior imaging creates a clear gap that a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen is well-positioned to address by evaluating the biliary tree, pancreas, and hepatic parenchyma with better resolution than the prior non-contrast or limited studies.

resolve.reason: MRCP evaluates the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis and cannot image the pulmonary vasculature, so it is mechanically incapable of resolving the flagged discordance — a massively elevated D-dimer with pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, tachycardia, and borderline hypotension in a patient with prior DVT strongly suggesting pulmonary embolism — and the physician's reasoning is entirely focused on gallstone pancreatitis without acknowledging this more pressing concern.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive next step to evaluate the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis given massively elevated lipase (7585), worsening transaminases (ALT 160, AST 223), elevated direct bilirubin (2.7), and elevated alkaline phosphatase (121), in the setting of known cholelithiasis but no CBD dilation or obstruction seen on prior CT/RUQ US — MRCP has superior sensitivity for CBD stones compared to ultrasound and avoids radiation/contrast.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAM:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast.

TECHNIQUE:
64-slice MDCT data were acquired from the abdomen and pelvis after
the administration of 100 cc of IV Optiray contrast.  Images displayed in
multiple planes.

FINDINGS:


ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST:
The visualized lung bases are free of nodules,
consolidations, or effusions.  There are no focal liver lesions.  There is no
intra- or extra-hepatic biliary dilatation.  The common bile duct measures 7
mm in maximum diameter (300b:18).  The gallbladder is mildly distended, but
the wall is thin and there are no stones visualized.  The pancreas appears
normal with no pancreatic duct dilatation.  There is no evidence of
peripancreatic fat stranding, pancreatic necrosis, or abscess.  The spleen and
adrenal glands are normal.  The kidneys enhance and excrete contrast
symmetrically.  Patient is status post gastric bypass with staple lines
visible at proximal and distal anastomotic sites.  There is no dilation of
stomach, small or large bowel to indicate obstruction.  Post-surgical anatomy
is incompletely visualized due to the absence of oral contrast.

There is a fat-containing periumbilical ventral abdominal wall hernia (2:50)
with a minimal amount of surrounding stranding.  This is most likely
incisional in origin.  The diameter of the defect is 20 mm.  There is no bowel
within the defect.

PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:
The remainder of the small and large bowel are normal. 
There is a normal appendix.  There is normal uterus and ovaries.  There is no
free pelvic fluid.  The bladder is normal.  There is no pelvic or inguinal
lymphadenopathy.

CT ANGIOGRAM:
The celiac axis, SMA, and ___ are patent.  There are no
concerning bowel loops for ischemia.

BONE WINDOWS:
There are no fractures or concerning lytic or sclerotic
lesions.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction; gallbladder pathology (biliary sludge, microlithiasis, acalculous cholecystitis) as the etiology driving pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: The CT showed a mildly distended gallbladder without stones and a borderline common bile duct (7 mm), but CT is insensitive for gallstones (especially small stones/sludge) and cannot reliably assess the gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, or sonographic Murphy's sign — the physician needs to know whether biliary pathology (cholelithiasis, sludge, or early cholecystitis) is the underlying cause of this acute pancreatitis episode.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary sludge, or pericholecystic fluid/wall thickening on ultrasound that would identify a biliary etiology for the markedly elevated lipase; alternatively, a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis not visible on CT.

resolve.reason: The ultrasound directly targets the discordance flag by seeking a biliary explanation for the markedly elevated transaminases and borderline CBD, but neither the order nor its reasoning addresses the study_inadequacy flag — the incomplete post-surgical anatomy from absent oral contrast in a gastric-bypass patient remains entirely unaddressed.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase 1602, markedly elevated transaminases (AST 749, ALT 454), mildly elevated total bilirubin (2.1), and a borderline CBD on CT (7 mm) in a patient with a mildly distended gallbladder, ultrasound is the standard first-line modality to evaluate for biliary etiology of pancreatitis, as it is far more sensitive than CT for gallstones and sludge.

resolve.reason: MRCP is the optimal modality to directly visualize the biliary tree and detect choledocholithiasis, resolving both the incomplete ultrasound/CT evaluation of the gallbladder and biliary anatomy and the discordance between markedly elevated transaminases and a normal-appearing pancreas on CT, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets both the study inadequacy (limited Murphy's sign, no oral contrast on CT) and the biochemical discordance (AST 749, ALT 454 unexplained by pancreatitis alone).

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The combination of markedly elevated lipase (1602), significantly elevated AST/ALT (749/454), elevated total bilirubin (2.1), elevated alkaline phosphatase (179), gallstones on ultrasound, and a CBD measuring 7 mm on CT creates a strong clinical picture of possible choledocholithiasis causing gallstone pancreatitis, and MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for detecting CBD stones that CT and ultrasound have not excluded.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar T1 and T2 weighted images were obtained with a 1.5
Tesla magnet including dynamic 3D images obtained prior to, during and after
the uneventful IV administration of 8 cc of Gadavist. 1 cc of Gadavist mixed
with 50 cc of water was administered orally.

FINDINGS:
The study is limited by motion.  Additional contrast-enhanced sequences could
not be obtained due to the patient's claustrophobia.

The visualized lung bases demonstrates subsegmental atelectasis at the left
lung base. No pericardial or pleural effusion.

The liver demonstrates normal signal intensity on the in and out of phase
images with no evidence of steatosis. There is a 8 x 9 mm T2 hyperintense
lesion in segment 7 which is not well seen on the post-contrast sequence and
may represent a cyst or hemangioma.

There is moderate intrahepatic biliary tree dilatation with irregularity of
the ducts which is predominantly seen centrally and in the left hepatic lobe. 
The common hepatic duct is slightly prominent measuring 10 mm and the common
bile duct measures 9 mm. No obstructing CBD stone identified.  No strictures
seen.  There is mild periportal edema.

The gallbladder demonstrates circumferential wall thickening up to 6 mm
(series 8, image 27) and is distended with a transverse diameter of 5 cm. 
Layering sludge is noted within the gallbladder but no gallstones are
identified.  There is a focal area of T2 hyperintensity in the fundus which
may represent focal adenomyomatosis (series 5, image 36) .

The spleen measures 11.2 cm and is unremarkable. Two 3 mm T2 hyperintense
cystic lesion are noted in the pancreatic body and tail which most likely
represents side-branch IPMNs (series 5, image 27). The pancreas demonstrates
normal signal intensity without any duct dilatation or solid mass.

Bilateral simple cortical renal cysts are noted, the largest one arising from
the upper pole of the left kidney measuring 8 mm (series 5, image 20).

Slightly prominent portocaval lymph node measuring 11 x 15.5 mm. There is a
trace amount of free fluid in the right paracolic gutter.

There is a 11 x 13 mm T2 hyperintense lesion noted in the right hemivertebral
body of L4 most likely in keeping with an hemangioma.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction / cholangiopathy (primary sclerosing cholangitis or cholangitis given intrahepatic biliary irregularity and dilatation on MRCP), acalculous cholecystitis
why_trace.information_gap: The MRCP was motion-limited and could not complete all contrast sequences; it showed gallbladder wall thickening (up to 6 mm), distension (5 cm transverse diameter), layering sludge, and intrahepatic biliary dilatation with ductal irregularity — but could not definitively characterize the gallbladder or confirm/exclude acute cholecystitis or acalculous cholecystitis in real time. The physician needs dynamic, bedside assessment of the gallbladder and biliary tree to determine whether acute cholecystitis (acalculous or otherwise) is present and driving the clinical picture.
why_trace.expected_finding: Sonographic Murphy's sign, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder wall thickening/edema, or increased wall vascularity on Doppler confirming acute (acalculous) cholecystitis; alternatively, confirmation of biliary ductal dilatation without a new obstructing stone to help narrow the biliary vs. pancreatic etiology of the markedly elevated lipase and transaminases.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ultrasound can resolve the gallbladder/biliary component of the inadequate MRCP and partially address the discordance by identifying acute cholecystitis, but it cannot evaluate the pancreas for necrotizing pancreatitis — the most critical open question given a lipase of 8433 with prior hemodynamic instability, elevated lactate, and leukocytosis — which requires contrast-enhanced CT or repeat MRI.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The MRCP was technically limited by motion and claustrophobia, leaving the gallbladder and biliary tree incompletely characterized; a targeted bedside ultrasound directly addresses the unresolved question of acute cholecystitis given the gallbladder wall thickening, distension, sludge, leukocytosis (WBC 24.1), elevated transaminases, and prior episode of hemodynamic instability.

He denies any other associated symptoms and denies any ameliorating or exacerbating factors (including eating) but has essentially had persistent pain from the past 36 hours. He has had intermittent waves of worsening pain, cannot qualify nature, so bad that it prevents him from sleeping. why_trace.other_hypothesis: mesenteric ischemia (given extensive CAD/vascular history), biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis, bowel obstruction, colitis
why_trace.information_gap: The anatomic source and severity of the intra-abdominal pathology driving 36 hours of persistent lower abdominal/epigastric pain remains uncharacterized — labs show markedly elevated lipase (189, >3x ULN), leukocytosis (WBC 14.8 with 81% neutrophils), and elevated total bilirubin (2.7), but no imaging of the abdomen has yet been performed to identify the organ involved, extent of inflammation, or any vascular compromise.
why_trace.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema/peripancreatic stranding consistent with acute pancreatitis, or alternatively colonic wall thickening suggesting diverticulitis/colitis, or biliary ductal dilation/choledocholithiasis; given the patient's severe atherosclerotic burden, the physician may also be watching for bowel wall ischemia or pneumatosis
grounding: Lipase: 189.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
White Blood Cells: 14.8 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)

History: 'lower abdominal pain, band involving b/l LQ without radiation to back, legs, or chest... persistent pain from the past 36 hours... intermittent waves of worsening pain... so bad that it prevents him from sleeping'

discordance.evidence: "White Blood Cells: 14.8 K/uL" / "Absolute Neutrophil Count: 11.99 K/uL" paired with "soft, TTP at RUQ and epigastrium, without rebounding or guarding, nondistended with normal active bowel sounds" — a markedly elevated WBC with left shift in the setting of a benign abdominal exam; additionally "Lipase: 189.0 IU/L" with pain localized to bilateral lower quadrants and RUQ/epigastric tenderness rather than the epigastric radiation pattern typical of pancreatitis, and "Bilirubin, Total: 2.7 mg/dL" with normal AST/ALT/ALP, raising concern for an obstructive or hemolytic process not explained by the leading diagnosis

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is capable of adjudicating the discordant lab-exam constellation by identifying the anatomic source of leukocytosis, elevated lipase, and hyperbilirubinemia, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets each discordant element — pancreatitis, biliary pathology, colitis, and mesenteric ischemia — rather than pursuing a single pre-committed diagnosis.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Persistent 36-hour abdominal pain with a lipase >3x ULN, leukocytosis with left shift, elevated bilirubin, and no prior abdominal imaging creates a clear anatomic information gap that CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast directly addresses while also evaluating for vascular/ischemic pathology given the patient's extensive CAD and CABG history.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAMINATION:
CHEST (PA AND LAT):

TECHNIQUE:
Chest PA and lateral

FINDINGS:
There is break in the inferior-most sternal wire, unchanged compared to ___. 
Remaining median sternotomy wires are aligned and intact.

There are multiple linear areas of opacity, likely scarring and/or
atelectasis.  No focal consolidation concerning for pneumonia. 
Cardiomediastinal silhouette is unchanged.  No pleural effusion is seen.

EXAMINATION:
CT ABD AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST:

TECHNIQUE:
Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen following intravenous contrast administration with split
bolus technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:
Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 7.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 36.1 mGy (Body) DLP =
18.1 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 6.6 s, 51.6 cm; CTDIvol = 26.1 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,347.6 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,366 mGy-cm.

FINDINGS:


LOWER CHEST:
There is a 10 mm subpleural left lower lobe nodule (02:10).  No
pleural effusion.No pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:


HEPATOBILIARY:
The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or
extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder contains small stones or
sludge, but is otherwise unremarkable.

PANCREAS:
There is mild fat stranding around the pancreatic head and uncinate
process concerning for ____.  The pancreatic parenchyma enhances
homogeneously.  There is no evidence of associated hypoenhancement.  SMA and
SMV appear patent without evidence of pseudoaneurysm formation.  No
peripancreatic fluid collections.

SPLEEN:
The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:
The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY:
The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
No hydronephrosis.  Simple cysts measure up to 8.3 cm in the upper pole of the
left kidney and up to 1.8 cm in the interpolar region of the right kidney. 
There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL:
The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout. There is extensive
sigmoid diverticulosis.  There is also diverticulosis of the remainder of the
colon.  No evidence of diverticulitis.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS:
Prostate and seminal vesicles are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR:
There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Extensive atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES:
There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES:
The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction (common bile duct stone causing both elevated lipase and elevated total bilirubin)
why_trace.information_gap: The CT showed fat stranding around the pancreatic head/uncinate process consistent with pancreatitis but did not characterize the biliary ductal system (no biliary dilatation seen on CT, yet total bilirubin is elevated at 2.7 mg/dL and lipase is markedly elevated at 189 IU/L); the physician needs to determine whether a common bile duct stone or biliary obstruction is the underlying etiology driving both findings, which CT with split-bolus contrast is less sensitive for than MRCP.
why_trace.expected_finding: A common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) or biliary ductal dilatation not visible on CT, which would explain the elevated total bilirubin (2.7 mg/dL) in the context of peripancreatic fat stranding and markedly elevated lipase, confirming gallstone pancreatitis as the etiology

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is the optimal modality to interrogate the biliary ductal system for choledocholithiasis and ductal dilatation, directly reconciling the discordance between lower-abdominal pain history and the pancreatobiliary findings (peripancreatic fat stranding, lipase 3× ULN, bilirubin 2.7 mg/dL), and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets this anatomic-laboratory conflict.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis when CT demonstrates peripancreatic inflammation and labs show both elevated lipase (189 IU/L, >3x ULN) and elevated total bilirubin (2.7 mg/dL) without CT evidence of biliary dilatation, as MRCP has superior sensitivity for CBD stones compared to contrast CT.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis; hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: The etiology of the confirmed biochemical pancreatitis (lipase 1064) is unknown — specifically whether gallstones or biliary sludge are present, and whether the biliary tree is dilated, which would point to a gallstone/obstructive cause requiring urgent intervention versus a non-biliary cause (e.g., hypertriglyceridemia, which is already documented at 208 mg/dL)
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder wall thickening, or common bile duct dilation suggesting cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis as the precipitant of pancreatitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder shifting suspicion toward hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis

resolve.reason: The ultrasound is appropriately ordered to clarify pancreatitis etiology, but the physician's reasoning is entirely focused on identifying a biliary source and does not acknowledge or address the flagged discordance — the elevated lactate of 2.5 mmol/L suggesting possible occult hypoperfusion that ultrasound cannot resolve.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >17× the upper limit of normal confirming acute pancreatitis, RUQ/epigastric tenderness, and no prior imaging, a right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the standard first-line imaging to identify a biliary etiology — the most common and immediately actionable cause — and is directly supported by the clinical and laboratory findings visible in the workup.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
PA and lateral radiographs of the chest.

FINDINGS:
The lungs appear clear without focal consolidation, pleural
effusion, or pneumothorax aside from minimal retrocardiac atelectasis.  Ovoid
opacity over the right lower hemithorax with surgical clip is compatible with
the patient's history of right mastopexy.  The heart is normal in size, normal
cardiomediastinal contours.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis as the precipitating cause of pancreatitis; also ovarian/gynecologic pathology given bilateral lower quadrant tenderness
why_trace.information_gap: The lipase is markedly elevated at 734 IU/L (>12× upper limit of normal) confirming pancreatitis, but the etiology is unknown — specifically, whether there is gallstone disease (cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis) driving the pancreatitis, or biliary ductal dilation, which would change management urgently; the RUQ ultrasound done in the ED was documented as 'WNL' but the order here is for a dedicated liver/gallbladder single-organ study, suggesting the prior study was felt to be incomplete or inadequate for full biliary evaluation.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary sludge, common bile duct dilation, or pericholecystic fluid/wall thickening that would identify a biliary etiology for the acute pancreatitis
grounding: Labs: Lipase: 734.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Labs: Amylase: 338.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)
Labs: Triglycerides: 48.0 mg/dL (ref 0.0-149.0)
HPI: 'RUQ US - WNL' (prior ED imaging)
HPI: 'bilateral lower quadrant tenderness along with a HA'
HPI: 'She describes a stabbing pain which is improved with pain medications'
HPI: 'she awoke with stomach and back pain'
Labs: ALT: 15.0 IU/L, AST: 25.0 IU/L, Alkaline Phosphatase: 95.0 IU/L, Bilirubin Total: 0.3 mg/dL (liver enzymes not significantly elevated, but biliary obstruction can precede enzyme rise)

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: no trigger detected — step-2 skipped

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With a lipase of 734 IU/L confirming acute pancreatitis and triglycerides normal at 48 mg/dL (ruling out hypertriglyceridemia as cause), identifying a biliary etiology via dedicated gallbladder/liver ultrasound is the standard next step to guide management (e.g., urgent ERCP or cholecystectomy planning).

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
MDCT axial images of the abdomen and pelvis after oral and Optiray
IV contrast with multiplanar reformats.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITH IV CONTRAST:
There is mild dependent atelectasis at
the lung bases.  Mild periportal edema is noted of the liver, which is a
nonspecific finding but can be seen with fluid resuscitation.  Patient is
status post cholecystectomy.  Spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands are
unremarkable.  There are a few tiny hypodensities of the kidneys, too small to
characterize.  Kidneys are otherwise normal and enhance and excrete contrast
symmetrically.  Ureters are of normal caliber and opacify well.  There is no
free intraperitoneal gas or fluid.  Nasogastric tube terminates in the
stomach.  Small and large bowel are noted to be diffusely fluid-filled.  There
is no inflammatory change or wall thickening of large or small bowel.  The
appendix is normal.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:
The uterus, adnexa are unremarkable.  A
Foley catheter is present within the decompressed urinary bladder.  Rectum is
unremarkable.  There is no free pelvic fluid.

BONE WINDOWS:
Patient is status post ORIF of proximal right femur.  No
concerning lesions are seen.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis, acalculous cholecystitis (given critical illness and prior cholecystectomy status — actually post-cholecystectomy so gallbladder absent; therefore biliary ductal pathology, hepatic parenchymal disease, or mesenteric ischemia/infectious enterocolitis in immunocompromised host
why_trace.information_gap: The CT abdomen showed elevated amylase/lipase and diffusely fluid-filled bowel without a clear structural cause; the biliary tree and hepatic vasculature were not fully characterized on CT, and the physician needs to assess for biliary ductal dilation, hepatic parenchymal abnormality, or portal/hepatic vascular compromise that could explain the elevated LDH, elevated amylase/lipase, periportal edema, and septic shock in this immunocompromised patient.
why_trace.expected_finding: Biliary ductal dilation (choledocholithiasis or biliary obstruction), hepatic parenchymal abnormality, or portal/hepatic venous flow abnormality; alternatively, evidence of acalculous cholecystitis remnant duct pathology or hepatic abscess in the setting of AIDS
grounding: Prior imaging 1: 'Mild periportal edema is noted of the liver, which is a nonspecific finding but can be seen with fluid resuscitation'
Prior imaging 1: 'Patient is status post cholecystectomy'
Laboratory tests: 'Lipase: 146.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Amylase: 246.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Lactate Dehydrogenase (LD): 268.0 IU/L (ref 94.0-250.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Lactate: 4.0 mmol/L (ref 0.5-2.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 122.0 IU/L (ref 39.0-117.0)'
History: 'HIV/AIDS (last CD4 ___ ED record), PCP pneumonia, HCV'

discordance.evidence: Vitals/hemodynamics: 'Hypotensive, given 5L IVF and started on Levophed. RIJ cvl placed. Pt was dyspneic, intubated.' Levophed@0.18 with lactate 4.0 mmol/L and bands 28%, anion gap 24, bicarbonate 15 — yet CT abdomen shows 'There is no inflammatory change or wall thickening of large or small bowel... no free intraperitoneal gas or fluid... appendix is normal' with only 'diffusely fluid-filled' bowel and a 'soft min dist, tender b/l LQ, no peritonitis' exam.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The ultrasound can interrogate the hepatobiliary system and may identify a source of septic shock (e.g., biliary obstruction, hepatic abscess, portal vein thrombosis), but the reasoning is narrowly focused on hepatobiliary pathology and does not directly address the core discordance — that the patient's severity of septic shock (vasopressor-dependent, lactate 4.0, bands 28%, AG acidosis) is unexplained by the benign CT abdomen, leaving non-abdominal or non-hepatobiliary sources of shock (e.g., cardiac, pulmonary, mesenteric ischemia) unaddressed.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT already noted periportal edema and the patient is post-cholecystectomy, so a liver/biliary ultrasound is reasonable to evaluate the biliary tree and hepatic vasculature for ductal dilation or vascular compromise not fully characterized on CT, though the CT did not explicitly flag biliary pathology and the dominant picture is septic shock with enterocolitis in an AIDS patient, making this a somewhat broad search rather than a tightly targeted order.

liver demonstrates normal echogenicity, without focal lesion.  The portal vein

visualized pancreas is unremarkable.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP can interrogate the biliary tree and might identify a correctable biliary source, but the dominant alarm is that the degree of septic shock (vasopressors, lactate 4.0, AG 24, 28% bands) is grossly unexplained by any abdominal finding so far, and MRCP addresses only one narrow biliary hypothesis while leaving the broader unexplained source of life-threatening sepsis — which could be pulmonary, cardiac, urologic, or another occult focus — unaddressed.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: Amylase 246 and lipase 146 are both elevated above normal, CT showed no pancreatic or biliary pathology but is insensitive for choledocholithiasis and ductal strictures, and ultrasound showed only a 4 mm CBD without stones — MRCP is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary tree and pancreatic duct non-invasively, especially given the HIV/AIDS background raising concern for AIDS cholangiopathy.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
EXAM:
Chest frontal and lateral views.

FINDINGS:
Frontal and lateral views of the chest were obtained.  There are
low lung volumes, which accentuate the bronchovascular markings at the lung
bases.  Given this, no focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax
is seen.  Cardiac and mediastinal silhouettes are unremarkable.  No evidence
of free air is seen beneath the diaphragms.

TECHNIQUE:
Contiguous axial images were obtained through the abdomen with the
administration of oral and IV contrast.  Multiplanar reformats were generated
and reviewed.

FINDINGS:


CT OF THE ABDOMEN:
The visualized lung bases are clear with no focal
consolidation or pleural effusion.  The visualized heart and pericardium are
unremarkable.  

The liver appears low in attenuation consistent with fatty infiltration with
some areas of fat sparing adjacent to gallbladder fossa.  The gallbladder,
spleen and bilateral adrenal glands appear unremarkable.  Both kidneys enhance
and excrete contrast symmetrically without evidence of hydronephrosis or renal
calculi.

There is swelling of the pancreatic head with slight decrease in attenuation
of the pancreatic head, suggesting edema and extensive stranding surrounding
the pancreas, with inflammatory change and fluid tracking along the second and
third parts of the duodenum, around the porta hepatis and down along the
anterior pararenal space as well as posterior to the ascending colon.  There
is no evidence of free air.  The common bile duct shows mild periductal
enhancement at the level of the head of the pancreas.  No calcified gallstone
is noted.  There is no intrahepatic bile duct dilation.  

The abdominal aorta appears unremarkable.  The portal vein, splenic vein, and
SMV appear patent. An area of soft tissue identical to the prior examination
in the portacaval space measures 20 x 9 mm and is unchanged in size compared
to the most recent prior examination and most likely represents a portacaval
lymph node.  A calcified structure anterior to the esophagus (2:8) is
unchanged from the prior examination may represent a calcified
node.Intra-abdominal loops of large bowel appear grossly unremarkable.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
Four consecutive thoracic vertebral bodies show presence
of Schmorl's nodes.  Slight anterior wedging of L1 and L2 are noted.  Mild
kyphosis is noted. Overall, findings may indicate prior Sc___'s disease.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction / choledocholithiasis; pancreatic ductal pathology (e.g., disruption, stricture, or underlying mass driving pancreatitis)
why_trace.information_gap: The CT shows pancreatic head edema and extensive peripancreatic stranding consistent with pancreatitis rather than isolated duodenitis, but the etiology and the status of the pancreatic duct and biliary tree (common bile duct, ampulla, presence of choledocholithiasis) remain undefined — information that CT with contrast cannot reliably provide.
why_trace.expected_finding: MRCP is expected to reveal whether there is choledocholithiasis, common bile duct dilation, pancreatic duct dilation or disruption, or an ampullary lesion that could explain the pancreatic head edema and periductal enhancement seen on CT.
grounding: CT Abdomen: 'swelling of the pancreatic head with slight decrease in attenuation of the pancreatic head, suggesting edema and extensive stranding surrounding the pancreas'
CT Abdomen: 'The common bile duct shows mild periductal enhancement at the level of the head of the pancreas'
CT Abdomen: 'No calcified gallstone is noted. There is no intrahepatic bile duct dilation.'
CT Abdomen: 'inflammatory change and fluid tracking along the second and third parts of the duodenum, around the porta hepatis and down along the anterior pararenal space'
Labs: 'Lipase: 115.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — elevated lipase consistent with pancreatic inflammation
Labs: 'C-Reactive Protein: 244.8 mg/L (ref 0.0-5.0)' — markedly elevated inflammatory marker
History: 'CT at OSH consistent inflammation of the duodenum at D2/D3 with associated stranding and free fluid' — original read as duodenitis, now CT here reframes as pancreatic head pathology

discordance.evidence: CT abdomen: 'swelling of the pancreatic head with slight decrease in attenuation of the pancreatic head, suggesting edema and extensive stranding surrounding the pancreas, with inflammatory change and fluid tracking along the second and third parts of the duodenum, around the porta hepatis and down along the anterior pararenal space as well as posterior to the ascending colon' — consistent with acute pancreatitis, not isolated duodenitis as the working diagnosis states; additionally, Lipase: 115.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0); CRP: 244.8 mg/L (ref 0.0-5.0); ESR: 97.0 mm/hr (ref 0.0-15.0)

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is capable of adjudicating the CT-vs-working-diagnosis discordance by directly evaluating the pancreatic duct and biliary tree for choledocholithiasis, ductal dilation, or ampullary pathology, and the physician's reasoning explicitly acknowledges the CT findings of acute pancreatitis, rejects the isolated duodenitis diagnosis, and orders MRCP to define etiology and guide intervention.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT abdomen demonstrates pancreatic head swelling with edema, extensive peripancreatic stranding, periductal enhancement of the common bile duct at the pancreatic head, and no calcified gallstones — findings that strongly suggest pancreatitis of unclear etiology and warrant non-invasive biliary/ductal imaging to guide management (e.g., need for ERCP).

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
MDCT axial images were obtained from the lung bases to the lower
abdomen without IV or oral contrast.  Coronal and sagittal reformations were
obtained.

CT OF THE ABDOMEN WITHOUT IV CONTRAST:
Visualized lung bases are clear. There
is no pleural effusion.  Visualized heart and pericardium are unremarkable.

Evaluation of the abdomen is markedly limited due to retained contrast from a
small bowel follow-through performed on the same day at 10 a.m., creating
dense streak artifact.  However, there is no evidence for free intraperitoneal
air.  Limited views of the liver, gallbladder, and spleen appear unremarkable.
Assessment of the posterior duodenal wall is limited due to the retained
contrast.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion is identified.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis, biliary pancreatitis, hepatic pathology
why_trace.information_gap: The CT abdomen was severely limited by retained barium contrast and could not adequately evaluate the biliary tree, gallbladder, or pancreatic head; the physician needs real-time soft-tissue imaging of these structures to determine whether a biliary etiology (cholelithiasis, choledocholithiasis, cholecystitis) is driving the markedly elevated lipase and abnormal liver enzymes.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder wall thickening (cholecystitis), or a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis/biliary obstruction as a precipitant of pancreatitis; alternatively, pancreatic edema or peripancreatic fluid if the pancreatic head is visible.
grounding: Labs: Lipase: 600.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Labs: Alkaline Phosphatase: 204.0 IU/L (ref 39.0-117.0)

resolve.reason: Ultrasound can evaluate the gallbladder and biliary tree but cannot resolve the primary flagged situation — the non-diagnostic posterior duodenal wall due to barium artifact — as ultrasound is poorly suited to assess the duodenum for perforation or ulceration, leaving that critical question unanswered.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The CT was explicitly limited by barium artifact and could not evaluate the posterior duodenum, gallbladder, or biliary tree, while the labs show lipase 10× normal, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and elevated ALT, making biliary ultrasound the logical next step to assess for a biliary cause of pancreatitis.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Right upper quadrant ultrasound.

FINDINGS:
The liver appears grossly normal without focal liver lesion.  No
intra- or extra-hepatic biliary dilation is seen.  The CBD measures 5 mm.  The
portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow.  The gallbladder contains
multiple shadowing stones; however, appears nondistended without wall
thickening or evidence of pericholecystic fluid.  Limited views of the
pancreas show no focal lesion.  No free fluid is seen.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone causing biliary obstruction and gallstone pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: Whether a stone is impacted in or has recently passed through the common bile duct, which would explain the gallstone-triggered pancreatitis and guide the need for urgent ERCP or cholecystectomy timing
why_trace.expected_finding: A choledocholithiasis (stone in the common bile duct) or biliary sludge/microlithiasis not visualized on ultrasound, or alternatively a normal CBD confirming the stone has passed and no intervention beyond supportive care is immediately needed
grounding: HPI: 'She was found to have gallstones in ___ and was told to follow-up with a surgeon but she chose to pursue conservative management'
HPI: 'return of intense epigastric pain last night at 8pm... vomited several times... not able to tolerate PO... fevers/chills'
Labs: 'Lipase: 521.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)'
Labs: 'Amylase: 246.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 12.6 K/uL' with 'Neutrophils: 90.7%'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.9 mg/dL' — normal, but does not exclude passed or intermittently obstructing stone
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 79.0 IU/L' and 'ALT: 18.0 IU/L' — liver enzymes not markedly elevated but do not exclude choledocholithiasis
Prior imaging: 'The gallbladder contains multiple shadowing stones... The CBD measures 5 mm... Limited views of the pancreas show no focal lesion'

study_inadequacy.evidence: Limited views of the pancreas show no focal lesion.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP provides dedicated cross-sectional imaging of the pancreas and biliary tree, directly resolving the study_inadequacy by replacing the limited ultrasound views with a modality capable of fully visualizing the pancreatic parenchyma and ductal anatomy, and the physician's reasoning explicitly acknowledges the inadequate pancreatic visualization on ultrasound as part of the rationale for ordering MRCP.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound confirmed gallstones but provided only limited pancreatic views and a CBD of 5 mm without definitive stone visualization, leaving choledocholithiasis as the unresolved etiology of a markedly elevated lipase (521 IU/L) in a patient with known gallstones — MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard to evaluate the biliary tree in this setting.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Right upper quadrant ultrasound.

FINDINGS:
The liver appears grossly normal without focal liver lesion.  No
intra- or extra-hepatic biliary dilation is seen.  The CBD measures 5 mm.  The
portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow.  The gallbladder contains
multiple shadowing stones; however, appears nondistended without wall
thickening or evidence of pericholecystic fluid.  Limited views of the
pancreas show no focal lesion.  No free fluid is seen.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar T1- and T2-weighted images were acquired on a 1.5
Tesla magnet including dynamic 3D imaging obtained prior to, during and after
the uneventful intravenous administration of 0.1 mmol/kg (15 mL) of
gadolinium-DTPA.

FINDINGS:
There is marked peripancreatic edema and induration of the peripancreatic fat,
consistent with the patient's clinical diagnosis of ____. The pancreas
appears enlarged and edematous with relatively reduced signal intensity on
pre-contrast T1-weighted images, but enhances homogenously without evidence of
pancreatic necrosis.  The pancreatic duct is not dilated.  Inflammatory
stranding and fluid extends within the mesenteric root, to the paracolic
gutters and along the anterior perirenal fascia bilaterally.   No evidence of
pseudocyst formation. There is edema of the wall of the hepatic flexure,
stomach and duodenum consistent with reactive changes secondary to the upper
abdominal inflammatory process.  The gallbladder is distended, but not thick
walled.  Gallstones seen within the neck of the gallbladder, no
choledocholithiasis.  The common bile duct is not dilated, measuring 4 mm. 
There is loss of signal within the hepatic parenchyma on out-of-phase compared
to in-phase T1-weighted images, consistent with hepatic steatosis.  There is a
poorly defined region of hypoenhancement adjacent to the gallbladder (12:26),
which is unchanged in appearance compared to the CT ___.  This
corresponds to an area of focal fatty change based on the out-of-phase
T1-weighted images (3A:9).  A peripheral segment VI area of hypoenhancement
(12:50) is only seen on one phase post-contrast and is isointense to liver
parenchyma on the remainder of the series.  This likely represents a focal
perfusional abnormality, as no T2 correlate is seen.  No intrahepatic duct
dilatation.  The portal vein and superior mesenteric veins are slightly
narrowed by the peripancreatic/perivascular edema, but patent.  The hepatic
veins are patent.  The hepatic arterial anatomy is conventional.  The splenic
vein is patent.  The spleen is not enlarged.  Both adrenal glands and both
kidneys are unremarkable except to note a cyst in the interpolar region of the
left kidney, measuring 6 mm (13:50).  A tiny cyst noted in the upper pole of
the right kidney.  No upper abdominal lymphadenopathy.   The visualized bone
marrow is unremarkable.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: pancreatic necrosis, vascular complication (splenic/portal vein thrombosis), or developing peripancreatic fluid collection/abscess not yet visible on MRCP
why_trace.information_gap: The MRCP confirms acute pancreatitis with extensive peripancreatic inflammation but was performed at an earlier time point; the physician needs to assess whether the disease has progressed — specifically whether pancreatic necrosis, hemorrhage, vascular thrombosis, or an organized fluid collection has developed since the MRCP, and to better characterize the extent of retroperitoneal spread.
why_trace.expected_finding: Evidence of interval development of pancreatic necrosis (non-enhancing pancreatic parenchyma on contrast phases), new or enlarging peripancreatic fluid collections, hemorrhagic transformation, or splenic/portal/SMV thrombosis given the noted narrowing of portal and superior mesenteric veins on MRCP.
grounding: MRCP FINDINGS: 'marked peripancreatic edema and induration of the peripancreatic fat, consistent with the patient's clinical diagnosis'
MRCP FINDINGS: 'enhances homogenously without evidence of pancreatic necrosis'
MRCP FINDINGS: 'portal vein and superior mesenteric veins are slightly narrowed by the peripancreatic/perivascular edema, but patent'
MRCP FINDINGS: 'Inflammatory stranding and fluid extends within the mesenteric root, to the paracolic gutters and along the anterior perirenal fascia bilaterally'
Labs: Lipase 521.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)
Labs: WBC 12.6 K/uL with Neutrophils 90.7%
History: 'pain worsened', 'vomited several times', 'not able to tolerate PO', 'fevers/chills'
Vitals: HR 105 (tachycardia), Temp 96.8 (low-normal, possible early sepsis pattern)

study_inadequacy.evidence: "Limited views of the pancreas show no focal lesion."

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is capable of fully characterizing the pancreas where ultrasound was non-diagnostic, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets interval pancreatic necrosis, vascular thrombosis, and disease progression — directly resolving the open pancreatic question left by the limited ultrasound.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: CT with contrast is a reasonable severity-staging tool for acute pancreatitis (CT Severity Index / Balthazar scoring) and can detect necrosis and vascular complications, but the MRCP already provided detailed soft-tissue characterization and showed no necrosis; the added radiation and contrast exposure is justified only if clinical deterioration or a new complication is suspected, which is not explicitly documented here.

visible_prior_imaging.exam: CT ABD & PELVIS W & W/O CONTRAST, ADDL SECTIONS
visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
MDCT axial images were acquired from the lung bases through the
lesser trochanters following administration of oral contrast only. 
Subsequently, rapid axial images were acquired through the abdomen and pelvis
during infusion of 130 cc of intravenous Optiray contrast material. 
Multiplanar reformations were performed.

ABDOMEN CT:
There are new small bilateral pleural effusions.  Minimal
bibasilar atelectasis is greater on the left.  The lung bases are otherwise
clear.

The liver is slightly hypodense, consistent with fat deposition, as seen on
recent MR.  ___ focal liver lesions are identified.  There is ___ intrahepatic
biliary duct dilatation.  The portal vein is patent.  There is high density
fluid/sludge in the gallbladder.  Small gallstones were better demonstrated on
recent MRCP from ___.  There is ___ gallbladder wall thickening
or pericholecystic fluid.  The spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys are
unremarkable.

As seen on MR from ___, there is marked peripancreatic stranding
with a small quantity of fluid tracking throughout the left anterior pararenal
space and into the pelvis.  The pancreatic head enhances heterogeneously.  
The remainder of the pancreas shows normal enhancement.  The splenic vein
remains patent  There is ___ evidence of a pseudoaneurysm.  The stomach, small
bowel, colon, and appendix are grossly normal.  Small mesenteric and
retroperitoneal lymph nodes do not meet CT size criteria.  The abdominal aorta
is normal in caliber and its main vessels are patent.

PELVIS CT:
The bladder, uterus, and adnexa are unremarkable.  As previously
stated, there is a small quantity of free fluid in the pelvis.  ___
pathologically enlarged pelvic lymph nodes are seen.

BONE WINDOW:
___ suspicious lytic or blastic lesions are identified.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the precipitating cause of pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: Whether a common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) is present and acting as the etiology of this episode of pancreatitis, given the known gallstones and recurrent attacks
why_trace.expected_finding: A stone impacted in the distal common bile duct or within the biliary tree that would explain the gallstone-triggered pancreatitis, or alternatively its absence confirming no biliary obstruction

Labs: 'Amylase: 246.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)'
