study_inadequacy.evidence: "Limited views of the pancreas show no focal lesion."

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: MRCP is capable of providing full pancreatic and biliary visualization that the limited RUQ ultrasound could not, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets the unresolved biliary/pancreatic etiology question that the inadequate prior study left open.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: An MRCP is a rational next step to evaluate for choledocholithiasis as the etiology of acute pancreatitis in a patient with known gallstones and recurrent episodes, but the prior MRCP already showed no choledocholithiasis and a non-dilated CBD (4 mm), and the prior CT also showed no biliary dilation, making a repeat MRCP of uncertain incremental value unless the clinical picture has changed or the prior studies are felt to be outdated.

baseline.patient_history: Ms. ___ is a ___ s/p DD liver transplant ___ (on prograf  2.5/2.5 and MMF 1 gram BID with a tacrolimus level 10.2 on  ___ for HCV cirrhosis who presented to liver clinic today  and was noted to have persistently increasing LFTs. Her  post-operative course has been complicated by hepatic artery  stenosis s/p stent on Plavix/ASA, biliary strictures s/p last   stent ___ and now rising hepatic/cholestatic indices:   ___: Tbili 0.9, ALP 385, AST 217, ALT 202   ___: Tbili 1.2, ALP 275, AST 278, ALT 232   ___: Tbili 1.4, ALP 410, AST 400, ALT 316   ___: Tbili 1.9, ALP 432, AST 509, ALT 395. Cr 1.7   Her last liver biopsy ___ noted features suggestive of  recurrent chronic HCV and features suggestive of venous outflow  obstruction. She had an doppler U/S ___ which noted patent  hepatic vessels and common hepatic stent. She had an MRA/MRV  this morning and the formal read is pending, however there was  suggestion of possible narrowed appearance of the hepatocaval  anastomosis.   The patient herself complains of worsening pruritus x one month,  described as generalized itching including the palms/soles,.  that is so severe she is damaging her skin. She also sustained a  left-sided fall onto cement on ___. She was evaluated at a  local ER, and reportedly had X-rays of skull/left elbow/left  knee and left hip which were negative (records not available at  this time) She notes residual bruising/soreness on her left  orbital, left elbow, left knee and left hip, but otherwise is  ambulatory and at baseline function. She denies fevers/chills,  n/v, GI bleeding. Does state that she is having some abdominal  tenderness, particularly in the RUQ. Her VS in clinic were: BP:  123/64. Heart Rate: 69. Weight: 129lbs.     Past Medical History: Liver transplant  Decompsenated cirrhosis s/p DCD liver transplant ___ Chronic HCV   ETOH abuse   Knee surgery   Depression, with a suicide attempt one and a half years ago       Social History: ___ Family History: No family history of liver disease or hepatitis
baseline.physical_examination: Admission Exam: VS: 98.6, 128/71, 66, 20, 98%RA   GENERAL: Slender, alert/oriented middle-aged female.   HEENT: No sclera icterus. MMM.   SKIN: multiple tattoos, petecheia scattered over arms and chest.    CARDIAC: RRR with no excess sounds appreciated   LUNGS: CTA b/l with no wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.   ABDOMEN: Distended but Soft. Dullness to percussion over  dependent areas but tympanic anteriorly. +hepatomegaly,  splenomegaly not readily appreciated, mild tenderness  appreciated diffusely, but more so over RUQ.   EXTREMITIES: Warm and well perfused, no edema, clubbing or  cyanosis. Ecchymotic areas on her left orbital, left elbow, left  patella and left hip, with subcutaneous hardening/calcification  over his left orbit   NEUROLOGY: no asterixis, alert and oriented x3   .

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar T1- and T2-weighted MR images were obtained on a 3
Tesla magnet before, during, and after administration of a total of 12 cc of
ProHance IV contrast.

FINDINGS:
The patient is status post orthotopic liver transplant.  There is a 2.0 x 2.4
x 1.1 cm segment VI T2-intense lesion, similar in size to ___,
which is nonspecific.  The liver and spleen are both T2-hypointense compared
to spinal musculature, suggesting hemosiderosis.  There is otherwise
homogeneous enhancement of the liver parenchyma without evidence of hepatic
congestion.  No lesion with arterial enhancement or early washout is present.

A waist is present at the anastomosis of the transplanted hepatic vein and
native IVC, which is exaggerated by significant susceptibility artifact from
adjacent suture material.  However, the hepatic veins are normal in appearance
and caliber without dilatation or filling defect.

Pneumobilia is present, attesting to nonobstructed status of the bile ducts. 
There is gradual enhancement of the walls of the central and segmental bile
ducts after contrast administration, which may be related to bile duct
stenting.  No secondary sign of cholangitis is present.

A trace amount of ascites is present along the posterior aspect of liver.  A
2.4-cm residual subhepatic fluid collection is decreased in size since ___.  There is blooming artifact from a common hepatic artery stent.  The
portal vasculature is unremarkable without filling defect.  Splenic and
gastric varices are again noted.  

The spleen is enlarged to 13.2 cm.  The pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys
are unremarkable.  The stomach and visualized portions of the small and large
bowel are unremarkable. 

Post-surgical changes are present of the anterior abdominal wall.  No
retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  No pneumoperitoneum.

FINDINGS:
Two frontal images of the abdomen show two biliary stents in
relatively the same position as a single biliary stent was seen on prior
abdominal radiograph.  There is appropriate pneumobilia noted in the area
surrounding the proximal portion of the biliary stents.  Visualized osseous
structures are unremarkable and there are no soft tissue calcifications.

ABDOMEN:


FINDINGS:
The two adjacent biliary stents are again visualized.  There is gas
in non-distended loops of bowel with stool seen in the ascending and
descending colon.  No free air is identified on the lateral decubitus film. 
Contrast is visualized in the bladder.  No specific abnormality is seen on
this film, and if clinical suspicion remains high, CT would be helpful.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Hepatic venous outflow obstruction / hepatocaval anastomotic stenosis; biliary stricture progression or stent dysfunction; hepatic artery restenosis; ascites/fluid collection; post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder; renal pathology contributing to AKI
why_trace.information_gap: The MRI suggested a 'waist' at the hepatocaval anastomosis with significant susceptibility artifact from suture material obscuring full assessment, and the MRA/MRV formal read is pending — the physician needs cross-sectional anatomic detail of the hepatic vasculature, biliary stents, fluid collections, and parenchyma without the artifact limitations of MRI to clarify whether outflow obstruction or another structural complication is driving the escalating LFTs and abdominal findings.
why_trace.expected_finding: Evidence of hepatic venous outflow obstruction (e.g., caudate lobe hypertrophy, periportal edema, ascites progression, IVC/hepatic vein narrowing at anastomosis), biliary stent malposition or new biliary dilation, interval change in the subhepatic fluid collection, or vascular complication not fully characterized on MRI due to susceptibility artifact from the hepatic artery stent and suture material.
grounding: History: 'MRA/MRV this morning and the formal read is pending, however there was suggestion of possible narrowed appearance of the hepatocaval anastomosis'

Prior imaging 1 (MRI Abdomen): 'A waist is present at the anastomosis of the transplanted hepatic vein and native IVC, which is exaggerated by significant susceptibility artifact from adjacent suture material'
History: 'last liver biopsy ___ noted features suggestive of recurrent chronic HCV and features suggestive of venous outflow obstruction'
History: 'rising hepatic/cholestatic indices: Tbili 1.9, ALP 432, AST 509, ALT 395. Cr 1.7'
Labs: 'Creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL (ref 0.4-1.1)' — renal impairment motivating non-contrast choice
History: 'hepatic artery stenosis s/p stent on Plavix/ASA, biliary strictures s/p last stent'
Prior imaging 1 (MRI Abdomen): 'There is blooming artifact from a common hepatic artery stent'
Physical exam: 'Distended but Soft. Dullness to percussion over dependent areas... +hepatomegaly... mild tenderness appreciated diffusely, but more so over RUQ'

study_inadequacy.evidence: She had an MRA/MRV this morning and the formal read is pending, however there was suggestion of possible narrowed appearance of the hepatocaval anastomosis.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen without contrast can provide complementary structural information about parenchymal and biliary findings but is poorly suited to definitively resolve the primary flagged question — whether there is true hemodynamically significant narrowing at the hepatocaval anastomosis — which requires vascular-phase contrast enhancement or formal MRA/MRV interpretation, and the non-contrast CT cannot substitute for the pending formal read of the MRA/MRV that was specifically obtained for that purpose.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: CT without contrast is a reasonable next step to assess anatomy free of MRI susceptibility artifact and to evaluate the biliary stents and fluid collections, but contrast-enhanced CT or formal MRA/MRV read would provide superior vascular detail for the hepatocaval anastomotic stenosis that is the leading concern; the non-contrast choice is likely driven by renal impairment (Cr 1.7) limiting contrast use.

TECHNIQUE:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with oral contrast.

FINDINGS:


CT OF THE ABDOMEN:
There is a small right-sided pleural effusion
significantly decreased in size since the prior study.  Otherwise, pericardium
is unremarkable.

Within the abdomen, there is a history of orthostatic liver transplant with
surgical anastomosis seen at the level of the IVC.  There is pneumobilia.  A
hepatic artery stent is in place and unchanged.  Two common bile duct stents
are seen extending from the liver to the duodenum.  There is biliary
dilatation, unchanged from prior study.  Bilateral adrenals are unremarkable. 
Bilateral kidneys are normal.  The spleen is enlarged measuring up to 15 cm.

There is a significant increase in the amount of abdominal and pelvic fluid
since the prior study, with the majority of the fluid being found within the
pelvis.  In the pelvis, there is also hyperdense contents, some of which are
layered (2:73) concerning for blood products, given the patient's drop in
hematocrit and their density.  No sentinel clot is noted.  Stomach, small and
large bowels are unremarkable in course and caliber.  The pancreas is normal
with no peripancreatic stranding arising from it.  The patient is status post
tubal ligation.  Bladder is normal in appearance.  The ureters are
unremarkable.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Hemoperitoneum / pelvic hemorrhage (possibly from fall or coagulopathy in transplant patient), ascites with hemorrhagic component, gynecologic source (e.g., ovarian pathology, ruptured cyst), or evolving intra-abdominal fluid collection
why_trace.information_gap: The CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast identified a significant increase in abdominal/pelvic fluid with hyperdense, layered contents in the pelvis concerning for blood products; the physician needs real-time characterization of this pelvic fluid — whether it is simple ascites, hemorrhagic fluid, or an organized collection — and to assess for a discrete gynecologic or pelvic source
why_trace.expected_finding: Characterization of the pelvic fluid as hemorrhagic vs. simple ascites, identification of any discrete pelvic collection or hematoma, and assessment of pelvic organs (ovaries, uterus) for a contributing source such as a ruptured cyst or other gynecologic pathology
grounding: CT Abdomen: 'There is a significant increase in the amount of abdominal and pelvic fluid since the prior study, with the majority of the fluid being found within the pelvis.'

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The pelvic ultrasound is directly capable of characterizing the hyperdense layered pelvic fluid seen on CT as hemorrhagic versus simple ascites and identifying a discrete source, and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets this discordance — hemoperitoneum versus ascites in the setting of a fall and dropping hematocrit — making both capability and intent well-aligned with the flagged situation.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The prior CT without contrast showed a significant new increase in pelvic fluid with hyperdense layered contents suspicious for blood products in the context of a recent fall and dropping hematocrit, making a targeted pelvic ultrasound the logical next step to characterize the fluid and identify its source without additional radiation or contrast exposure.

baseline.patient_history: ___ with known h/o cholelithiasis presenting with elevated lipase after going to ___ office with 3 days of epigastric pain. She reports that pain is only epigastric, not related to meals except once eating resolved it, usually lasts ___ hours, is associated with nausea but no vomiting. She has a poor appetite as baseline and that is "possibly slightly worse currently." She is passing flatus and had non-bloody non-stool this AM. She has been taking tylenol for this pain with small effect but no other alleviating factors. She reports some bloating but non bloody bowel movements. She did state that she felt feverish but did  not take a temperature over the last 2 days.   Of note is that she had elevated ALT and AP on ___ after presenting with similar epigastric pain and was worked up with RUQ US that showed gallstones but no cholecystitis. She put off plans for CCY due  to a hysterectomy and wanted to recover from that before having CCY. FINDINGS:
The liver is normal in echotexture.  There is a 0.8 x 0.6 x 1 cm
hypoechoic lesion in segment II/III, with peripheral flow on color flow
images.  This is unchanged in appearance since prior ultrasound exam.  No new
hepatic lesions are identified.  There is no intrahepatic biliary duct
dilatation.  The portal vein is patent demonstrating hepatopetal flow.  The
gallbladder is contracted in this non-NPO patient.  The gallbladder wall
thickness cannot be assessed due to contraction.  There is no pericholecystic
fluid collection.  The CBD is of normal caliber measuring 3 mm.  There is no
evidence of choledocholithiasis.  The pancreas is largely obscured by
overlying bowel gas.

Multiple small calcified gallstones are noted within the
gallbladder near the neck.  However, mobility was demonstrated.  A sonographic
___ sign was not present.  The common bile duct is normal and measures 5
mm.  Visualized aspect of the head and body of the pancreas is within normal
limits.  No peripancreatic fluid can be seen.  The tail was not visualized due
to overlying bowel gas.  Further evaluation of the abdomen is somewhat limited
due to overlying bowel gas.  The liver is homogeneous in echogenicity
throughout without intrahepatic biliary dilatation or focal liver masses.  The

pubic symphysis after administration 130 cc of Optiray contrast.  Coronal and
sagittal reformatted images were also displayed.

The lung bases demonstrate bibasilar
atelectasis.  The heart size is normal.  The spleen, adrenal glands, kidneys,
liver, stomach, and intra-abdominal loops of bowel are within normal limits. 
The gallbladder demonstrates a 4-mm gallstone, unchanged since ___.  There is
a large duodenal diverticulum which is also stable since ___.

There is an extensive amount of fat stranding and fluid seen around the head
and a portion of the body of the pancreas.  These findings are consistent with
____.  The pancreas itself enhances normally throughout.  There are no
pseudocysts or other focal fluid collections.  There is prominence of the
common bile duct; however, this measures only up to 7 mm, within normal limits
given the patient's age.

There is no free air.  There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric
lymphadenopathy.

CT OF THE PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST:
The uterus, rectum, bladder, and
intrapelvic loops of bowel are within normal limits.  There is no pelvic or
inguinal lymphadenopathy.  Small amount of free fluid is noted within the
pelvis.  This fluid tracks along the right psoas muscle and anterior to
Gerota's fascia extending from the ____.

BONE WINDOWS:
No concerning osseous lesions are identified.  There is grade 1
anterolisthesis of L5 on S1 of approximately 4 mm.  Degenerative changes are
noted most prominently in the lumbar spine.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis as pancreatitis trigger; alcohol-related hepatitis; ascites from portal hypertension given abdominal distension and heavy alcohol use
why_trace.information_gap: The RUQ ultrasound done at the outside ED was explicitly noted as a 'limited study' — it did not adequately visualize the biliary tree or pancreas, leaving the etiology of the markedly elevated lipase (1498) and the cause of abdominal distension unresolved; specifically, whether a biliary stone is triggering pancreatitis or whether there is free fluid/ascites cannot be determined from the prior study.
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones or biliary ductal dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as a precipitant of pancreatitis, OR free intraperitoneal fluid (ascites) consistent with portal hypertension from chronic alcohol use, OR peripancreatic edema/fluid; alternatively, confirmation that the biliary tree is clear, shifting etiology toward alcohol-induced pancreatitis alone.

resolve.reason: The repeat dedicated abdominal ultrasound directly targets the study_inadequacy flag by re-imaging the biliary tree to exclude choledocholithiasis, and the reasoning explicitly acknowledges this gap, but neither the order nor the reasoning meaningfully addresses the more pressing discordance flag — the persistently elevated lactate of 3.1 after fluids with an anion gap of 25, which raises concern for mesenteric ischemia or necrotizing pancreatitis and would require CT (ideally CT angiography or contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis) rather than ultrasound to adjudicate.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: The prior RUQ ultrasound was explicitly a 'limited study' that did not exclude choledocholithiasis, and with lipase 1498 and abdominal distension in a heavy drinker, a dedicated abdominal ultrasound is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary tree, liver parenchyma, and for ascites before escalating to CT.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Grayscale and Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

FINDINGS:
The liver demonstrates diffuse increase in echogenicity. A hypoechoic lesion
measuring 1.8 x 0.9 cm is seen within the right hepatic lobe and likely
represents a region of focal fatty sparing.  There is no evidence of
intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The common bile duct
measures 0.4 cm.  The gallbladder is normal and without evidence of stones or
gallbladder wall thickening.  Evaluation of the pancreas is limited by the
patient's body habitus and by adjacent bowel gas, but the visualized portions
appears within normal limits and without evidence of choledocholithiasis or
pancreatic ductal dilatation.  The right kidney measures 11.4 cm, and is
without evidence of hydronephrosis or solid renal mass. There is no ascites.

CHEST RADIOGRAPH:


FINDINGS:
The lung volumes are low.  Bilateral pleural effusions, better seen
on the lateral than on the frontal radiograph.  Subsequent areas of mild
atelectasis at the lung bases.  Borderline size of the cardiac silhouette
without evidence of fluid overload.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: Complications of acute pancreatitis (necrosis, peripancreatic fluid collections, pseudocyst), hepatic pathology (given fatty liver and transaminase elevation), bowel obstruction, or mesenteric/vascular process; also concern for underlying malignancy given 2-month history of abdominal distension and firmness
why_trace.information_gap: The ultrasound was limited by body habitus and bowel gas and could not adequately visualize the pancreas, so the extent and severity of pancreatitis (necrosis, fluid collections, ductal involvement) and any other intra-abdominal pathology remain undefined despite a markedly elevated lipase of 1498 and a persistently elevated lactate of 3.1 after fluids
why_trace.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema, peripancreatic fat stranding, fluid collections, or necrosis consistent with acute pancreatitis; additionally, the physician likely hopes to characterize the hepatic lesion seen on ultrasound, evaluate for ascites, and exclude other causes of diffuse abdominal pain such as bowel obstruction or mesenteric ischemia
grounding: Labs: 'lipase 1498'
Labs: 'lactate 4.0 (3.1 following fluids)'
Labs: 'Anion Gap: 25.0 mEq/L (ref 8.0-20.0)'
Physical exam: 'soft but with involuntary guarding, moderate tenderness in epigastrum and mildly tender in lower quadrants, distended with normoactive bowel sounds'
Prior imaging 1: 'Evaluation of the pancreas is limited by the patient's body habitus and by adjacent bowel gas, but the visualized portions appears within normal limits'
Prior imaging 2: 'Bilateral pleural effusions, better seen on the lateral than on the frontal radiograph'
History: 'his belly has become bigger and firmer since having this abdominal pain' (2-month history)
History: 'sitting up helps his abdominal pain' (positional relief suggesting pancreatic etiology)
Labs: 'AST 69, ALT 130' (transaminase elevation in context of fatty liver)

study_inadequacy.evidence: Evaluation of the pancreas is limited by the patient's body habitus and by adjacent bowel gas, but the visualized portions appears within normal limits and without evidence of choledocholithiasis or pancreatic ductal dilatation.

discordance.evidence: lactate 4.0 (3.1 following fluids) ... Abdomen: soft but with involuntary guarding, moderate tenderness in epigastrum ... non-peritoneal although has tap tenderness ... Anion Gap: 25.0 mEq/L

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: CT abdomen/pelvis with and without contrast is both technically capable of fully characterizing the pancreas (resolving the ultrasound's limited visualization) and of adjudicating the discordant lactic acidosis/high anion gap by identifying or excluding pancreatic necrosis, mesenteric ischemia, or other severe intra-abdominal pathology — and the physician's reasoning explicitly targets both the inadequate prior study and the unexplained metabolic derangement.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With a lipase of 1498, persistent lactic acidosis (lactate 3.1 after fluids), involuntary guarding, abdominal distension, bilateral pleural effusions on CXR, and an ultrasound that was explicitly limited in pancreatic visualization, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to characterize the severity of pancreatitis and exclude complications or alternative diagnoses.

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as a precipitating cause of pancreatitis; alcoholic hepatitis contributing to transaminase elevation
why_trace.information_gap: Whether gallstones or biliary pathology (cholelithiasis, choledocholithiasis, cholecystitis) are present and potentially driving the acute pancreatitis, given that the etiology (gallstone vs. alcohol) has direct management implications
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones in the gallbladder, pericholecystic fluid or wall thickening suggesting cholecystitis, or a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder that would shift etiology toward alcohol

discordance.evidence: Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L (ref 8.0-20.0) — elevated anion gap in the setting of a presentation otherwise attributed to acute pancreatitis (lipase 1836, epigastric pain, N/V), with normal lactate not yet reported, normal bicarbonate (23), and no obvious explanation (no ketoacidosis, no renal failure, glucose 100, creatinine 0.7). The elevated anion gap is not an expected feature of uncomplicated acute pancreatitis and raises concern for an additional or alternative process (e.g., early ischemia, toxic ingestion, or occult acidosis) not accounted for by the leading diagnosis.

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: The abdominal ultrasound is directed entirely at identifying biliary etiology of pancreatitis and cannot resolve the unexplained elevated anion gap (21 mEq/L), which requires laboratory investigation (e.g., lactate, ketones, toxicology, repeat electrolytes) to exclude ischemia, toxic ingestion, or occult acidosis.

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase 1836 confirming acute pancreatitis and elevated triglycerides (184) plus heavy alcohol use as competing etiologies, right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the standard first-line imaging to identify gallstone disease as a treatable precipitant before attributing the episode solely to alcohol.

visible_prior_imaging.report:
TECHNIQUE:
Frontal and lateral radiographs of the chest were obtained.

FINDINGS:
There is a 6-mm right mid lung zone pulmonary nodule, likely granuloma.  There
is prominence of the right infrahilar region, likely vessels.  No focal
consolidation.  The cardiomediastinal silhouette is normal.  There is no
pleural effusion. Left deviation of the trachea, likely due to goiter

why_trace.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis with transient biliary obstruction as the trigger for pancreatitis
why_trace.information_gap: The etiology of the acute pancreatitis remains unclear — the RUQ ultrasound at the outside hospital showed no cholelithiasis but did show gallbladder wall edema, and the mildly elevated transaminases (AST 82→62, ALT 74→62) raise concern for a biliary/obstructive cause that the prior US may have missed or incompletely characterized
why_trace.expected_finding: Gallstones, sludge, or common bile duct dilation that would indicate a biliary etiology for pancreatitis, or alternatively confirmation of gallbladder wall edema without stones to help characterize the gallbladder pathology
grounding: History: 'lipase>4000' and 'lipase of 2506' confirming acute pancreatitis
History: 'RUQ US without evidence of cholelithiasis though he did have some gall bladder wall edema'
History: 'AST of 82, ALT of 74' at outside hospital with 'AST of 62, ALT of 62' on arrival — mildly elevated transaminases
History: 'he does not drink alcohol and notes no new medications' — common etiologies excluded
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.6 mg/dL' — currently normal, but transient elevation cannot be excluded
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 84.0 IU/L' — within normal range but transaminases remain elevated

advanced_question.basis: belief max_p>=0.5 on a rubric disease & severity/etiology/complication gap

resolve.reason: no trigger detected — step-2 skipped

REWARD.appropriateness_reason: With lipase >2500, mildly elevated transaminases, gallbladder wall edema on outside imaging, and no identified etiology (no alcohol use, no new medications), a dedicated RUQ/liver-gallbladder ultrasound at the receiving institution is appropriate to evaluate for biliary pathology as the precipitant of pancreatitis.
